name: HMS Ultimatum (P 34)
events: The history of HMS P 34 / HMS Ultimatum as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine and was created in October 2014. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in March 2026.

28 Jul 1941 Around 1430 B/28, P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed from her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with Trusty (Lt.Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN). They were escorted by HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). (1)

29 Jul 1941 Around 1730 B/29, P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of trials and training. She had just completed her acceptance trials and was taken over from the shipyards and placed in full commission upon arrival at Holy Loch.
[No logs of HMS P 34 are available for the months of until June 1942 some details for the period from August 1941 to May 1942 will be missing.] (1)
24 Aug 1941 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for Barrow. She was sent back to her builders yard to make good several defects before she was to be sent to the Mediterranean.
During the passage to Barrow P 34 was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR).
25 Aug 1941 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at Barrow from Holy Loch.
16 Sep 1941 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Barrow for Gibraltar. She was to proceed to Malta to join the 10th submarine flotilla based there.
During the passage south through the Irish Sea HMS P 34 was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) until Bishops Rock.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed. Also no passage / patrol report is available.]

21 Sep 1941 While en-route to Gibraltar HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) is ordered by signal to conduct a short patrol off Cape Finisterre, Spain. This makes the passage HMS P 34's fired war patrol.

The signal originated from the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) and was timed 1306 A/21. It contained the following orders; (1) Carry out dive patrol in area within 30 miles of position 42°50'N, 10°40'W. Look out for enemy U-boat. (2) My 1410 A/20 leave patrol so as to pass through position 'Q' at 1600 A/25 and rendezvous with the escort at position 'R' at 0300 A/26. Report if unable to comply within 1 hour. (3) Gibraltar broadcasting L/F frequency is now 44.8 kc/s.
[No info is available to us on the routing of HMS P 34 after she had parted company with the escort of Bishops Rock.] (2)

23 Sep 1941 In a signal timed 2300 A/23 to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) informed the Flag Officer that her position, course and speed were 42° 20'N, 10°40'W, 180°, 10 knots. She had been delayed by bad weather conditions. (2)
26 Sep 1941 Around 1145 A/26, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 1st war patrol when she arrived at Gibraltar. (3)
1 Oct 1941 At 2200 A/1, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for passage to Malta.

She was to pass through position 38°00'N, 09°00'E about 0400 A/5. [She actually passed through this position at 0650 A/4.] Further routing will be signalled by the Naval Officer in Command Malta or the Capt. S.10. Further waypoints were also contained the signal timed 1310 A/30 from the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.). [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (4)

2 Oct 1941 At 1750 A/2, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passed through position 'A' (36°20'N, 02°15'W). [This was obviously one of the points HMS P 34 was to pass through on her way eastwards.]
At 2350 A/2, HMS P 34 sighted two illuminated vessels bearing 340°. No further details are given. Most likely this were either Spanish vessels or more likely Vichy-French vessels. (5)

3 Oct 1941 At 0300 A/3, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passed through position 'B' (37°00'N, 01°00'E). [This was obviously one of the points HMS P 34 was to pass through on her way eastwards.] (5)

3 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1110 B/3, the Vice Admiral Malta sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Adjust speed to dive in the vicinity of position 38°00'N, 11°44'E to make passage through QBB 65 during daylight on the 7th thence from position 37°15'N. 12°55'E direct via the north-west searched channel to arrive Marsamxett (Malta) before 1930 B/8 or at 0700 B/9. (6)

4 Oct 1941 At 0650 A/4, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passed through position 'C' (38°00'N, 09°00'E). [This was obviously one of the points HMS P 34 was to pass through on her way eastwards.] (5)
8 Oct 1941 At 1000 A/8, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar. (5)

17 Oct 1941 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). Initially She was ordered to intercept an enemy convoy in position 36°11'N, 11°43'E.

This was to intercept the southbound convoy made up of the Italian transports Beppe (4859 GRT, built 1912), Caterina (4838 GRT, built 1920), Marin Sanudo (5081 GRT, built 1926), Paolina (4894 GRT, built 1911), Probitas (5084 GRT, built 1918) and Amba Aradam (405 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the Italian destroyers Folgore, Fulmine, Antoniotto Usodimare, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani sailed from Naples at 0955 B/16-1335 B/16. At 1500 B/16, Probitas which had developed engine defects turned back, escorted by Fulmine. They arrived at Naples at 1648 B/16-1652 B/16).

At 0910 B/18, Beppe was hit in the forepeak by a torpedo of HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN), her rudder was out of the water. Vincenzo Gioberti picked up some of the crew and her military passengers, in all 68 persons. With some difficulty, Beppe managed to do 4 knots. Soon, it was realised that she needed towing and the destroyer attempted to do so but the cable quickly broke. At 0400 B/19, Beppe and Vincenzo Gioberti were joined by the destroyer Nicoloso da Recco which had sailed from Tripoli with the destroyer Sebenico at 1900 B/18 to assist them. Vincenzo Gioberti was short of water and Nicoloso da Recco signalled her to proceed to Tripoli where she arrived at 1707 B/19. At 2030 B/18, the Italian tug Ciclope sailed from Tripoli and joined them at 0900 B/19. In the meantime, Sebenico proceeded to reinforce the escort of the convoy which had come under air attack from Swordfish aircraft of 830 Squadron (FAA) at 2210 B/18. The steamer Caterina had been hit by a torpedo; the destroyer Alfredo Oriani stood by her. The German tug Max Berendt sailed from Tripoli at 0430 B/19 to tow her, followed by the Italian tug Marsigli at 0500 B/19. The torpedo boats Generale Antonino Cascino and Calliope sailed from Zuara and Sidi Bilal respectively at 0445 B/19 to assist the stricken vessel, but Caterina sank at 1720 B/19 in position 350° - Tripoli Light - 62 miles. Generale Antonino Cascino picked up 195 survivors, there were four victims. The convoy which now consisted of Marin Sanudo, Paolina and Amba Aradam, screened by the destroyers Folgore and Antoniotto Usodimare arrived at Tripoli at 1130 B/19-1155 B/19, followed by Generale Antonino Cascino at 1525 B/19, Alfredo Oriani and Sebenico at 2205 B/19-2230 B/19, Max Berendt at 0525 B/20 and sailed again to assist Beppe. Nicoloso da Recco arrived at Tripoli at 1445 B/20. Beppe towed by Ciclope, Marsigli and Max Berendt and escorted by Calliope arrived at 0610 B/21-0700 B/21.

[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed. Also no patrol report is available so virtually no details of this patrol are not known to us. Also quite a number of signal regarding the submarines orders and movements seem to be missing from the war diary summaries which is also not helpful.]

17 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1145 A/17, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) were informed that HMS Rorqual (Lt. L.W. Napier, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) would proceed from position 37°16'N, 12°56'E to position 38°00'N, 11°44'E during daylight/19. HMS Rorqual would then proceed towards Cavoli Island and HMS P 34 was to operate east of 12°00'E and south of 38°40'N. [This patrol area for HMS P 34 is however a bid strange as HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta on the 19th to operate in exactly this patrol area. It therefore seems likely that HMS P 34 had been ordered to patrol somewhere else, most probably further to the east, most probably between Cape St. Vito and Palermo, but no signal on this is listed in the war diary summaries.] (6)

23 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1929 A/23, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) of the following; Aircraft reconnaissance reported at 0700 A/23 a 3000-ton tanker close off Kelibia course north.

This 'tanker' may have been the Italian transport Perseo (5225 GRT, built 1921) which sailed from Sousse at 1700 B/22 at 5 knots and arrived at Cagliari at 2000 B/24. [Perseo was an engine aft ship which looked thus like a tanker.] (6)

26 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1046 A/26, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Leave patrol P.M./26 through positions 38°20'N, 12°35'E, 38°20'N, 12° 00'E and 38°00'N, 11°44'E making the submerged passage of Q.B.B. 65 during daylight/27 to arrive at Malta by the north-west swept channel A.M./28. Attempt to communicate with HMS Tetrarch (Lt.Cdr. G.H. Greenway, RN) by S.S./T. on 27/10. P 34 and HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) are not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 26/27 October. Other movements will follow. (6)

27 Oct 1941 At 1400 A/27, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) communicated with HMS Tetrarch (Lt.Cdr. G.H. Greenway, RN) by S.S./T. in approximate position 37°28'N, 12°35'E. This was the last ever heard of HMS Tetrarch.
28 Oct 1941 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

6 Nov 1941 Around 1400 B/6, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) after carrying exercises with the M/S drifter HMS Justified (T/Skr. W.J.I. Beamish, RNR) and the minesweeper HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR).
The exercises were completed shortly before 1700 B/6 and HMS P 34 set course to proceed on patrol.
She was ordered to form a patrol line with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) in the Ionian Sea to intercept the Duisburg convoy.

This convoy, which was decimated by 'Force K' on 9 November, consisted of the transports Duisburg (German 7389 GRT, built 1928), Maria (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1926), San Marco (Italian, 3076 GRT, built 1919), Sagitta (Italian, 5153 GRT, built 1919) and the tanker Minatitlán (Italian, 7651 GRT, built 1940) screened by the Italian destroyers Maestrale, Fulmine and Euro. It had transited the Straits of Messina around 0240 B/8. The convoy was later joined by the transports Rina Corrado (Italian, 5180 GRT, built 1918) and Conte di Misurata (Italian, 5014 GRT, built 1908) escorted by the Italian destroyers Grecale, Libeccio and Alfredo Oriani which sailed from Messina at 1400 B/8.
HMS P 34 was to patrol in in position 'N', which was 37°34'N, 19°50'E. This was the southernmost patrol position of the patrol line of three submarines. (5)
6 Nov 1941 For the daily positions of HMS P 34 during her 3rd war patrol see the map below.

8 Nov 1941 At 2245 B/8, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in her patrol position, position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E). She then commenced to patrol through this position on courses 130° - 310° at 4 knots, three miles on either side of position 'N'. (5)
9 Nov 1941 At 0630 B/9, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to periscope depth and commenced patrolling on courses 040° - 220°, two miles on either side of position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E).
At 1123 B/9, a distant explosion was heard. [This was probably one of the ships of the Duisburg-convoy blowing up.]
At 1900 B/9, HMS P 34 surfaced and commenced to patrol on courses 040° - 220°, three miles on either side of position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E). (5)

10 Nov 1941 At 0640 B/10, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to periscope depth and commenced patrolling on courses 040° - 220°, two miles on either side of position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E).

11 Nov 1941 At 0600 B/11, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to periscope depth and commenced patrolling on courses 040° - 220°, two miles on either side of position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E).
At 1150 B/11, a merchant vessel was sighted bearing 280° at a range of 6 miles. Commenced attack.

At 1203 B/11, the attack was broken off when it was seen that the ship was the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1926). This was indeed the Virgilio which was searching in the area for survivors of the Duisburg-convoy until 1630/11.
At 1900 B/11, HMS P 34 surfaced and commenced to patrol on courses 040° - 220°, three miles on either side of position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E). (5)

12 Nov 1941 At 0650 B/12, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to periscope depth and commenced patrolling on courses 040° - 220°, two miles on either side of position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E).
At 1921 B/12, HMS P 34 surfaced and commenced to patrol on courses 040° - 220°, three miles on either side of position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E). (5)

13 Nov 1941 At 0650 B/13, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to periscope depth and commenced patrolling on courses 170° - 350°, two miles on either side of position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E).
At 0815 B/13, HMS P 34 went to 60 feet owing to the weather conditions. She then commenced to patrol on courses 040° - 220°.
At 2030 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced and kept her head on to the sea on course 160° proceeding at a speed of 2 knots. (5)
14 Nov 1941 At 0700 B/14, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived 60 feet and proceeded on course 319° at a speed of 3 knots to regain position 'N' (37°34'N, 19°50'E).
At 1200 B/14, HMS P 34 was in position 37°35'N, 19°54'E. She then commenced to patrol on courses 040° - 220° while coming to periscope depth every 15 minutes.
At 2030 B/14, HMS P 34 surfaced.
At 2205 B/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1006 B/14 was received. Course was altered to 054° to proceed to the new patrol position in 37°50'N, 20°15'E at 8 knots. (5)

14 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1006 B/14, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) to shift the patrol line 053 - 27 miles to be in position to intercept two fast southbound supply ships.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the troop transports (naval auxiliaries) Città di Genova (Italian, 5413 GRT, built 1930) and Città di Napoli (5418 GRT, built 1929) which were to proceed from Taranto to Benghasi escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta and Giovanni da Verrazzano.
The position of HMS Urge to intercept this convoy was the result of an ULTRA intercept. [Code breaking of the enemy's naval cypher, source: file DEFE3/690/462, (National Archives, London)]. (7)
15 Nov 1941 At 0135 B/15, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 37°50'N, 20°15'E and commenced patrolling on courses 040° - 220° through this position.
At 0650 B/15, HMS P 34 dived to periscope depth and commenced patrolling on courses 190° - 330° three miles on either side of position 37°50'N, 20°15'E.

At 1200 B/15, HMS P 34 altered course to 202° in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1333 B/12 in which P 34 was to leave patrol at noon. The signal timed 1333 B/12 had contained the following orders; Leave patrol to pass through position 37°20'N, 19°50'E at 2100 B/15 making a short area report addressed to ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) when safe to do so and then proceed through positions 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 36°00'E, 14°53'E to arrive Marsamxett (Malta) at 0730 B/18. (5)

17 Nov 1941 At 1030 B/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1745 B/16 in which she was ordered to patrol within 10 miles of position 35°45'N, 19°30'E until dusk on the 18th and then return to Malta as routed by Capt. S.10.
At 1040 B/17, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded at 9 knots on course 101° to get into the position ordered. (8)
18 Nov 1941 At 0400 B/18, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was in position 35°45'N, 19°30'E and commenced patrolling on courses 090° - 270° three miles on either side of this position.
At 1755 B/18, HMS P 34 left patrol to continue her passage to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1745 B/16. (5)

18 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1118 B/18 from the Capt. S.10, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and RHS Glavkos (Lt.Cdr. V. Arslanoglou) were ordered to proceed through positions 35°51'N, 15°15'E and and 36°00'N, 14°53'E to arrive at Malta at 0730 B/21 and 0730 B/22 respectively. (7)

19 Nov 1941 At 1930 B/19, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 1714 B/19 to the Naval Officer in Command Malta requesting permission to arrive at Malta at 0900 B/19. Permission to do so was granted in the reply signal timed 2123 B/19. (5)
20 Nov 1941 Around 0900 B/20, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

28 Nov 1941 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of Cape Spartivento in area 'G' focusing on position 37°48'N, 16°05'E.
On departure speed trials were carried out on the measured mile.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 34 during this patrol see the map below.

29 Nov 1941 At 0540 B/29, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0249 B/29, in which she was ordered to patrol south of Cape dell'Armi in area 'F' focusing on position 37°51'N, 15°43'E. (5)
30 Nov 1941 At 0024 B/30, well to the south of the toe of Italy, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted vessels to the west.
At 0028 B/30, these vessels were identified as two trawlers, bearing 270° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 040°.
In the afternoon, HMS P 34 entered her patrol area. (5)
1 Dec 1941 At 0150 B/1, while to the south of Cape dell'Armi, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a ship which was burning her navigation lights coming out of the Straits of Messina.
At 0200 B/1, HMS P 34 dived to close.

At 0220 B/1, this ship was identified as a hospital ship. It was thought to be the Italian Arno (7785 GRT, built 1912) which was indeed correct. The Arno had sailed from Naples at 1035 B/30 for Benghazi where she arrived at 1030 B/2.
At 0310 B/1, HMS P 34 surfaced.
At 0605 B/1, HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet. A/S watch was set.
At 0800 B/1, a small coaster was seen at a range of 6 miles. This vessel was proceeding on course 090° close inshore.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 1810 B/1, HE was picked up which was thought to be originating from a trawler. On coming to periscope depth nothing was seen.
At 1850 B/1, the HE ceased. During the period the HE was heard, tapping noises were heard on the Asdic.
At 2030 B/1, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled on course as requested to keep a favourable moon.
At 2205 B/1, It was thought a ship was sighted close inshore but it was lost from sight immediately. (5)
2 Dec 1941 At 0305 B/2, while to the south of Cape dell'Armi, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a ship which was burning her navigation lights coming out of the Straits of Messina.
At 0320 B/2, HMS P 34 dived to close. The ship was subsequently identified as a hospital ship. It was thought to be the Italian Sicilia (9646 GRT, built 1924).

This was however not Sicilia but the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) which sailed from Naples at 1120 B/1 and arrived at Tripoli 'escorted' by the Italian torpedo boat Partenope (which had sailed from Tripoli at 0300 B/3 to meet her) at 0915 B/3.
At 0355 B/2, HMS P 34 surfaced.
At 0600 B/2, HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet. A/S watch was set. Periscope patrol was commenced at 0740 A/2.
At 0910 B/2, a merchant vessel of about 6000-ton was sighted bearing 230° at a range of 8 miles. Enemy course was 110°.

This was most probably the Italian tanker Lucania (8106 GRT, built 1902) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori which sailed from Messina at 0715 B/2 and arrived at Taranto at 1645 B/3.
At 1800 B/2, HMS P 34 went to 70 feet and A/S watch was set.
At 2015 B/2, HMS P 34 surfaced and commenced to patrol on courses 090°-270°. (5)
3 Dec 1941 At 0630 B/3, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 70 feet. A/S watch was set. Periscope patrol was commenced at 0740 A/3.
At 1800 B/3, HMS P 34 went to 70 feet and A/S watch was set.
At 2015 B/3, HMS P 34 surfaced and commenced to patrol on courses 090°-270°.
At 2255 B/3, a white light was sighted on bearing 110°. Course was altered to investigate.

At 2320 B/3, this light was identified as a hospital ship. P 34 dived to avoid being sighted. It was thought the hospital ship was the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) proceeding on course 290°.
This was however not the Virgilio but most probably the Italian hospital ship Arno (8024 GRT, built 1926) which sailed from Benghazi at 1730 B/2 and arrived at Naples at 0745 B/5.
At 0015 B/4, HMS P 34 surfaced and re-commenced to patrol on courses 090°-270°. (5)
4 Dec 1941 At 0630 B/4, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 70 feet. A/S watch was set. Periscope patrol was commenced at 0740 A/2.
At 1800 B/4, HMS P 34 went to 70 feet and A/S watch was set.
At 2000 B/4, HMS P 34 surfaced and commenced to patrol on courses 090°-270°.
At 2300 B/4, HMS P 34 sighted a white light bearing 210°. P 34 closed to investigate.
At 2315 B/4, the light was identified as a hospital ship, thought to be the Italian Arno (7785 GRT, built 1912) proceeding on course 300°.
This was most probably the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) which sailed from Tripoli at 1745 B/3 and arrived at Naples at 1400 B/5. (5)

5 Dec 1941 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) attacked an Italian convoy made up of the merchants Vertunno (3159 GRT, built 1905) and Tigrai (1302 GRT, built 1918). They were escorted by the torpedo-boat Giuseppe Sirtori. The torpedoes missed and Sirtori retaliated with thirty-five depth charges. The two submarines sighted just before the attack were Veniero and Mocenigo proceeding from Naples to Taranto.

0020 A/5 - While south of Cape dell'Armi fast HE was heard on the port side which was soon heard to slow down. P 34 dived. The HE remained on a steady bearing and faded out after about an hour. The HE then re-appeared and finally disappeared at 0240 A/5.
0300 B/5 - Surfaced and patrolled on courses 090°-270°.
0640 B/5 - Dived to 70 feet and A/S watch was set. Periscope patrol was commenced at 0740 A/5.
1140 B/5 - Smoke was sighted on bearing 090°.
1250 B/5 - Sighted masts and funnel of a ship with smoke of another ship astern. Closed to investigate.
1255 B/5 - Commenced attack on the leading merchant vessel. A ship of about 4000 tons. Went to 70 feet and closed at full speed.

1310 B/5 - Came to periscope depth. Sighted two submarines but these could not be attacked. Had P 34 not gone deep an attack would have been possible. Continued the attack on the leading merchant vessel. It was now also seen that the merchant vessels were escorted by an older type torpedo-boat.
1328 B/5 - In position 37°48'N, 16°05'E fired three torpedoes from 5000 yards.
1331 B/5 - Heard one explosion.
1335 B/5 - Heard two more explosions.
1337 B/5 - A counter attack commenced. A total of 31 depth charges was dropped. P 34 was hunted all afternoon.

1430 B/5 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted a Generale-class torpedo boat and two motor torpedo boats closing from the direction of the Straits of Messina. The enemy was dodged as much as possible while remaining at periscope depth only going deep for patrolling aircraft.
1615 B/5 - Lost sight of the torpedo boat and the motor torpedo boats.
1644 B/5 - Picked up HE. Tried to investigate but had to go deep for an aircraft. This HE was lost after about 15 minutes. On return to periscope depth nothing was sighted.
1800 B/5 - Increased speed to 4 knots to clear the area and went to 90 feet.
2230 B/5 - Surfaced and proceeded to seaward on course 180°.
2250 B/5 - Sighted a red flare which rose from the surface. Dived.
2330 B/5 - Surfaced and proceeded on course 180°.
2337 B/5 - Withdrew 25 miles to the seaward and patrolled on courses 090° - 270°. (5)

6 Dec 1941 At 0345 B/6, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a red flare bearing 265° and immediately afterwards a motor torpedo boat was sighted laying stopped. It was considered that the flare seen at 2250 B/5 had also been fired by a motor torpedo boat.
At 0347 B/6, HMS P 34 dived and altered course stern on to the motor torpedo boat.
At 0510 B/6, HMS P 34 surfaced patrol commenced to patrol on course 090°-270°.
At 0645 B/6, HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet. A/S watch was set.
At 0740 B/6, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth near position 37°30'N, 15°39'E.
At 2005 B/6, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 165° for Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1310 B/5.
She was routed in accordance with her sailing orders and was to return to Malta at 0730 B/8. (5)

6 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2121 B/6, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Exceptional vigilance is necessary against U-boats, majority southbound passage of Straits probably at night. (9)

7 Dec 1941 At 0615 B/7, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived for a red flare fired from the surface. This was considered to have been fired from a motor torpedo boat. P 34 went to 70 feet and A/S watch was set but nothing was picked up on the Asdic.
At 0740 B/7, HMS P 34 came to periscope depth and continued her passage to Malta while submerged.
At 1706 B/7, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1424 B/7 was received which ordered P 34 to proceed to the vicinity of position 36°00'N, 16°00'E until OOO1B/8. Course was set to comply with these orders.
At 1930 B/7, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued passage to the area ordered. (10)

8 Dec 1941 At 0003 B/8, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 36°00'N, 16°00'E. Course was altered to 256° and speed was increased to 10 knots to proceed to Malta in accordance with the orders.
Around 1000 B/8, HMS P 34 ended her ended her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

12 Dec 1941 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Ionian Sea in a patrol line with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) and ORP Sokol (Lt.Cdr. B. Karnicki, ORP).

The patrol line was as follows 4 - 37°10'N, 18°46'E - 070° - 10 - 160° - 30 with HMS P 34 in position 'J' (37°10'N, 18°46'E), HMS Upholder in position 'K', HMS P 31 in position 'L' and ORP Sokol in position 'M'.
For the daily positions of HMS P 34 during this patrol see the map below.

14 Dec 1941 At 0214 B/14, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'J' (37°10'N, 18°46'E) and commenced to patrol on courses 340° - 160° through this position at a speed of 2 knots.
At 0710 B/14, HMS P 34 dived to periscope depth and commenced to patrol on courses 250° - 070° two miles on either side of position 'J'.
At 2000 B/14, HMS P 34 surfaced and commenced to patrol on courses 340° - 160° through position 'J'.

At 2345 B/14, HMS P 34 received a signal from the Capt. S.10 timed 2153 B/14 that the anticipated enemy movements did not occur and that the patrol line was dispersed. For HMS P 34 this signal contained the following orders; Pproceed to establish anti U-boat patrol off Augusta on 16/12. Leave as necessary to pass through position 37°02'N, 15°37'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E to arrive at Malta before noon/17. (5)

15 Dec 1941 At 0700 B/15, as HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was proceeding eastward, in a position about 110 miles west of Zante, a U-boat was observed proceeding on the surface. HMS P 34 closed submerged to intercept but lost sight of the enemy. This was probably the Italian Ciro Menotti on a transport mission to Benghazi. (5)
16 Dec 1941 At 0345 B/16, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived for a red flare fired from a surface craft. This was considered to have been fired from a motor torpedo boat.
At 0430 B/16, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded.
At 0443 B/16, HMS P 34 dived for an aircraft. It was decided to remain submerged.
At 0520 B/16, HMS P 34 was estimated to be 10 miles to the east of Augusta.
At 0650 B/16, HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet. A/S watch was set. Periscope depth patrol was commenced at 0740 B/16. The land was closed.
At 1700 B/16, intermittent depth charging was heard from the direction of the Straits of Messina. This ceased around 1845 B/16.
At 1915 B/16, HMS P 34 surfaced and set course for Malta in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2153 B/14. (5)
17 Dec 1941 At 0940 B/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

29 Dec 1941 Around 1855 B/29, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol well to the south of the Straits of Messina in area 'J' contained by lines joining the following positions; 36°56'N 15°24'E, 37°50'N 16°40'E, 38°04'N 16°15'E and 37°35'N 15°12'E.

30 Dec 1941 At 0050 B/30, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 315° at a range of 3 miles. P 34 turned towards and closed. The other submarine was subsequently identified as a U-class submarine and thus must be HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) which had departed Malta at the same time as HMS P 34 and which had been in company until around 2217 B/29.
At 0059 B/29, HMS P 34 dived and recognition signals were exchanged with HMS Unique through S.S./T. (5)

31 Dec 1941 At 0015 B/31, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a red flare that was fired from a surface craft. This was thought to be a motor torpedo boat so HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet to avoid detection.

At 0027 B/31, HE of a motor boat was picked up on bearing 260°. This was thought to be a fishing vessel instead of a motor torpedo boat. The HE passed slowly up the port side until finally fading out on bearing 310° at 0045 B/31.
At 0230 B/31, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded westwards at 3 knots.

At 0310 B/31, another red flare was sighted on bearing 218° at a range of about 3 miles. This was probably a motor torpedo boat. P 34 dived to avoid detection. Lt. Harrison decided to remain submerged and to close the land.
At 0635 B/31, HE from a motor boat was picked up on bearing 156°. This eventually passed close up the starboard side and finally faded out on bearing 305° at 0710 B/31.
At 2035 B/31, HMS P 34 surfaced. Course was set to 090° to clear the land and patrol in the vicinity of position 37°12'N, 15°41'E. (5)

2 Jan 1942 At 0400 B/2, east of Augusta, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a red flare being fired from a surface craft bearing 280° at a range of 2 miles. It was considered this flare was fired by a motor torpedo boat. HMS P 34 dived to avoid being sighted in the bright moonlight. Lt. Harrison decided to remain dived and to close the land at 70 feet.
At 0745 B/2, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth.
At 1400 B/2, two two-masted shooners were sighted on bearings 280° and 190°. Their courses were 180° and 090°. Both were hull down.
At 1420 B/2, another two masted schooner was sighted hull down on bearing 330° proceeding on an approximate course of 090°.

Between 1449 B/2 and 1512 B/2, explosions were heard. Nothing was in sighted except the three schooners which were all hull down. After the last explosion a large amount of smoke and haze appeared over Syracuse. Looked like an air strike had taken place.
At 1810 B/2, HMS P 34 went to 70 feet with Asdic watch set.
At 1956 B/2, HMS P 34 surfaced. There was bright moonlight. Course was set towards Cape Spartivento to patrol in the vicinity of position 37°48'N, 16°06'E. (5)
3 Jan 1942 At 0630 B/3, near Cape Spartivento, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived and commenced to close the land at 70 feet with Asdic watch set.
At 0745 B/3, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth in the vicinity of position 37°48'N, 16°06'E.
At 0845 B/3, the upperworks of a ship were sighted on bearing 155°. Course of this vessel was about 070°.
At 0900 B/3, the vessel was identified as a Spica-class torpedo boat. Range was 4 miles. The torpedo boat altered course to 270° and disappeared on bearing 230° at 0935 B/3.
At 1330 B/3, a Spica-class torpedo boat was sighted on bearing 060° at a range of about 4 miles. The torpedo boat disappeared soon afterwards to the south-east.
This was probably the Italian torpedo boat Centauro in both cases. She sailed from Messina at 2151 B/2 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1648 B/3.
At 1810 B/3, HMS P 34 went to 70 with the Asdic watch closed up.

At 2030 B/3, HMS P 34 surfaced. The sea was calm with no swell. There was a full moon and P 34 patrolled as requisite for the moon conditions. Asdic watch was set on completion of the battery charge. (5)

4 Jan 1942 At 1734 B/4, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1523 B/4 which ordered her to patrol in the Gulf of Taranto. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] She was to form a patrol line together with Unique and Proteus. HMS P 34 was to occupy the westernmost position in this patrol line [unfortunately no further details on this patrol line could be found.]

At 2345 B/4, HMS P 34 stopped and trimmed down to examine the starboard propeller which caused unusual vibration at 200 revolutions and approximately 50% increase in load at all revolutions. Also there was excessive noise coming from it. Nothing however could be seen to be foul or to the propeller blades. Not could any other cause for this be discovered either internally or externally. It was decided to limit revolutions to the starboard shaft to 300. The obstruction however cleared itself during the subsequent rough weather and the shaft then behaved normally.
At 0100 B/5, course was set for the new ordered patrol position. (5)
6 Jan 1942 At 0520 B/6, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'A' (39°27'N, 17°20'E) of the patrol line.
At 0530 B/6, HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet with the Asdic watch closed up.

At 0800 B/6, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at pericope depth in the vicinity of position 'A' as requisite for the sea and swell which had now increased to 44 and the wind to force 5. The visibility had decreased to 3 miles with rainsqualls.
At 1820 B/6, HMS P 34 went to 70 feet.
At 2030 B/6, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled at slow speed in the vicinity of position 'A'. Asdic watch was set. (5)
7 Jan 1942 At 0700 B/7, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 70 feet and patrolled near position 'A' (39°27'N, 17°20'E) of the patrol line. Asdic watch was closed up.
At 0800 B/7, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth. Weather and visibility had improved a bit as compared to the day before.
At 1400 B/7, HMS P 34 altered course to 240° to close the land and obtain a fix as no fix had been obrained for 48 hours.
At 1635 B/7, a fix was obtained from the Point Alice lighthouse. Visibility had improved to about 5 miles. Course was altered to 080° to return to point 'A'.
At 1735 B/7, HMS P 34 went to 70 feet.
At 2036 B/7, HMS P 34 surfaced.

At 2315 B/7, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1131 B/7 was received with ordered HMS P 34 to leave patrol and return to Malta. Course was altered to 180° to comply with these orders. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (5)
10 Jan 1942 At 1310 B/10, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)
20 Jan 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the South of the Straits of Messina in area 'J'.
21 Jan 1942 At 2010 B/21, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1846 B/21, which ordered P 34 to patrol in the vicinity of position 37°45'N, 15°55'E. (11)

22 Jan 1942 At 1125 B/22, returning to periscope depth south of the Straits of Messina, in approximate position 37°45'N, 15°35'E, after having been forced deep by three aircraft at 1112 B/22, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a convoy of two merchant ships escorted by at least four destroyers / torpedo boats bearing 020°. Enemy course was 100°. Range was about 4 to 5 miles. The merchant ships were large ones of about 8000 tons each. HMS P 34 was already well abaft the beam of the convoy and the was no hope to close the range.

The ships sighted were probably the Italian transports Monviso and Vettor Pisani and their escort, the Italian destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Antonio da Noli, Geniere, Ugolino Vivaldi and Lanzerotto Malocello and the Italian torpedo boat Castore. However, we cannot be absolutely sure about this. [See below for more details on the convoy operatation of which these ships were part.]

Operation T 18 The Italian transport Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1939) sailed from Naples at 1806 B/17-1820 B/17 escorted by the italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi and Lanzerotto Malocello and arrived at Messina at 0834 B/18-1031 B/18.
The Italian transport Monviso (5322 GRT, built 1941) sailed from Naples at 1727 B/20-1745 B/20 escorted by the Italian destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Euro and arrived at Messina at 1000 B/21.

The Italian transports Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940) and Ravello (6142 GRT, built 1941) sailed from Naples at 1730 B/21-1830 B/21 escorted by the Italian destroyer Maestrale and the Italian torpedo boats Procione and Orsa. Off Messina, Maestrale was detached and entered port at 1107 B/22.

Monviso and Vettor Pisani and escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Antonio da Noli, Geniere, Ugolino Vivaldi and Lanzerotto Malocello and the Italian torpedo boat Castore sailed from Messina at 0655 B/22-0800 B/22. They were joined off Messina by Monginevro and Ravello escorted by Orsa (equipped with sonar gear) and perhaps also by Procione which apparently left them sometime later to go to Palermo where she arrived at 2015 B/22. Ravello, due to a defective steering gear, turned back to Messina shortly after crossing the Straits. The Italian liner Victoria (13098 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the battleship Duilio and the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Ascari, Alfredo Oriani and Scirocco sailed from Taranto at 1535 B/22-1624 B/22.

The following forces were deployed to cover the convoy; The light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca D'Aosta, Eugenio Monteccucoli and Muzio Attendolo (7th Cruiser Division) and the destroyers Bersagliere, Carabiniere, Alpino and Fuciliere (13th Destroyer Squadron) sailed from Taranto at 0947 B/22-1100 B/22. The squadron was attacked by HMS Torbay (Cdr. A.C.C. Miers, DSO, RN) at 1347 B/22 but missed.

The two convoys merged at 1300 B/23. At 1730 B/23, three bombers attacked, releasing their torpedoes from about 2500 metres. One hit Victoria on the starboard stern; one Blenheim bomber was claimed to have been shot down and Ascari recovered the survivors. The convoy maintained its formation and speed, and at 1800 B/23, the Seventh Cruiser Division joined. At 1840 B/23 and 1850 B/23, Victoria was again attacked by enemy bombers and hit by two torpedoes and sank at 1900 B/23 in position 33°30'N, 17°41'E. The destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera and Ascari remained behind to pick up survivors, 1046 were saved from a total of 1400. The three destroyers then rejoined the convoy.
During the night of 23/24, the Duilio-squadron (minus Ascari) left the convoy and arrived at Taranto at 0610 B/25-0800 B/25.
At 0900 B/24, the 7th Cruiser Division screened by Bersagliere, Carabiniere, Alpino and Fuciliere turned back and arrived at Taranto at 1520 B/25-1625 B/25.

The remaining ships of the convoy proceeded to their destination. They were missed by a salvo of torpedoes from HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) at 0857 B/24 in position 024° - Homs – 11 miles. They were met by the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo which sailed from Tripoli at 1841 B/23-1845 B/23 to reinforce them. They arrived at Tripoli at 1430 B/24-1715 B/24. (11)

23 Jan 1942 At 2325 B/23, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2155 B/23 which ordered her to patrol in the vicinity of position 37°52'N, 15°40'E for the next 24 hours. After these 24 hours, HMS P 34 had freedom of action in her patrol area. (11)

24 Jan 1942 At 1155 B/24, in position 37°53'N, 15°38'E, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted the mast of a vessel approaching. P 34 turned towards but the vessel turned out to be only a small motor fishing vessel of about 70 tons which passed at a range of about half a mile and disappeared in the direction of Messina at 1240 B/24.
In the evening Lt. Harrison decided to remain in the vicinity of the current patrol position. (11)
25 Jan 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian passenger ship Dalmatia L. (3352 GRT, built 1903) south-west of Capo dell'Armi in position 37°45'N, 15°30'E.

Dalmatia L. was escorted by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori. Dalmatia L. was hit in the forward section and was abandoned by her crew but she did not sink immediately. Sirtori was joined by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Cesare Abba which had observed the torpedoing from a distance. Depth charges were dropped and then efforts were made to take the stricken vessel in tow. The tug Titano arrived to take in charge of the towing and part of the crew of Dalmatia L. returned aboard their ship but at 0140 B/26 she finally broke in two and foundered.
1305 B/25 - Sighted the upperworks of a merchant vessel and one other ship bearing 350°, range was about 10000 yards. Started attack and closed at high speed.
1326 B/25 - The target was a heavily laden merchant ship of about 7000 tons escorted by an older type torpedo boat.
1339 B/25 - Fired four torpedoes from 5000 yards.
1343 B/25 - Heard a torpedo explosion. H.E. of the target ceased.
1346 B/25 - A counter attack commenced. 9 Depth charges were dropped in the next 10 minutes. Only one was close.

1354 B/25 - Returned to periscope depth. The merchant vessel was seen to be stopped with a slight list to post. The torpedo-boat remained near the merchant vessel dropping a further 16 depth charges at irregular intervals until 1600 hours.
1712 B/25 - Heard an explosion.
1714 B/25 - Heard another explosion.
2115 B/25 - Surfaced and set course to patrol off Cape Spartivento. (11)

29 Jan 1942 At 2015 B/29, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) picked up motor boat HE approaching on bearing 285°. This HE passed ahead and finally faded out on bearing 080° at 2118 B/29. Nothing could be seen of this motor boat through the periscope in spite of the bright moon. It is considered this was either a fishing vessel or a motor torpedo boat running on auxiliary engines.

At 2315 B/29, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1211 B/29 was received which ordered HMS P 34 to leave patrol and return to Malta the next evening proceeding through position 37°15'N, 16°07'E and arrive at Malta on 1 February through the north-east swept channel. (11)
30 Jan 1942 At 2030 B/30, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) surfaced after a day of submerged patrol. Course was then set to 180° at 9 knots to return to Malta. (11)

31 Jan 1942 At 0245 B/31, east-south-east of Cape Murro di Porco, in position 36°55’N, 15°54’E, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a lighted hospital ship bearing 160° proceeding on course 320°. (11)
1 Feb 1942 At 0815 B/1, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)
14 Feb 1942 At 1915 A/14, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Kerkenah in area 'K' ().
She was to pass through position 34°36'N, 14°10'E about 0001 B/15 and then through position 35°00'N, 12°25'E on her way to area 'K'. (34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 70)
16 Feb 1942 At 0420 B/16, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted Kerkennah No.4 buoy and patrolled in its vicinity.
At 0630 B/16, HMS P 34 dived and patrolled in the vicinity of Kerkennah No.4 buoy in position 34°53'N, 11°47'E. Asdic watch was set.
At 2015 B/16, HMS P 34 surfaced and set course for position 34°45'N, 11°50'E to patrol on the enemy convoy routes during the night.

At 2055 B/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1151 B/16 was received. This signal contained the following orders; Proceed through positions 34°20'N, 12°30'E and 33°25'N, 13°45'E to arrive in position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E) by 0700 B/18. HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) and HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) leave Malta P.M./16 as follows to arrive patrols same time. HMS Una through positions 34°30'N, 14°11'E to position 'Y', (32°48'N, 14°11'E). HMS P 39 through position 34°30'N, 14°14'E to position 'Z', (32°46'N, 14°22'E). Object is an important enemy convoy that may be expected to arrive Tripoli shortly from the eastward. Submarines encountered at night outside area 'K' are not to be attacked unless definitely identified as enemy.

In a signal timed 1810 B/16, the Capt. S.10 had already ordered ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) to patrol in positions 'W' (32°41° 14°32'E) and 'V' (32°48'N, 14°34'E) respectively.

This was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).

Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy.

To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys.

At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona).

At 0710 B/22, the Caio Duilio squadron sighted the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoy covered by the Gorizia squadron. Scirocco left the convoy as it crossed the 34th parallel and arrived at Messina at 1424 B/23. The Caio Duilio squadron turned back and arrived at Taranto at 0202 B/24-0230 B/24. The Gorizia squadron also left the convoy and returned to Messina, arriving at 1159 B/24-1340 B/24. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (12) 17 Feb 1942 At 0012 B/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) altered course to 120° and proceeded at 10 knots to comply with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1151 B/16. At 0331 B/17, HMS P 34 was in position 34°20'N, 12°30'E and course was altered to 131° for position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. At 0725 B/17, HMS P 34 dived in position 33°56'N, 13°03'E. Course was still 131° but speed (submerged) was only 2 knots. At 2015 B/17, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued on course 131°. At 2330 B/17, HMS P 34 was in position 33°33'N, 13°45'E. Course was altered to 163°. (11) 17 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1435 B/17 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Earlier ordered dispositions anticipated the convoy approaching through position 32°48'N, 14°00'E on course 270° or course 290° and possibly course - 263°. Positions may be moved by signal course - 360° or course 180° as necessary according to air reconnaissance. If forced to retire submarines have freedom of movement as follows; P 34 west of 14°05'E. Una between 14°05'E and 14°17'E. P 39 between 14°17'E and 14°28'E. P 38 and Sokol east of 14°28'E. (13) 18 Feb 1942 At 0448 B/18, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). At 0700 B/18, HMS P 34 dived and commenced patrolling at periscope depth in the vicinity of position 'X'. At 2100 B/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled head to sea at slow speed near position 'X'. (11) 19 Feb 1942 At 0430 B/19, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 70 feet in the vicinity of position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). HMS P 34 remained at 70 feet with the Asdic watch closed up due to the weather conditions until 1645 B/19 when she commenced to patrol at periscope depth. At 2100 B/19, surfaced and proceeded to regain the patrol position and then patrolled in the vicinity of position 'X'. By midnight the weather had moderated considerably. (11) 20 Feb 1942 At 0725 B/20, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived and proceeded to regain position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). Star sighted had been obtained just five minutes before. At 2100 B/20, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol in the vicinity of position 'X'. At 2230 B/20, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2207 B/20 was received which cancelled his signal timed 1205 B/20 and ordered the submarines to return to their previous patrol positions. [For the orders contained in the signal timed 1205 B/20 see the other 'event' for 20 February 1942.] (11) 20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1205 B/20 Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). At 2000 B/20 proceed as follows; HMS P 34 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E to Area K off Kerkenah. HMS Una through positions 33°20'N, 13°45'E and 33°20'N, 12°55'E to Area N south of 33°45'W and west of 12°55'E. HMS P 39 was to operate in area L, 32°10'N, 15°00'W - 000° - 40 - 50. Three more submarines will sail shortly to concentrate again with HMS Una and HMS P 39 in the vicinity of Khoms. In a signal timed 2207 B/20 they were ordered by Capt. S.10 to return to their previous patrol positions by 0001 B/23. (13) 20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel my signals timed 1125 B/20th to P 38 and 1205 B/20th to Una, P 34 and P 39. Retire at least 50 miles within areas in my signal timed 1435 B/17th and return to the patrol positions just vacated by 0001 B/23. Further order will follow. The re-deployment / re-positioning of these submarines was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B). Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy. To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys. At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona). At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron. At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay. At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23. At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate. She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22. At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24. At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy. Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23. Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23. The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24. The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24. (13) 21 Feb 1942 At 0720 B/21, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived and patrolled in the vicinity of position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). Star sights had been obtained just five minutes before. At 2000 B/21, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol in the vicinity of position 'X'. (11) 21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 B/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); My signal timed 2207 B/20. Four submarines leave Malta through position 34°30'N, 14°10'E to positions as follows by dawn/23. P 36, P 35, Unbeaten through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E to position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E), position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), position 'S' (32°44'N, 14°47'E) respectively and Upholder direct from first position given to position 'R' (32°50'N, 14°53'E). My signal timed 1435 B/17, amend and to retire from area. P 36 and P 38, between 14°28'E and 14°40'E former south latter north of 32°45'N. East of 14°40'E, Upholder north of 32°48'N, P 35 south of 32°41'N and Unbeaten between those latitudes. Convoy may be expected any time after dawn/23 and if an eastbound convoy is encountered first that proceeding westward to Tripoli is primary target and position must not be compromised before its passage. (13) 22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots. In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots. The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (13) 23 Feb 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) fires four torpedoes against an Italian convoy about 80 nautical miles east of Tripoli, Libya in position 32°51'N, 13°58'E. All torpedoes fired missed their targets. The convoy attacked was made up of the Italian transports Ravello (6142 GRT, built 1941), Unione (6070 GRT, built 1942 and brand new) and Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940). Escort was provided by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Nicolo Zeno, Maestrale, Strale, Premuda, and the Italian torpedo boats Pallade and Calliope. The torpedoes were spotted by an escorting aircraft which machine-gunned them to give the alarm and the convoy altered course to avoid them. Unione was missed ahead and astern by two torpedoes. The destroyer Vivaldi depth charged the submarine. 0400 B/23 - P 34 dived to 70 feet in position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). Asdic watch was set. 0700 B/23 - Commenced patrolling at periscope depth. 0912 B/23 - Sighted a tanker of about 5000 tons and a small coaster of about 500 tons bearing 175°. They were proceeding on course 280° very close inshore. These were not attacked in view of the expected convoy. [We have been unable to identify these vessels. The only thing that could be found was the Italian tanker Giulio Giordani (10534 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the Italian destroyer Emmanuele Pessagno which sailed from Brindisi at 2205 B/20 via Corfu (1112 B/21-1335 B/21) and arrived at Tripoli at 1720 B/23. This does, however, not even match the description given by P 34 closely. 1125 B/23 - Sighted two merchant vessels and three destroyers bearing 040°. Enemy course was 250°. Started attack. 1130 B/23 - The convoy appeared to consist of three modern merchant ships of 9000 tons in line ahead. Seven destroyers were seen to be the escort. They were of various types. 1149 B/23 - Fired a salvo of four torpedoes from 4500 yards. 1157 B/23 - Heard three explosions. 1158 B/23 - A counter attack commenced. A total of 57 depth charges were dropped some quite near. P 34 managed to escape. Between 1408 B/23 and 1508 B/23 - Distant explosions were heard. 1420 B/23 - Both hunting destroyers were now out of sight. 1515 B/23 - Went to 60 feet and commenced to reload the torpedo tubes. Also course was altered to 300° to close the position of the attack. 1700 B/23 - Completed reloading the torpedo tubes. 1745 B/23 - Arrived in the position of the attack. Went to periscope depth. Nothing was seen. Altered course to 360° to clear the area. 2015 B/23 - Surfaced and proceeded on course 339° at 8 knots to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 B/23. [For the text of this signal see the other 'event' for 23 February 1942.] 2342 B/23 - In position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. Altered course to 308° to proceed to area 'K'. 23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E. HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E. HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E. They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (13) 24 Feb 1942 At 0230 B/24, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 2255 B/23 to the Capt. S.10 which stated the following; Your 2123 B/23. At 0912 B/23 sighted one tanker one small motor vessel close inshore. At 1149 B/23 attacked convoy of three motor vessels of about 9000 tons escorted by seven destroyers proceeding on course 250°. Result uncertain possibly three hits, other explosions heard during morning and afternoon. This was a response to the signal of Capt. S.10 timed 2123 B/23 which contained the following orders; When safe to do so make short report of what westbound ships were sighted today (23rd). Other submarines are not to report yet. (12) 25 Feb 1942 At 0225 B/25, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol in the vicinity of position 34°37'N, 11°53'E (in area 'K'). (11) 26 Feb 1942 At 2015 B/26, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) surfaced and left the area near Kerkennah no.4 buoy to return to Malta as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1240 B/25. This signal contained the following orders for HMS P 34; Arrive at Marsamxett (Malta) 0715 B/28. Proceed through positions 35°00'N, 12°32'E and 35°36'N, 14°10'E en-route to Malta. (11) 28 Feb 1942 At 0734 B/28, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11) 11 Mar 1942 At 1930 B/11, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Calabrian coast from Cape Spartivento. For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 34 during this patrol see the map below. (11) 13 Mar 1942 At 0730 B/13, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol at periscope depth in the vicinity of Cape Spartivento. She had dived to 70 feet at 0513 B/13 with Asdic watch closed up. At 2035 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced. Course was set to proceed to the vicinity of Punta Stilo to patrol there in accordance with the patrol orders. (11) 14 Mar 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian submarine Ammiraglio Millo (offsite link) in the Ionian Sea off Punta (Cape) Stilo, Calabria, Italy in position 38°27'N, 16°37'E. 0500 B/14 - Dived to 70 feet with Asdic watch set. Commenced to close the land. 0730 B/14 - Commenced to patrol at periscope depth and patrolled in the vicinity of Punta Stilo. 1130 B/14 - Sighted a small merchant vessel close inshore proceeding on course 035°. Turned towards and closed. 1136 B/14 - Sighted a dazzle painted merchant ship of 3000 tons. This looked like, and probably was, an armed merchant cruiser of the Citta di Napoli-class. Another small merchant ship, of about 3000 tons was sighted astern of this suspected armed merchant cruiser. Started attack on the leading ship which was zigzagging up to 80° and at irregular intervals. The other two ships were on a steady course. One aircraft was seen patrolling overhead, escorting this convoy. Although P 34 closed at high speed, the attack was abandoned at 1222 B/14. The range had never been less than 7500 yards. It was unfortunate the convoy had not been sighted sooner but the visibility towards the land at that time was bad. The ships sighted were probably the steamers Enrichetta (Italian, 4652 GRT, built 1907) and Pegli (Italian, 1593 GRT, built 1910), the tanker Ennio (Italian, 464 GRT, built 1917) escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Francesco Morosini (2423 GRT, built 1928) which sailed from Messina at 0205 B/14 at 8 knots and arrived at Taranto at 0925 B/15. 1255 B/14 - Sighted the conning tower of a submarine bearing 270°. Range was about 8000 yards. Started attack. 1319 B/14 - Fired four torpedoes from 2200 yards. Two hits were obtained and the submarine was seen to sink. 1325 B/14 - Surfaced and picked up fourteen survivors. These included four officers and ten ratings. 1402 B/14 - Dived and withdrew from the area. 1700 B/14 - Went to 80 feet and commenced to reload the torpedo tubes. 2015 B/14 - Surfaced and proceeded on course 180° at 9.5 knots. Lt. Harrison had decided to return to Malta to land the Italian survivors and embark four new torpedoes. A signal on P 34 returning to Malta was passed at 0015 B/15. On the way back P 34 was ordered to proceed through position 37°13'N, 16°45'E. 16 Mar 1942 At 0750 B/16, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta to land the Italian survivors and to load four new spare torpedoes and provisions. (11) 17 Mar 1942 At 1930 B/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta to resume her patrol. She was now ordered to patrol to the south of the Straits of Messina between positions 37°13'N, 16°02'E and 37°32'N, 15°44'E (preferably in position 'L', 37°36'N, 15°49'E, until 0400 B/20 when she was to move to the position vacated by HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN). At 1958 B/17, when in position 060° - St. Elmo light - 1 mile a motor torpedo boat was seen laying stopped which did not reply to the challenge. HMS P 34 dived. At 2005 B/17, this motor torpedo boat dropped 12 small charges in the vicinity of P 34. HE was heard on the starboard quarter until 2120 B/17. At 2123 B/17, HMS P 34 surfaced in position 068° - St. Elmo light - 3.5 miles. On surfacing a motor torpedo boat was seen laying stopped about 800 yards on the starboard beam. P 34 dived again. At 2135 B/17, this motor torpedo boat dropped 14 small charges in the vicinity of P 34. HE was lost at 2358 B/17. [We have been unable to identify this suspected motor torpedo boat.] At 0010 B/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 071° at 5 knots with the Asdic watch closed up until 0105 B/17 when speed was increased to 9.5 knots when the battery charge was completed. No German S-boats (motor torpedo boats) were operating of Malta during this night. Perhaps the motoro torpedo boats encountered were Italian MAS boats. 19 Mar 1942 At 1030 B/19, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'L' (37°36'N, 15°49'E) and commenced in this vicinity on courese 090° - 270°. At 2100 B/19, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol in the vicinity of position 'L'. (11) 20 Mar 1942 At 0400 B/20, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) set course for position 'M' (37°43'N, 15°33'E) in accordance with orders received. Around 0800 B/20, she arrived in position 'M' and patrolled at periscope depth on course 090° - 270° through position 'M'. At 1230 B/20, the masts of a small schooner were sighted bearing 360° at a range of about 6 miles. At 1305 B/20, a small object was sighted bearing 030° at a range of about 6 miles. This was a small boat which may have been a motor torpedo boat. Both the schooner and motor torpedo boat were laying stopped or proceeding very slowly. It is considered both were on A/S patrol. At 1500 B/20, both the schooner and motor torpedo boat passed out of sight in the direction of the Straits of Messina. At 2100 B/20, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol on course 090° - 270° through position 'M'. (11) 21 Mar 1942 At 0150 B/21, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) picked up HE on bearing 133°. At 0210 B/21, it was reported that the HE was that of a motor boat but nothing was seen. HMS P 34 dived. The HE was then reported as being from a reciprocating engine doing about 80 revs. This was considered to most likely be from a trawler. The vessel, which could still not be seen, passed across starboard quarter and faded out at 0230 B/21 on bearing 090°. As the battery was full and Allied aircraft were known to be operating in the vicinity Lt. Harrison decided to remain dived. At 0300 B/21, HE was heard on bearing 020°. This passed down the starboard side and was very probably a destroyer or torpedo boat. Two depth charges were heard to explode at 0430 B/21. After that this HE practically faded out then re-appeared passing up the starboard side, finally fading out on bearing 100° at 0530 B/21. Perhaps the HE heard was of a ship of the convoy made up of the German transport Brook (1225 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian destroyer Strale and the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari which sailed from Messina at 0015 B/21. At 0428 B/21, the convoy was attacked by Swordfish torpedo bombers, 18 miles south of Capo dell'Armi; two torpedoes missed Strale. At 1200 B/21, the torpedo boat was detached and proceeded to Trapani where she arrived at 0800 B/22. The other two vessels arrived at Argostoli at 0845 B/22-0915 B/22. During the night of 22/23, Brook ran aground in the Argostoli anchorage but managed to free herself without damage. They sailed again at 1745 B/26 and arrived at Navarino at 0744 B/27 and sailed from there at 1930 B/27. They arrived at Benghazi at 0917 B/29-1100 B/29. At 0700 B/21, HMS P 34 came to periscope depth and continued to patrol in the vicinity of position 'M' (37°43'N, 15°33'E). At 1020 B/21, a motor torpedo boat was sighted on bearing 260°. This motor torpedo boat was painted white. Range was about 4 miles. The enemy was proceeding on course 020° towards Messina at 20 knots. This was not an A/S vessel. This was the German S-boat (large motor torpedo boat) S-59 which had departed Augusta at 0830 B/21 for Palermo where she was to undergo an engine refit. At 2100 B/21, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol through position 'M' on course 090° - 270°. (14) 22 Mar 1942 At 0205 B/22, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) picked up HE bearing 010°. Course was altered towards to investigate but nothing was seen. At 0210 B/22, HMS P 34 dived. The HE was reported as being from a reciprocating engine doing 80 revs. The source of the HE passed down the starboard side fading out at 0230 B/22 on bearing 050°. This was thought to be coming from the same trawler as on the previous night. At 0245 B/22, HMS P 34 surfaced. At 0500 B/22, HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet. Asdic watch was set. At 0700 B/22, HMS P 34 came to periscope depth, patrolled in the vicinity of position 'M' (37°43'N, 15°33'E). At 1005 B/22, four distant explosions were heard followed by several single explosions at short intervals. At 2100 B/22, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled head on to sea at slow speed on course 138° due to the weather conditions. (11) 23 Mar 1942 At 0400 B/23, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet with Asdic watch set. She remained patrolling at 80 feet after daybreak due to the weather conditions being unsuitable for periscope patrol. In a signal timed 1744 B/23, from the Capt. S.10, HMS P 34 was informed that she had freedom of action areas 'M' and 'N' after 0800 B/24. At 2100 B/23, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled at slow speed, head on to sea on course 100°. At 2230 B/23, HMS P 34 received a signal timed 1941 B/23 from the Capt. S.10 that an enemy 6" cruiser had broken off from an Italian task force and was possibly proceeding single towards Messina. This probably referred to the Italian light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Francesco Stocco which was returning to Messina and was reinforced by the torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cantore at 1145 B/24. They arrived at Messina at 1345-1354 B/24. (15) 24 Mar 1942 At 0400 B/24, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch closed up. At 0800 B/24, it was decided to remain at 80 feet as the weather conditions were not suitable for periscope depth patrol. At 1048 B/24, HE was picked up bearing 035°. Went to periscope depth but sighted no surface vessel. An aircraft was seen patrolling though. At 1100 B/24, a puff of smoke was sighted on bearing 076° but nothing could be seen of a vessel. Not even with 10 feet of periscope. HE was lost at 1105 B/24 and at 1115 B/24 the patrolling aircraft approached, so P 34 went back to 80 feet. This vessel could not have been the expected cruiser as it was leaving the Straits of Messina instead of approaching but it may possibly have been a destroyer or torpedo boat proceeding to seaward to rendezvous with the reported cruiser. At 1715 B/24, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1426 B/24 was received which contained the following orders for P 34; P 34 is to leave patrol P.M./25 to position 37°00'N, 15°40'E to arrive at Malta A.M./27. (15) 25 Mar 1942 At 0130 B/25, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the signal timed 0040 B/25 from the Capt. S.10. This signal contained the following information. Two enemy cruisers at 2350 B/24 in position 36°25'N, 16°48'E, course 330° speed 15 knots. (this position was amended later in the signal timed 0121 B/25, to 36°45'N, 16°45'E). Albacores will attack around 0300 B/25. No enemy cruiser(s) was / were at sea on this day. These were the actually the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade which sailed from Messina at 0625 B/24-0635 B/24 to search for survivors of the Italian destroyer Scirocco which had foundered.
