S.10's signal timed 0826 B/13 was received which contained the following orders; Enemy cruisers retired. Dive immediately course 040° convoy escort will attack submarines south of 36°10'N after 0900 B/13. from 1008 B/13 onwards, intermittent underwater explosions were heard. These were only intese for a short period around 1045 B/13. During this period several enemy aircraft were sighted, mostly dive bombers. At 1725 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1227 B/13 was received which contained the following orders for HMS P 34; Proceed through position 36°24'N, 11°59'E to area 'H', west of 12°01'E and between 35°50'N and 36°45'N. (off the Gulf of Hammamet). At 2130 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 180° at 6 knots to get clear of HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) with which S.S./T. contact had showed that at 2043 B/13 she was bearing 290° at only 3300 yards. At 2200 B/13, HMS P 34 altered course to 264° to proceed towards the Gulf of Hammamet. At 2250 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1723 B/13 was received which contained the following orders; Convoy Operation not yet completed. HMS P 34, HMS P 44, HMS P 46 and HMS Utmost are to keep north-east of the line Gozo - Pantellaria until further orders. Course was set to 060° to comply with the orders received. (27) 13 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 0345 B/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) were informed of the following; Convoy two hours late. Delay easterly movement on the surface until 0800 B/13 and dive at 1400 B/13. In a signal timed 0826 B/13, the Capt. S.10 informed the above submarines of the following; Enemy cruisers retired. Dive immediately course 040° convoy escort will attack submarines south of 36°10'N after 0900 B/13. (28) 14 Aug 1942 At 0108 B/14, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet when an aircraft dropped a flare right overhead. At 0225 B/14, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 060° at 4 knots. At 0235 B/14, another aircraft flare was dropped overhead and once again HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet surfacing again an hour later when she proceeded on course 360° at 4 knots. At 0520 B/14, HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet commencing to patrol at periscope depth near position 36°40'N, 13°00'E, 40 minutes later. At 1400 B/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1029 B/14 was received which contained the following orders for her; Proceed as in my signal timed 1227 B/13 to operate temporarily west of 12°01'E, between 36°45'N and 36°20'E. At 1410 B/14, course was altered to 252° to comply with the above orders. At 2120 B/14, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued on course 252° but now at a speed of 7 knots while charging. Speed was increased to 9 knots two hours later. (29) 15 Aug 1942 At 0515 B/15, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch closed up. At 0530 B/15, P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth. Pantelleria Island was in sight and HMS P 34 patrolled on course 270° about 20 miles to the south of Pantelleria. At 1815 B/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1121 B/15 was received in which P 34 was ordered to patrol in position 36°33'N, 11°50'E. Course was altered to 073°. At 2120 B/15, HMS P 34 surfaced and set course 063° to proceed to the ordered patrol position. (29) 15 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1625 B/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN); One merchant ship and one destroyer in position 38°43'N, 12°56'E, course 205°, speed 15 knots at 1615 B/15. Aircraft may attack before 0200 B/16. In a follow up signal timed 2231 B/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to P 34 and P 44; One merchant ship and two destroyers in position 37°25'N, 11°47'E, course 180°, speed 15 knots at 2200 B/15. [For the composition and further movements of this convoy see the event for 16 August 1942.] (28) 16 Aug 1942 At 0020 B/16, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 36°33'N, 11°50'E and commenced to patrol in the expected line of advance of an enemy convoy. The expected convoy was made up of the Italian transport Rosolino Pilo (8326 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Maestrale and Vincenzo Gioberti which sailed from Naples for Tripoli at 0615 B/15-0700 B/15. When it was learned that an important enemy convoy had sailed from Gibraltar (operation PEDESTAL), they were ordered to Trapani where they arrived at 0414 B/16-0605 B/16. They finally sailed from Trapani at 0530 B/17-0635 B/17. They were attacked by four Beaufort torpedo bombers from 86 Squadron (RAF), Rosolino Pilo was hit by a torpedo at 1550 B/17 in position 35°55'N, 11°49'E; Vincenzo Gioberti was machine-gunned and had about 50 wounded, including her Captain. She turned back and arrived at Trapani at 2200 B/17. Two MAS boats sailed from Pantelleria to assist the freighter. Maestrale was ordered to turn back and arrived at Trapani at 0316 B/18. The tug Montecristo had been ordered to take her in tow. Rosolino Pilo was finished off by a torpedo from HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) at 0009 B/18. There were no casualties. She carried 4000 tons of stores for the Germans with a civilian crew of 50 and a military crew of 32 Italians and 39 Germans, and 110 German passengers. She had already been abandoned by her passengers and crew before the submarine attack, except her captain and four crew. Members who had remained on board and were evacuated shortly before she sank. Montecristo picked up the survivors. At 0120 B/16, the generators were stopped and Asdic watch was set. Although visibility was good and HE conditions perfect nothing was seen or heard of the expected convoy and it is considered that the convoy could not have passed within 10 miles of the patrol position. At 0528 B/16, HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch still closed up. At 0600 B/16, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth in the vicinity of position 36°33'N, 11°50'E. At 1330 B/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0930 B/16 was received which ordered HMS P 34 to operate in the original patrol area. At 2133 B/16, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 270° to patrol in the vicinity of the Kurba Bank. (29) 17 Aug 1942 At 0300 B/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 36°26'N, 11°11'E and commenced to patrol in the vicinity of that position. At 0400 B/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0242 B/17 was received which ordered HMS P 34 to patrol in position 36°34'N, 11°53'E (south-south-west of Pantelleria), by 1001 B/17. Course was set to 076° to comply with these orders. Speed was increased to 9 knots. At 0520 B/17, a large amount of oil fuel was sighted in the vicinity of position 36°30'N, 11.30'E. At 0600 B/17, HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet continuing on course 076° at 3 knots. At 1200 B/17, HMS P 34 was in position 36°37'N, 11°50'E. Course was altered to 150° to proceed to the ordered position. At 1310 B/17, the bridges and funnels of two destroyers were sighted on bearing 050°. These destroyers were escorting a merchant vessel. Range was about 6 to 7 miles. Enemy course 180°. Position of the convoy was 36°38'N, 12°00'E, just to the south of Pantelleria. It was quite clear the convoy was going to pass out of range. The nearest range obtained was about 10000 yards. The merchant vessel was seen to be a modern 8000-tons one, she was also camouflaged. An air escort of at least six aircraft was patrolling in the area. The convoy was last seen at 1845 B/17 steering 180°. At 1700 B/17, smoke was sighted bearing 180°. This turned out to be a destroyer, probably one of the convoy escort, returning, steering 360° at 25 to 30 knots. The enemy passed at a range of about 5000 yards. His smoke was last seen to the northward at 1725 B/17. At 1950 B/17, another destroyer passed steering 005° at between 25 to 30 knots. At 2053 B/17, HE was picked up on bearing 290°. This disappeared to the eastward. At 2130 B/17, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded towards the Gulf of Hammamet to patrol in the vicinity of the Kurba Bank. (27) 18 Aug 1942 At 0010 B/18, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) observed a large explosion to the southwards. This was considered to be a merchant vessel blowing up after having been torpedoed by either the RAF or a submarine [in fact this was HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) handywork]. The explosion was in the position where flares had been dropped at 2230 B/17. At 0246 B/18, a darkened shape was seen bearing 090° which was proceeding towards but not at high speed as no bow wave was seen. P 34 dived to avoid being sighted. This was almost certainly a destroyer but no attack developed and the enemy apparently had not seen the submarine. At 0430 B/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued her passage. At 0535 B/18, HMS P 34 dived with the Asdic watch set. At 0615 B/18, periscope depth patrol was commenced patrolling in the vicinity of the Kurba Bank. At 1245 B/18, two puffs of smoke were seen to the north-west, very close inshore. The originators of the smoke were never sighted but it is considered they were most likely Vichy-French coastal traffic. At 1400 B/18, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1140 B/18 was received which ordered her to also patrol in the southern limit of the patrol area (where P 44 had been patrolling). The patrol area now thus also included Kerkennah and Lampion. The 2130 B/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded to the southward on the estimated Kuriat - Kelibia traffic route with the intention to patrol near Kuriat the following day. (27) 19 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1140 B/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Leave patrol as necessary through positions 35°13'N, 12°31'E and 35°37'N, 14°08'E to R/V with minesweeper at end of the searched channel at 0959 B/23. (28) 20 Aug 1942 At 0200 B/20, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) which had patrolled on the estimated route between Kuriat and Kelibia since 2130 B/19, altered course to 245° to proceed towards Sousse. At 0530 B/20, HMS P 34 dived to 60 feet with the Asdic watch closed up. At 0630 B/20, she came to periscope depth and continued on towards Sousse. The mine detector unit was run all the time. During the forenoon the anchorage off Sousse was investigated in the hope that Italian phosphate ships might be at anchor but nothing was sighted and HMS P 34 retired to seaward. At 1513 B/20, course was set to 210° to proceed towards Monastir to investigate. No shipping was subsequently seen there either and once again P 34 retired to seaward. At 1620 B/20, in position 35°53'N, 10°47'E, a small vessel was seen bearing 145° steering 270°. A motor torpedo boat appeared to be astern of the vessel and it was accordingly decided that it was a small but valuable ship, although the course she was steering indicated otherwise. An attack was started and P 34 closed at high submerged speed. At 1630 B/20, the vessel was identified as the Vichy-French Optimiste (????) towing a large sized motor launch. The attack was broken off. The vessel was clearly marked with French national colours on the bridge, funnel and ship's sides. At 2030 B/20, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled on the suspected convoy route between Kuriat and Kelibia. (27) 21 Aug 1942 At 0010 B/21, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) altered course to 123° to patrol in the vicinity of Lampedusa on the Pantelleria - Kerkennah convoy route. At 0530 B/21, HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch closed up. One hour later periscope depth patrol was commenced in the vicinity of position 35°31'N, 11°57'E. At 2130 B/21, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded towards Kerkennah. At 2300 B/21, HMS P 34 set course to return to Malta in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1140 B/19. (27) 23 Aug 1942 At 0825 B/23, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was off the end to the searched channel and sighted her minesweeper escort. At 0850 B/23, HMS P 34 surfaced and closed the minesweeper which turned out to be HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). At 0900 B/23, HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) also surfaced and in company the three ships proceeded up the searched channel to Malta. At 1205 B/23, HMS P 34 ended her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. Only vessels out of range were observed. (27) 2 Sep 1942 At 1530 B/2, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the West coast of Greece. However on making a trial dive shortly after 1800 hours, the D.S.E.A. escape hatch was found to be leaking badly and the problem could not be found. During a second trials dive the problem was just as bad as during the first trials dive. Also it had been noticed that the port propeller shaft was very noisy at slow speeds and at 1823 B/2, Lt. Harrison decided to return to Malta to have the defects rectified. At 2000 B/2, HMS P 34 was back at Malta. For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 34 during this patrol see the map below. (27) 3 Sep 1942 At 0630 B/3, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), having completed her repairs, sailed again to resume her patrol. On making a trials dive the D.S.E.A. hatch was found to be satisfactory but the port propeller shaft was still very noisy at slow speed. It was decided to continue on patrol in the hopes the problem to the port shaft would rectify itself. She was to pass through position 35°22'N, 15°05'E around 0430 B/4 and then proceed towards position 38°23'N, 19°45'E (west of Cephalonia). (27) 4 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1639 B/4, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); P 34 proceed direct to arrive in position 37°55'N, 19°57'E by 0301 B/6 thence to centre of area ?Z [????]. P 43 proceed direct to arrive in position 36°06'N, 20°48'E by 2259 B/6. Passage to be made on the surface during daylight as necessary to comply. Further instructions will be signalled by Capt. S.1. (30) 5 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1615 C/5, sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Cancel the previous orders. Proceed to area 'S', southern half. Diving by day. further instructions will follow. On receipt of this signal HMS P 34 was in position 37°19'N, 18°38'E. Course was set to 105° to comply with the orders received. At 2104 B/5, course was changed to 145° to comply with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1707 C/5 which had contained the following orders; My 1615 C/5. Proceed through positions 36°58'N, 19°15'E and 36°30'N, 20°42'E, thence operate in the south-west corner of area 'S'. (30) 6 Sep 1942 At 2150 C/6, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1850 C/6 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 35°48'N, 21°37'E. Course was altered to 127° and speed was increased to 8 knots to comply with these orders. At 2350 C/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2115 C/6 was received moving the patrol position 318° - 40 miles. Course was altered to 103° and speed was reduced to 6 miles to comply with these orders. (27) 6 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1850 C/6, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders; Proceed forthwith diving by day to following positions; HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), 35°48'N, 21°37'E HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), 35°15'N, 21°00'E HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), 35°05'N, 21°51'E HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) 33°33'N, 21°42'N HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, DSO, RN) only to be in position 32°31'N, 23°34'E by dawn September 8th. In a signal timed 2047 B/6, they were informed that three merchant ships and ten destroyers had been reported at 1740 B/6 in position 38°46'N, 20°13'E proceeding on course 170° at 10 knots. They were also informed that aircraft may attack the enemy convoy north of Navarino tonight. In a signal timed 2115 C/6, further instructions were sent by Capt. S.1. HMS P 34, HMS P 43 and HMS Una move to intercept from positions ordered in my 1850 C/6 as follows; HMS P 34, 318° - 40 miles. HMS P 43, 024° - 32 miles. HMS Una, 250° - 18 miles. Be in new positions by dawn 7th Sept. HMS Traveller, ignore my 1850 C/6 and be in position 32°45'N, 20°35'E by 0001 C/8. These submarines were to operate against an Italian convoy operation; At 0036 B/6-0150 B/6, convoy 'N', consisting of the transports Sestriere (Italian 7991 GRT, built 1942) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere (escort leader), Lampo, Legionario (equipped with new German DETE Gerät) and the Italian torpedo boats Pegaso and Partenope sailed from Brindisi. At 0012 B/6-0205 B/6, convoy 'P', consisting of transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Manara (Italian 7124 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the italian destroyers Fuciliere (escort leader), Bombardiere, Corsaro, Freccia, Camicia Nera, Geniere and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade sailed from Taranto. The two convoys were to merge for defensive purposes, but then to split, this time the three Italian ships proceeding to Benghazi and Ankara to Tobruk. At 0100 B/6, the torpedo boat Procione sailed from Piraeus to reinforce the convoy. At 1040 B/6, the two convoys merged and the destroyer Aviere assumed overall command. The combined convoy was now known as convoy 'L'. At 1525 B/6, Procione joined convoy and was thus present during air attack when Manara was torpedoed at 1530 B/6. The air attack was carried out by fourteen to sixteen torpedo bombers, Manara was hit in the stern section and taken in tow by Freccia and escorted by Legionario and Procione. They proceeded to Corfu. At 1855 B/6, Legionario left them to rejoin the convoy. At 1003 B/7, Manara was brought to the Bay of Arilla (near Cape Cavobianco, Corfu). At 1810 B/6, the convoy split. Ankara escorted by the destroyers Fuciliere (which resumed duty as escort leader), Geniere, Lampo, Bombardiere and the torpedo boat Partenope slowed down at 10 knots to distance itself from Ravello and Sestriere escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Legionario, Corsaro, Camicia Nera and the torpedo boats Pallade and Pegaso, which forged ahead. The Ankara section was now known as Convoy 'P', the Ravello section as Convoy 'N'. At 0449 B/7, Legionario rejoined the Ankara convoy. At 0547 B/7, the Ankara convoy sighted the Ravello/Sestriere convoy and at 0737 B/7, the two convoys merged again and were now known, once again as convoy 'L', with Aviere once more assuming overall command. At 0927 B/7, Lampo reported torpedo tracks in position 36°10'N, 21°05'E (this was the attack by HMS P 34) and Sestriere signalled she had avoided torpedoes from the port side by turning to starboard. Lampo was ordered to hunt the submarine. At 1355 B/7, she signalled that the A/S search had not yielded result and that she expected to rejoin the convoy at at about 1900 B/7. She caught up at 1738 B/7. At 1810 B/7, three enemy aircraft attacked the combined convoy which opened up with antiaircraft fire. Bombs fell near Ankara. At 1941 B/7, the convoy split again, Fuciliere assuming command of convoy 'P' which proceeded to Tobruk. Lampo had been assigned to join the escort of the Benghazi convoy, but as it had been reduced to two ships, Fuciliere requested from Aviere that she reinforce the Ankara convoy and this was granted. Convoy 'N' was now formed of Ravello and Sestriere escorted by Aviere, Corsaro, Legionario, Camicia Nera, Pegaso and Pallade. At 0216 B/8, in position 33°30'N, 22°43'E, a low-flying aircraft dropped two or three bombs which narrowly missed Fuciliere (2 metres!) causing massive damage, the shock disabled her gyro compass and her radio but she managed to make 12 knots and remained with the convoy. At 0302 B/8, Fuciliere, by visual signal, ordered Geniere to take over command of the convoy. At 0353 B/8, Fuciliere left the convoy, to make for Crete. Partenope had also turned north to escort her but Fuciliere ordered her to rejoin the convoy. At 0850 B/8, Bombardiere informed Fuciliere that she had been ordered by Geniere to assist her. Fuciliere replied that she was in urgent need of a towing. Bombardiere was proceeding at 25 knots but had trouble locating Fuciliere as the latter could only give a very approximate position due to the breakdown of her gyrocompass. She finally found her at 1830 B/8 near Sphakia and at 2355 B/8 had taken the stricken vessel in tow. At 0729 B/9, Geniere rejoined them. At 0849 B/9, they dropped anchor at Castel Selino (SE Crete) and at 1230 B/9, the Italian destroyer Nicolò Zeno arrived to assist them. At 1810 B/9, the four destroyers left for Navarino where they arrived at 0905 B/10-0919 B/10. In the meantime, at 0636 B/8, the Ankara convoy was joined by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Brioni (1987 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the torpedo boat Orione which had sailed from Suda at 1330 B/7. Ankara, Brioni, Geniere, Lampo, Partenope and Orione arrived at Tobruk at 1315-1428 B/8. Geniere sailed at 1701 B/8 for Taranto but was later diverted to assist Fuciliere (see above). Convoy 'N' arrived at Benghazi at 1021 B/8-1321 B/8. Ravallo, Sestriere, Legionario and Pallade entered harbour while Aviere, Corsaro and Camicia Nera remained outside, awaiting instructions from Supermarina, while Pegaso was detached to assist Fuciliere but eventualle Pegaso was recalled and entered Benghazi at 2000 B/8. (31) 7 Sep 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) fired four torpedoes at an enemy convoy in the central Mediterranean about 45 nautical miles south-west of Schiza Island, Greece in position 36°17'N, 21°03'E. All torpedoes fired missed their target. The convoy consisted of the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, 1941), Sestriere (Italian, 7991 GRT, 1942) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Legionario, Fuciliere, Bombardiere, Geniere, Camicia Nera, Corsaro, Lampo and the torpedo boats Pegaso, Partenope and Pallade. Sestriere reported to have been missed by the torpedoes, which tracks had also been sighted by Lampo which was ordered to hunt the attacking submarine. She later signalled the results of this hunt as negative. 0207 B/7 - Arrived in position 36°18'N, 21°04'E and commenced patrolling in the vicinity of this position. 0520 B/7 - Dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch set. 0550 B/7 - Commenced to patrol at periscope depth. 0823 B/7 - Sighted various aircraft to the north-westward. These were Ju 88's and Cant's. Some of these were seen to drop bombs. Most likely these aircraft were escorting the expected convoy. 0835 B/7 - Sighted masts and funnels of the approaching convoy bearing 305°. 0840 B/7 - Started attack on the convoy which was soon seen to be made up of three large merchant ships in line ahead. Many destroyers formed the escort. Several aircraft were also overhead. The leading merchant vessel was an older type of about 6000 tons, the other two were modern ones of 9000 tons. 0921 B/7 - In position 36°17'N, 21°03'E fired four torpedoes at the rear ship from 7000 yards. Seventeen seconds after firing the first torpedo there was a very heavy explosion just ahead of P 34. The first torpedo must have prematured after running off its safety range. 0927 B/7 - Heard a torpedo exploding giving a running range of 8000 yards corresponding with the range on firing of 7000 yards. 0936 B/7 - A counter attack commenced by three destroyers using hydrophones. Several patterns of depth charges were dropped rather close but to shallow. P 34 was at 120 feet at the time. 1030 B/7 - An Asdic fitted destroyer took over the hunt from the other three. 1056 B/7 - The destroyer passed overhead and dropped a pattern of four depth charges very close causing some damage. 1226 B/7 - After hearing no HE and no Asdic transmissions for a while started to return to periscope depth. 1236 B/7 - Head Asdic transmissions on the starboard beam. 1238 B/7 - At periscope depth. Saw a destroyer at a range of 2500 yards on our starboard beam. Went deep again. The destroyer was in Asdic contact. 1258 B/7 - The destroyer passed overhead and dropped a pattern of 5 depth charges which were again very close causing some more damage. This was followed by another pattern a bit further off about one minute later. P 34 remained deep for the remainder of the afternoon. 1700 B/7 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. During the counter attack a total of 83 depth charges had been counted. 1715 B/7 - Went ahead on the port shaft. This resulted in clouds of black smoke which soon filled the submarine. A fire had started, as a result of damage from the depth charging, when starting up the motor so the port motor was placed out of action. 1744 B/7 - Surfaced for 5 minutes to clear the boat of the smoke. 1830 B/7 - Commenced to reload the torpedo tubes. 2030 B/7 - All tubes were now reloaded. One torpedo was found to be defective. 2124 B/7 - Surfaced and set course to return to Malta as a result of the damage inflicted by the depth charging. It was not possible to transmit a signal as the W/T was out of action. (27) 7 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 0956 C/7, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, DSO, RN), HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). On completion of attacks submarines should if possible follow up convoys to finish off damaged ships. This should be a standard practice, A/S measures permitting. In a follow up signal timed 1015 C/7, these submarines were informed that Allied aircraft had been ordered not to attack submarines unless positively identified as enemy in areas 'K', 'B' and 'T'. In a signal timed 1514 B/7 and amended in a signal timed 1619 B/7, these submarines were informed by Capt. S.10 that three transports and nine destroyers had been sighted at 1450 B/7 in position 35°30'N, 21°40'N, on course 170° at 10 knots. In a signal timed 2001 B/7, the Capt. S.10 informed these submarines that aircraft from Malta hope to attack the enemy convoy about midnight tonight. (31) 7 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1837 C/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). If nothing has been sighted by dark proceed patrol area 'K' in sectors from position (A) 35°42'N, 23°28'E as follows: HMS Una, 170° to 215°. HMS P 43, 215° to 235°. P 34, 235° to 270°. Except when in pursuit of enemy submarines should keep to their sector, P 34 and Una not to approach within 5 miles of position (A) and P 43 within 10 miles of position (A). Attention drawn to my signal timed 1015/7th [see above]. (31) 8 Sep 1942 At 2145 B/8, in position 36°10'N, 18°50'E, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a hospital ship on bearing 160° proceeding in the direction of the Straits of Messina on course 320°. This was the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) which departed from Tobruk at 1700 B/6 via Derna (0800 B/7-1830 B/7) and arrived at Naples at 0940 B/10. (27) 9 Sep 1942 At 0010 B/9, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) finally was able to pass her signal timed 2105 B/8 which contained the following; At 0926 B/7 in position 36°17'N, 21°03'E obtained one hit on rear 9000 ton ship of the convoy. Port main motor out of action. Am returning to Malta through positions 36°16'N, 18°56'E and 35°22'N, 15°05'E to arrive at 0959 B/13. This signal was acknowledged by the Capt. S.1 in his signal timed 0412 C/9. In a signal timed 1041 B/9, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 34 to proceed further through positions 35°12'N, 15°01'E and 35°12'N, 14°09'E on her way to Malta. (27) 13 Sep 1942 At 1230 B/13, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She, and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 0930 B/13. (27) 24 Sep 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Malta. (32) 25 Sep 1942 At 1445 B/25, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was to proceed to Gibraltar as she was to return to the U.K. to refit. En-route she was ordered to patrol of the south-west coast of Sardinia, south of 39°30'N. She departed Malta in company with HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN). They were escorted out until around 1700 B/25 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN). For the daily positions of HMS P 34 during this patrol see the map below. (27) 27 Sep 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) made the westbound submerged passage though the Italian declared mine area to the south / south-west of Sicily. (27) 30 Sep 1942 From 30 September to 2 October 1942, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) patrolled off the south-west coast of Sardinia but nothing at all was sighted. (27) 3 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0330 B/3, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) reported the following to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.); My position 39°01'N, 06°58'E, speed of advance 105 miles per day. In a reply, timed 0536 A/3, this signal was acknowledged. P 34 was ordered to proceed through position 39°01'N, 06°01'E and 38°52'N, 02°57'E. Further orders / routing would follow. In a signal timed 1036 A/3, F.O.C.N.A. signalled the following; (1) My 0536 A/3, Proceed dived by day through positions 38°52'N, 02°57'E at 0030 A/5 and 180° - Gape de Gata - 10 miles at 1600 A/7 thence arriving Gibraltar 1500 A/8 proceeding on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion on 8th. (2) Submerged bombing restrictions arranged 20 miles either side route from 35 miles ahead to 35 miles astern estimated position 1036 A/3. (3) Total bombing restrictions arranged on 8th. (4) Report if unable to remain inside bombing restrictions. (33) 4 Oct 1942 At 1750 B/4, in position 39°00'N, 03°30'E, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted the upperworks of a merchant ship bearing 300°. Course of this ship was 180°. Range 10 miles. This was most likely a Vichy-French vessel proceeding to Algiers. (27) 6 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 2101 A/6 (passed at 2257 A/6), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) reported the following to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.); Your 1036 A/3, My position 37°21'N, 00°24'W. E.T.A. 0730 A/9. In a reply timed 0906 A/7, F.O.C.N.A. replied as follows; Your 2101 A/6 acknowledged. Do not cross 05°00'W before 0700 A/9. Arrive at Gibraltar at 0900 A/9. (27) 8 Oct 1942 At 1225 B/8, in position 36°14'N, 04°04'W, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a merchant vessel proceeding on course 080° at 8 knots. This was considered to be most likely a Spanish vessel. (27) 9 Oct 1942 As HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) approached Gibraltar, she was detected by the RDF station on the Rock. As she was west of 05°W and submarines had been instructed not to cross the 5°W meridian before 0700 A/9, the destroyer HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN) was sent to intercept. HMS P 34 had meanwhile dived due to heavy fog which was fortunate. Even more fortunate was that the Asdic set of HMS Wishart was out of action. Soon after diving a ship was heard to pass overhead and this was soon seen to be a British destroyer. At 0650 A/9, HMS P 34 surfaced and identified herself to HMS Wishart which was about 4 miles away. Around 0930 A/9, P 34 ended her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (27) 18 Oct 1942 At 1430 A/18, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K. She was to pass through positions 225° - Cape St. Vincent - 20 at 2100 A/19, thence diving by day through position 39°01'N, 12°01'W at 0800 A/21, 43°01'N, 12°58'W at 1900 A/23 towards 49°05'N, 12°10'W. Belgrave, RNVR) at 0800 A/29 in position 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles for onward escorted surface passage to Portsmouth. 29 Oct 1942 At 0800 A/29, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was in position 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles. In the bad weather conditions, an easterly gale with bad visibility, her escort, HMS Egilsay (T/Lt. W.E. At 1530 A/29, the weather had moderated and P 34 closed the land and confirmed her position and then carried out a search for the trawler which was finally sighted at 1800 A/29. The destroyer HMS Tuscan (Lt.Cdr. G.I.M. Balfour, RN) and escort destroyers HMS Blankney (Lt.Cdr. D.H.R. Bromley, RN) and HMS Brecon (Lt.Cdr. T.D. Herrick, DSC and Bar, RN) were escorting the battleship and cruiser. (40) 4 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted attack exercises off Scapa Flow. (40) 5 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. Shelley, CBE, RN) conducted gunnery exercises at Scapa Flow. These were followed by underway refuelling exercises. She first fuelled the destroyer HMS Onslow (Capt. J.A. McCoy, DSO, RN) and later HMS Active (Lt.Cdr. P.G. Merriman, RN). She then conducted an exercises with a submarine, which must have been HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR). (40) 15 May 1943 Around 2200 B/15, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Scapa Flow for Lerwick. At sea she joined HMS Tuna (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS Seadog (Lt. C.R. Pelly, RN) and their escort HMS Bryony (T/Lt. T. Hand, RNR). Earlier in the day, HMS Ultimatum had carried out gunnery exercises off Scapa Flow. (40) 16 May 1943 At 1255 B/16, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) arrived at Lerwick from Scapa Flow. (40) 18 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted A/S exercises off Lerwick with aircraft. (40) 21 May 1943 At 0600 B/21, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Lerwick for her 16th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Norwegian Sea for an anti-Uboat patrol. For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (27) 24 May 1943 At 1708 B/24, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) received the signal timed 1447 B/24 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) in which it was reported that there were two German U-boats within 120 miles of Ultimatum's position. HMS Ultimatum at that time was in approximate position 64°32'N, 05°20'W. This most likely referred to the German submarines U-467 and U-488. The exact position of the German U-boat U-467 is not known but it looks likely that she was already passed HMS Ultimatum. U-467 had departed Bergen on 20 May 1943 for the North Atlantic but was sunk on 25 May 1943 to the south of Iceland in position 62°25'N, 14°52'W by a Fido homing torpedo from a US Catalina aircraft. There were no survivors. U-488 had departed Kristiansand (South) on 20 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. At 1600 B/24, she was in German naval grid AE 6735 (centred in 64°21'N, 08°52'30"W) which was a little over 90 miles from HMS Ultimatum's position. This too was already past the patrol position of HMS Ultimatum. The was also U-193 which had departed Bergen for the North Atlantic on 22 May and at 1600 B/24 was in German naval grid AF 4947 (centred in 63°57'00"N, 000°37'30"E) which was about 160 miles from HMS Ultimatum's position and not yet past her. (42) 26 May 1943 At 1706 B/26, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) received the signal timed 1443 B/26 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) in which it was reported that there was a German U-boat within 99 miles of Ultimatum's position. At 1517 B/26, HMS Ultimatum was in position 64°48'N, 05°14'W. This most likely referred to the German submarine U-667 which had departed Kristiansand (South) on 22 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. At 1600 B/26, U-667 was in German naval grid AF 4586 (centred in position 64°39'00"N, 000°22'30"W) which was about 120 miles from HMS Ultimatum's position. (43) 31 May 1943 At 1830 B/31, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) received the signal timed 1646 B/31 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) in which it was reported that there were two German U-boats within 120 miles of position 64°59'N, 05°01'W. This most likely referred to the German submarines U-341 and U-535. U-341 had departed Kristiansand (South) on 27 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. At 1600 B/31 she was in German naval grid AE 6694 (64°39'00"N, 004°37'30"W) which was a little over 20 miles from the position reported. U-535 had departed Kiel on 25 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. Her exact position is not known as she was lost on 5 July 1943 during her first war patrol. There were no survivors. which had departed Kristiansand (South) on 22 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. At 1600 B/26, U-667 was in German naval grid AF 4586 (centred in position 64°39'00"N, 000°22'30"W) which was about 120 miles from HMS Ultimatum's position. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted exercises off Blyth. (47) 11 Aug 1943 Early in the evening, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR), HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) and HMS Uther (Lt. P.S. Beale, RN) departed Blyth for Holy Loch. They were escorted by HMS ML 300 (T/Lt. F.C.R. Piesse, RNVR) until Dundee where HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR) took over the escort. (47) 14 Aug 1943 At 1430 B/14, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Holy Loch from Blyth. (47) 16 Aug 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Kingfisher (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) G.H. Gandy, RN) serving as target. Also gunnery exercises were carried out. (47) 17 Aug 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Scott (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Sharpey-Schafer, RN) serving as target. Also gunnery exercises were carried out. (47) 17 Aug 1943 HMS Varangian (Lt. J. Nash, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Scott (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Sharpey-Schafer, RN), HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville). (48) 20 Aug 1943 At 1906 A/20, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Holy Loch for her 17th war patrol. She was to patrol in the Bay of Biscay (A/S patrol) and to proceed to Gibraltar upon completion of this patrol. Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS Truant (Lt.Cdr. J.G. Hopkins, RN), Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville) and Minerve (Lt. H.F.D. Simon-Dubuisson). They were escorted by HMS Kingston Amber (T/Lt. Richard Adams, RNR). At 0130 A/20, HMS Ultimatum parted company with the other submarines and HMS Kingston Amber in position 180° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles. HMS Ultimatum was to proceed through positions 'UA' (49°01'N, 07°30'W), 'UB' (48°01'N, 07°30'W) thence direct to anti U-Boat patrol in zone 'J 7'. HMS Ultimatum was to leave patrol to proceed through position 'UC' (45°01'N, 06°01'W) at 0800 A/31. Thence by route 'Gold' to 07°00'W, thence inside 100-fathom line to pass through position 'ZZ' 43°01'N, 09°45'W at 0400 A/3 for onward passage to Gibraltar. For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (49) 22 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 2206 A/22, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); Cancel previous instructions. Prom position 'UB' (48°01'N, 07°30'W) HMS Ultimatum proceed direct diving by day to patrol in zone 'J 10'. From position 'CA' (48°10'N, 07°01'W) Curie proceed direct diving by day to patrol in zone 'J 11'. (50) 24 Aug 1943 At 1850 A/24, in approximate position 46°58'N, 07°15'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted a southbound sailing vessel. Investigation showed this to be a tunny fishing vessel which was indeed fishing at the moment. At 2245 A/24, a darkened ship was sighted. An attack was started. At 2300 A/24, this vessel was identified as a tunny fishing vessel thought to be the same one seen at 1850 A/24. (51) 27 Aug 1943 At 0130 A/27, in approximate position 45°42'N, 05°42'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted a distant white light. Ultimatum closed to investigate. At 0213 A/27, the white light was identiefied as a tunny fishing vessel which was proceeding under sail. Ultimatum altered course away and resumed patrol. At 0715 A/27, a sailing vessel was sighted which was closed. Again this was a French tunny fishing vessel. At 0837 A/27, HMS Ultimatum resumed patrol. (51) 28 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1216 A/28, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); You are to leave patrol after dark on 30th August and 31st August respectively and proceed dived by day through position 'UA' 46°01'N, 07°01'W to position 'UB' 46°01'N, 12°54'W thence route 'Green' to cross 43°01'N at 0300 A/5 and 0300 A/6 respectively. Daily advance 90 miles. (50) 29 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1028 A/29, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); Curie and HMS Ultimatum proceed after dark today to position 46°48'N, 04°48'W and position 04°15'N, 05°15'W respectively. Leave patrol to comply with my 1216 A/28. In a signal timed 1033 A/29, the following was signalled to these two submarines; Route 'Green' is course 180° from position 46°01'N, 12°54'W. (50) 30 Aug 1943 At 0848 A/30, in approximate position 46°17'N, 05°14'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted smoke and topmasts of two vessels bearing 116°. Course was altered to investigate. At 0902 A/30, the vessels appeared to be three trawlers hull down steaming in a single line ahead on an estimated course of 220°. All three were making large quantities of smoke. HMS Ultimatum continued closing at speed. At 0910 A/30, the trawlers, which could barely be seen through the periscope due to the heavy swell, appeared to have altered course to 180° and may have stopped. Two sailing fishing vessel were now also in sight close to the trawlers. At 0915 A/30, visibility was decreasing owing to rain and only a high vertical column of smoke was visible. HMS Ultimatum continued to close the sailing vessels which were eventually identified as tunny fishermen with side booms rigged. At 1005 A/30, HMS Ultimatum resumed patrol at periscope depth. (51) 31 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1529 A/31, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); (1) Cancel route given in para. 1 of my 1216 A/28. (2) Ultimatum passes through position 'UM' 46°00'N, 07°50'E at 2000 A/31 thence 180° to join route 'Gold' in position 44°00'N. 07°50'E thence by route 'Gold' to cross 43°00'N at 2200 A/3. (3) Curie joins route 'Gold' in 46°30'N at 0200 A/1 thence by route 'Gold' to cross 43°00'N at 0500 A/5. (4) Friendly aircraft may operate with flares. They will be informed of your movements. (50) 31 Aug 1943 At 0159 A/31, in position 46°10'N, 05°55'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted a darkened ship. An attack was started. At 0210 A/31, the vessel was identified as a sailing vessel which was showing a dimmed stern light. HMS Ultimatum altered course away and continued her passage. (51) 1 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 0911 A/1, the Admiralty informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) that several U-boats were reported in her immediate vicinity. HMS Ultimatum was at that time in approximate position 44°50'N, 07°55'W. [There were quite a number of U-boat in and off the Bay of Biscay on this day but none in the immediate vicinity of HMS Ultimatum.] (52) 1 Sep 1943 In a signal timed the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar, set the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR); Following is onward route after crossing 43°00'N at 2300 B/3. Proceed though position 38°50'N, 10°01'W. at 1715 B/6th. Then though positions 'U' (36°51'N, 09°13'W at 01(?50)B/8, 'V' (36° 37'N, 07°24'W at 0200 B/9, 'X' 35°53'N, 06°15'W at 1140 B/9, 'T' (35°49'N, 06°06'W) at 1230 B/9, thence by Q.L.A. 87 [no idea what this means !] to Gibraltar. Speed of advance, diving by day, 88 miles per day. Do not cross Meridian 07°00'W before 0630 B/9 but thereafter proceed on surface at your discretion at 9 knots. Submerged bombing restrictions arranged until 0200 B/9 thereafter total bombing restrictions 10 miles either side of your route from 35 miles ahead, to 35 miles astern of your estimated position. Report if unable to keep within your bombing restrictions. Note all times mentioned are zone minus 2 (B). (53) 3 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 0954 B/3, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville) were informed of the following by the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar; U-boat attacked by aircraft in position 42°32'N, 10°09'W at 0545 B/3. Enemy course had been 090°, speed 10 knots. This referred to U-600 which had been attacked by Wellington 'J' of 179 Squadron of the RAF. (53) 5 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1022 B/5, the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar, informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville) of the following; Aircraft sighted a submarine in position 41°14'N, 11°02'W. Enemy course was 020° at 14 knots at 0059 B/5. This submarine was not attacked. It was good that this 'enemy submarine' was not attacked as it was in fact HMS Ultimatum. The patrol report of Ultimatum gives the following (note the one hour time zone difference); 0006 A/5 - An aircraft burning a blue searchlight illuminated the submarine. Dived to 90 feet. 0020 A/5 - Came to periscope depth. Set radar watch. 0032 A/5 - Surfaced and continued passage while maintaining radar watch. Resumed zigzagging. (54) 9 Sep 1943 Around 1550 A/9, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 17th war patrol at Gibraltar. (27) 16 Sep 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted exercises with the harbour defences of Gibraltar. (55) 18 Sep 1943 At 1615 B/18, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Gibraltar for her 18th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Lions. Between 1645 B/18 and 2300 B/18 exercises were carried out on completion of which HMS Ultimatum proceeded on patrol. HMS Ultimatum was to proceed through positions 'P' (180° - Cape Calaburras - 10 miles) at 0220 B/19, 'Q' (180° - Cape de Gata - 10 miles) at 1630 B/19 and 'R'(135° - Cape Palos - 10 miles) at 0430 B/20. Further routing was ordered as follows by the Capt. S.8.; Position 38°32'N, 00°36'E at 1500 B/20, 39°47'N, 01°11'E at 0115 B/21, 42°08'N, 04°54'E at 0430 B/22, thence diving by day through 42°30'N, 05°30'E to patrol between 05°00'E and 06°30'E North of 42°30'N. Do not patrol inside 100 fathom line unless absence of mined is indicated by movements of shipping. Land targets in France were not to be attacked. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (27) 19 Sep 1943 At 1200 B/19, in position 36°31'N, 02°39'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted a Spanish merchant vessel proceeding on course 260°. HMS Ultimatum altered course away to avoid being sighted. At 2030 B/19, in approximate position 36°49'N, 01°35'W, a lighted merchant vessel was sighted on which a dummy attack was started. At 2045 B/19, this vessel was seen to wearing most likely a Portugese ensign. She was also showing a white flag with a red cross. Course of this vessel was 240°. At 2300 B/19, the Swiss merchant vessel Alqula (????) was sighted steering 240°. She was wearing a lighted national flag and the name 'Alqula' was illuminated beneath the funnel. (27) 21 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1630 B/21, the Capt. S.8 sent the following to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Your patrol area has been extended to 07°00'E. The south-east limits being the line joining 43°00'N, 07°00'E and 42°30'N, 06°30'E. A profitable area for patrol may be in Gulf of Frejus South of Cape Camarat and South of Hyeres Islands. Enemy radar stations are known to be situated in Hyeres Islands and one near Cape Sicie. (56) 25 Sep 1943 At 0340 B/25, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2328 B/24 [or 2338 B/24] which contained the following orders; After 0001 B/27 the eastern limit of your patrol area is 06°01'E. Aréthuse (Lt. J.M. Gouttier) will be operating to the eastward of you. (27) 27 Sep 1943 At 1045 A/27, south of Toulon, in approximate position 42°56'N, 06°01'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted smoke, funnel and mast of a vessel leaving Toulon. This vessel was subsequently identified as an armed trawler which proceeded eastwards out of range. The vessel was painted light grey with a black top to a long thin funnel. A small calibre gun, approximately 3", was mounted on the forecastle. No A/S impulsed was heard. Perhaps this was the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2216 which conducted a trial off Toulon on this day. On her return to harbour (see below) this vessel was seen to be probably towing a gunnery target which if this was indeed UJ 2216 would make perfect sense as this vessel besides the trial, also carried out gunnery exercises. At 1326 A/27, while patrolling 4 miles south of Cape Sicie, smoke was sighted to the westward. HMS Ultimatum turned towards and ran in to attack. During the approach gun flashes were observed from the shore and splashes 40 to 50 feet high were seen to the eastward. No gunnery target could be made out. When the land and target had been closed to within 4000 yards it was seen to be a small drifter towing a lighter. Estimated speed of the enemy was 5 knots steering just a quarter of a mile from the shore. Considered that the size of the target did not justify firing a torpedo and divulging the submarine's position so Lt. Kett resumed patrol south of Cape Sicie. The drifter and it's tow were seen to be steaming on towards Toulon. This was most likely the German auxiliary netlayer Netztender 44 towing a lighter which had departed Marseille at 0910 B/27 and arrived at Toulon at 1645 B/27. At 1630 A/27, the armed trawler, that had been seen in the morning, was seen to return to harbour. It may have been towing a gunnery target, although the range and swell prevented accurate observation. (57) 29 Sep 1943 At 1338 A/29, off Cassidaigne Reef (south-east of Marseille), in approximate position 43°07'N, 05°30'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a motor torpedo boat bearing 270° at a range of 6 miles. The enemy was proceeding at speed in the direction of Marseilles. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (27) 30 Sep 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) attacked an escorted German convoy of self propelled barges. Two torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained. It looks like one of the torpedoes prematured. This convoy was made up of three self propelled barges and was escorted by the Auxiliary minesweepers M 6041, M 6043, M 6044, M 6045, M 6046 and M 6047. The convoy departed Marseille around 0715 B/30 to Toulon where it arrived around 1730 B/30. Nothing was reported by the German escorts and it appears they were not aware of the attack despite HMS Ultimatum stating that listening charges were dropped. 1224 A/30 - Sighted a vessel bearing 340°, close to the land. Altered course towards. 1230 A/30 - Next look revealed that target consisted of four large self propelled supply barges escorted by seven armed patrol vessels. Continued to close. 1247 A/30 - In position 43°02'N, 05°44'E started firing a salvo of four torpedoes from very close range. After two torpedoes had been fired a violent explosion shook Ultimatum. One of the torpedoes must have hit. Depth control was lost and Ultimatum started to descend. The remaining two torpedoes of the salvo were not fired. 1250 A/30 - Ultimatum came to rest on the bottom at 160 feet. Both motors were stopped, all machinery was stopped and the ship was shut off for depth charging. Many listening charges were dropped by the enemy for the next 7 hours but no depth charges were dropped during this period. 2000 A/30 - A depth charge was dropped but it was not very close. 2230 A/30 - Started blowing main ballast. 2245 A/30 - Ultimatum came of the bottom. 2300 A/30 - Obtained a good trim at 90 feet. Cleared the area not surfacing until 0120 hours the next day. (57) 30 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1102 A/30, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Cancel my signal timed 2338 A/24. Your patrol area is extended to 07°00'E. The south east limit being the line joining 43°00'N, 07°00'E and 42°30'N, 06°30'E. Leave patrol diving by day through position 42°30'N, 06°17'E at 0600 A/2 and 42°01'N, 06°12'E at 2100 A/2. Thence on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through positions 39°59'N, 05°14'E at 1300 A/3 and 38°02'N, 03°13'E at 0800 A/4 to make rendezvous with the escort into Algiers in position 37°06'N, 03°12'E at 1500 A/4. (58) 1 Oct 1943 At 1500 A/1, while patrolling to the south-east of Marseille, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small vessel steaming to the inside of Riou Island. HMS Ultimatum closed to investigate. At 1727 A/1, when the range had been reduced to 3000 yards the vessel was seen to be a French pilot cutter. French national marking were painted below her bridge and while letters were painted on the hull. This vessel was considered not worth a torpedo although presenting a sitting target lying stopped 1.5 miles due south of Cassis. It was hoped this vessels presence suggested the arrival or departure of someting bigger to or from Cassis or Ciotat. Nothing however developed and at 1930 A/1, HMS Ultimatum retired to seaward. (27) 2 Oct 1943 At 0200 A/2, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) left patrol to proceed to Algiers. [No logbook is available of HMS Ultimatum for the month of October 1943. Some details for this month may thus be missing.] (27) 3 Oct 1943 At 1200 A/3, in position 39°48'N, 05°14'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted HMS Seraph (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, OBE, RN) proceeding northwards on the surface. HMS Ultimatum was dived and was not detected by HMS Seraph. (59) 4 Oct 1943 At 1610 A/4, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 18th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers. At 1355 A/4, she made rendezvous with HMS Staffa (T/Lt. R.L. Roat, RNVR) which then escorted her to the harbour. (27) 12 Oct 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) is docked at Algiers to make repairs to the propellers and the Asdic dome. (60) 15 Oct 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) is undocked. (60) 18 Oct 1943 Convoy KMS 30. This convoy was assembled near Oversay on 18 October 1943. It was made up of the following merchant vessels; Amstel (Dutch, 2115 GRT, built 1925), Baron Forbes (British, 3061 GRT, built 1915), Baron Stranraer (British, 3668 GRT, built 1929), Begum (British, 5843 GRT, built 1922), Belgian Seaman (Belgian, 7023 GRT, built 1941), Blairdevon (British, 3282 GRT, built 1925), Cape Hawke (British, 5081 GRT, built 1941), Carlton (British, 7210 GRT, built 1942), Celtic Monarch (British, 5824 GRT, built 1929), Corfirth (British, 1803 GRT, built 1934), Coulmore (British, 3670 GRT, built 1936), Cydonia (British, 3517 GRT, built 1927), Dan-Y-Bryn (British, 5117 GRT, built 1940), Empire Ballad (British, 6700 GRT, built 1942), Empire Cameron (British, 7015 GRT, built 1941), Empire Copperfield (British, 6013 GRT, built 1943), Empire Tern (British, 2479 GRT, built 1919), Eridan (French, 9928 GRT, built 1928), Errington Court (British, 4913 GRT, built 1925), Esneh (British, 1931 GRT, built 1919), Fort Fairford (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943), Fort Liard (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942), Fort Livingstone (British, 7135 GRT, built 1942), Fort Reliance (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Tadoussac (British, 7129 GRT, built 1941), Greathope (British, 2297 GRT, built 1926), Harberton (British, 4585 GRT, built 1930), Lech (Polish, 1568 GRT, built 1934), Ledaal (Norwegian, 3076 GRT, built 1899), Lodestone (British, 4877 GRT, built 1938), Marwarri (British, 8067 GRT, built 1935), Nolisement (British, 5084 GRT, built 1928), Olga E. Embiricos (Greek, 4677 GRT, built 1922), Riverton (British, 7307 GRT, built 1943), Sinnington Court (British, 6910 GRT, built 1928), Stancleeve (British, 5970 GRT, built 1942), Topdalsfjord (Norwegian, 4271 GRT, built 1921) and Wearwood (British, 4597 GRT, built 1930). On assembly the convoy was escorted by the destroyers HMS Highlander (Cdr. C.W. McMullen, DSC, RN), HMS Walker (Lt.Cdr. A.N. Rowell, RN) and the corvettes HMS Abelia (Lt. R.I. Floris, RNZNR), HMS Asphodel (Lt.Cdr. H.P. Carse, DSC, RNVR) and HMS Pennywort (A/Lt.Cdr. O.G. Stuart, RCNVR). The corvette HMS Clover (Lt. T.E. Fanshawe, RNR) was unable to join them due to defects but sailed later to overtake and join the convoy at sea which she did on 20 October. The Blairdevon and Fort Livingstone collided in position 55°39'N, 07°21'W, not long after assembly of the convoy. Both ships being heavily damaged and the Blairdevon was even in a sinking conditiom but both ships were successfully towed to Moville where they arrived on 19 October. HMS Pennywort remained behind with the damaged ships but later rejoined the convoy. The convoy encountered heavy weather conditions and several ships were forced to turn back, these were; Amstel, Baron Forbes, Begum, Empire Ballad and Nolisement. The Begum had to be towed back as she had lost her propeller. On 23 October 1943, the merchant vessels Corfell (British, 1802 GRT, built 1934), Scottish Monarch (British, 7004 GRT, built 1943) departed Ponta Delgada, Azores to join the convoy around 1200 Z/26 in position 41°53'N, 18°30'W. They were escorted by the destroyer HMS Viscount (Lt.Cdr. L.E. Woodhouse, RN) which was not to join the convoy. From 26 to 28 October 1943, HMS Warwick and HMS Asphodel were not with the convoy as they had to fuel at Ponta Delgada and then rejoin the convoy. During their absence the destroyer HMS Warwick (Cdr. Y.M. Cleeves, DSO, DSC, RD, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Buttercup (T/Lt. R.J. Jonckheere, RNR) and HMS Lavender (Lt. L.G. Pilcher, RNR) were with the convoy. Also joining the convoy were the destroyers HMS Active (Lt.Cdr. P.G. Merriman, DSC, RN) and HMS Brilliant (Lt.Cdr. J. Smallwood, RN). They had departed Gibraltar on 27 October with orders to join the convoy around 1800 A/28 in position 37°37'N, 18°00'W. On these destroyers the three escorts from the Azores were then to part company and return to the Azores. The convoy arrived off Gibraltar during the night of 31 October / 1 November 1943. The Corfirth arrived at Horta on 3 November after having been detached from the convoy. [or straggled from the convoy ?.] The Cydonia arrived at Lisbon on 1 November after having been detached from the convoy. The following ships were detached to Gibraltar; Cape Hawke, Carlton, Corfell, Dan-Y-Bryn, Empire Copperfield, Esneh, Fort Tadoussac, Greathope, Lech, Olga E. Embiricos, Scottish Monarch and Stancleeve. The original escort also proceeded to Gibraltar and was replaced by a new escort made up of the AA cruiser HMS Colombo (Capt. D.H. Hall-Thompson, RN), escort destroyer HMS Haydon (Lt.Cdr. R.C. Watkin, RN), frigate HMS Barle (Lt.Cdr. A.H. Davies, RNVR), corvettes HMS Bryony (T/Lt. T. Hand, RNR), HMS La Malouine (Lt. W.A. Ives, RNR) and the A/S trawler L'Ajacienne. The following merchant ships joined the convoy off Gibraltar; Benjamin Huntington (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), British Merit (British (tanker), 8093 GRT, built 1942), Empire Derwent (British, 4026 GRT, built 1930), Empire Trent (British, 5006 GRT, built 1927), F.T. Freylinghuysen (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), George Bancroft (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), John Mary Odin (American, 7244 GRT, built 1943), Jonathan Trumbull (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Joseph Warren (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Montaigne (French, 2770 GRT, built 1920), Nemours (French, 673 GRT, built 1935), Norjerv (Norwegian, 5582 GRT, 1919), Orminster (British, 5712 GRT, built 1914), Puck (Polish, 1065 GRT, built 1935), Wearfield (British (tanker), 9795 GRT, built 1943), William B. Woods (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and William Coddington (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943). On 2 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Oran; Coulmore, Empire Tern, George Bancroft, Joseph Warren and Montaigne while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Oran; Alexander Graham Bell (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Augustus S. Merrimon (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Caid (British, 255 GRT, built 1906), Fort de Douaumont (French, 5266 GRT, built 1918), Francis L. Lee (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), George B. McClellan (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), George M. Bibb (American, 7180 GRT, built 1943), Grand Quevilly (French, 2844 GRT, built 1914), Joseph Hewes (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Kemp P. Battle (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Kosciuszko (Polish, 6852 GRT, built 1915), Leland Stanford (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Mont Viso (French, 4531 GRT, built 1921), Robert Dale Owen (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Robert F. Stockton (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Selvik (Norwegian, 1557 GRT, built 1920), William C. Endicott (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), William F. Cody (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and Zebulon Pike (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942). The minesweeper HMS BYMS 2186 (?) also joined the convoy. Around 0051 A/3, the merchant vessel Mont Viso was torpedoed and sunk by the German submarine U-593. HMS Bryony went to the assistance of the torpedoed ship but it sank in two minutes. The corvette then picked up the survivors and later obtained an asdic contact on the enemy and attacked with depth charges during which the German submarine sustained damage due to which she was to return to Toulon for repairs. Around 0900 A/3, HMS Colombo was detached to return to Gibraltar. Between around 1100 A/3 and 1410 A/3 she was in the area where the Mont Viso had been sunk searching for survivors but none were found. She arrived at Gibraltar around 0805 A/4. On 3 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Algiers; Belgian Seaman, Caio, Empire Trent, Eridan, Fort Fairford, Fort Reliance, Lodestone, Orminster and Topsdaldjord as was HMS Haydon, while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Algiers; Anna Capano (Italian, 1345 GRT, built 1938), Chateau Pavie (French, 2047 GRT, built 1930), Empire Daring (British, 7059 GRT, built 1943), George C. Childress (American, 7245 GRT, built 1943), Lars Kruse (British, 1807 GRT, built 1923), Letitia (British, 13595 GRT, built 1925), Pan-Delaware (American (tanker), 8128 GRT, built 1918), Richmond Mumford Pearson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and Ronan (British, 1489 GRT, built 1938). HMS Bryony also made a short stop at Oran to land the survivors of the Mont Viso before rejoining the convoy. The corvette HMS Bergamot (Lt. R.T. Horan, RNR) also joined the convoy off Algiers as did the submarines HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN). On 4 November 1943, the Empire Daring arrived at Bougie after having been detached from the convoy. On 4 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Philippeville; George C. Childress, Letitia and Sinnington Court while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Philippeville; Afghanistan (British, 6992 GRT, built 1940), Cap Padaran (British (former French), 8009 GRT, built 1922), Fort Fork (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Lawrence (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943), Norman Monarch (British, 7005 GRT, built 1943) and Prometheus (British, 6095 GRT, built 1925). On 4 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Bone; Empire Derwent, F.T. Freylinghuysen, John Mary Odin, Jonathan Trumbull, William B. Woods and William Coddington while the following merchant vessel joined the convoy coming from Bone; Yenangyaung (British, 5447 GRT, 1937). On 5 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Bizerta; Anna Capano, George M. Bibb, Grand Quevilly, Kemp P. Battle and Wearfield while the following merchant vessel joined the convoy coming from Bizerta; Spero (Norwegian, 3619 GRT, built 1919). On 5 November 1943 the Errington Court was detached to Bizerta. On 6 November 1943 the Fort Liard, Puck and Selvik were detached to Malta as were HMS Ultimatum, HMS Ultor and HMS BYMS 2186. On 6 November 1943 the Afghanistan, Alexander Graham Bell, Augustus S. Merrimon, Baron Stranraer, Benjamin Huntington, British Merit, Cap Padaran, Celtic Monarch, Empire Cameron, Fort de Douaumont, Fort Fork, Fort Lawrence, Francis L. Lee, George B. McClellan, Harberton, Joseph Hewes, Kosciuszko, Lars Kruse, Leland Stanford, Norman Monarch, Pan-Delaware, Prometheus, Richmond Mumford Pearson, Riverton, Robert Dale Owen, Robert F. Stockton, Ronan, Wearwood, William C. Endicott, William F. Cody and Zebulon Pike were detached to Augusta while the following merchant vessel joined the convoy coming from Augusta; Brighton (British, 7345 GRT, built 1943), City of Windsor (British, 7218 GRT, built 1923), Comliebank (British, 5149 GRT, built 1924), Fort la Traite (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Pembina (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Newbrough (British, 5255 GRT, built 1941), Pellicula (British (tanker), 6254 GRT, built 1936) and Talma (British, 10000 GRT, built 1923). On 11 November 1943 the Brighton, City of Windsor, Comliebank, Fort la Traite, Fort Pembina, Norjerv, Talma and Yenangyaung arrived at Alexandria after having been detached from the convoy. They took HMS Bergamot and HMS Bryony with them. The remainder of the convoy arrived at Port Said also on 11 November escorted by HMS Barle and HMS La Malouine. HMS Barle then continued on toward Haifa where she arrived on the 12th. 19 Oct 1943 At 1640 A/19, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Algiers for her 19th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Southern France in area 'ST'. She was routed as follows; Through positions 38°01'N, 03°21'E at 0130 A/20, 40°02'N, 05°15'E at 1900 A/20, 41°59'N, 06°12'E at 1930 A/21 and 42° 30'N, 06°16'E at 0001 A/22 to patrol in zone 'ST'. Proceed on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion on 19th and 20th there after diving by day. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (61) 22 Oct 1943 In a signal timed 1002 A/22, The Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); You are to operate in zones 'SU' and 'ST' forthwith until 0100 A/25. (62) 24 Oct 1943 At 1735 A/24, while patrolling near Toulon, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted seven F-boats, painted brown, steaming towards Toulon. They were steaming widely spaced in single line ahead and appeared to have approached through the Little Pass between Porquerolles and Giens Peninsula. [We have been unable to identify these lighters. It is doubtful these were F-boats though as none should have arrived at Toulon on this day.] (27) 26 Oct 1943 At 0230 A/26, while patrolling near Toulon, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a brightly illuminated ship approaching from the direction of Cape Sicie. HMS Ultimatum closed to investigate. At 0307 A/26, the vessel was observed to be a German repatriation ship with a grey or white hull, and the word 'Freigeleite' painted on her side between two Swastikas on a red background in a white circle. As this vessel was known to be present in the area with the exception that she appeared to have sailed from Toulon instead of Marseille to approach was broken off and HMS Ultimatum dived. The vessels estimated course was 195° at 12 knots. At 0319 A/26, HMS Ultimatum surfaced and resumed patrol. Several hospital ships were in the area; The German hospital ship Djenné (Former French, 8790 GRT, built 1931) which had departed Marseille at midnight during the night of 25/26 October 1943 bound for Barcelona where she arrived at 1400 B/26. The German hospital ship Aquileia (former Italian, 9448 GRT, built 1914) sailed from Marseille at 1430 B/25 [she had arrived at Marseille at 1030 B/24 from Oran) and arrived at Barcelona at 2230 B/26. A third hospital ship, the German Gradisca (former Italian, 13868 GRT, built 1913) was at Marseille and was to sail on the 28th for Patras. We do not have information on her arrival there. Perhaps she was the one sighted ? Djenné sailed from Marseille at 2400 B/25 and arrived at Barcelona at 1400 B/26, hospital ship Aquileia sailed from Marseille at 1430 B/25 [she had arrived at Marseille at 1030 B/24 from Oran) and arrived at Barcelona at 2230 B/26. A third hospital ship Gradisca was at Marseille and was to sail on the 28th for Patras. I do not have information on her arrival there. Perhaps she was the one? At 1300 A/26, a small trawler was seen in Toulon Bay. She had a white funnel with one black band, yellow deck houses and a black hull. Shortly afterwards the trawler was seen to proceed towards the harbour. At 1415 A/26, the trawler reappeared and seemed to be patrolling in Toulon Bay. Around this time the auxiliary minesweeper M 6004 arrived at Toulon after having escorted a small convoy made up of a small harbour defence boat and three barges (convoy 5361). They came from Le Ciotat which they had departed around 0700 A/26. The trawler sighted may thus have been M 6004 which arrived at Toulon around 1345 A/26. She had parted company with the small convoy at 1300 B/26 which then also proceeded into Toulon. At 1720 A/26, HMS Ultimatum sighted what was thought to be the conning tower of a U-boat leaving Toulon. Range was 7 miles. Lt. Kett altered course to close on a 120° track and ran in at full speed. The enemy's estimated course was 130°. The target was against the land and in fading daylight and shortly afterwards could no longer be seen but HE could be picked up on the correct bearing. At 1738 A/26, the attack was broken off as the range could not be closed sufficiently and Lt. Kett would not fire torpedoes from long range using an Asdic bearing only. This must have been U-593 departing Toulon for a patrol near Algiers around 1715 A/26 escorted out by the auxiliary minesweepers M 6001, M 6002 and M 6005. At 2000 A/26, HMS Ultimatum picked up HE of a fast motorboat on bearing 095°. At 2020 A/26, the HE increased while the bearing remained steady. At 2040 A/26, the HE faded while the bearing remained steady. Eight minutes later no more HE was heard and HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 42°51'N, 06°00'E. (63) 27 Oct 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) attacked a German convoy made up of the Danish transport (under German control) Nicoline Maersk (4194 GRT, built 1925), German transport Canosa (former Italian, former French, 3823 GRT, built 1936) and the small German tanker Arpino (former Italian, former French, 584 GRT, built 1935). They were escorted by the German patrol vessel SG 15, auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2210, UJ 2211 and the German motor minesweepers R 10, R 14, R 15. No hits were obtained. This convoy had departed Genoa around 0830 A/26 bound for Marseille where the entire convoy arrived around 1635 A/27. No ships of the convoy entered Toulon though despite Lt. Kett thinking this was the case. 0855 A/27 - HE was reported ahead. 0905 A/27 - Sighted a motor torpedo boat approaching at speed. Took evasive action. The enemy passed close down the starboard side. 0920 A/27 - The enemy returned now passing close down the port side. 0931 A/27 - The motor torpedo boat dropped listening charges. 0940 A/27 - Sighted a second motor torpedo boat and also a flying boat patrolling low over the water. 0945 A/27 - Went to 80 feet. 0955 A/27 - HE now became very loud, passed close by and drew aft. The motor torpedo boats continued to patrol quite close until at ... 1040 A/27 - The enemy's HE faded out in the direction of Toulon so returned to periscope depth. 1110 A/27 - Heard A/S impulses ahead but sighted nothing. 1120 A/27 - Heard HE ahead. Several barrage balloons were sighted. Closed at speed. 1144 A/27 - Sighted a convoy of four or five ships. Range was about 8 nautical miles. They were making for Toulon. 1154 A/27 - Two merchant vessels escorted by two trawlers were seen to have left the convoy which was then entering Toulon Bay. The two merchant vessels and two trawlers which had split off proceeded to the westward, apparently towards Marseille. Ran in to attack one of these two merchant ships. 1219 A/27 - In approximate position 42°58'N, 05°56'E fired four torpedoes at the second merchant ship from 7000 yards. Went deep on firing. 1225 A/27 - Heard a torpedo explosion. 1228 A/27 - Heard the other three torpedoes explode against the land. 1229 A/27 - Heard the A/S trawlers hunting. 1240 A/27 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted only the two trawlers and the first merchant vessel of the line. Assumed the other ship to have sunk. Retired to seaward to reload. (64) 28 Oct 1943 At 0705 A/28, while patrolling off Toulon, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) heard HE bearing 095°. At 0708 A/28, two motor torpedo boats passed very close by and proceeded at speed into Toulon harbour. The were probably either S 58 and S 60 which arrived at Toulon from Italy at 0730 A/28 or S 57 and S 153 which arrived at Toulon five minutes after the first pair. At 0830 A/28, two motor torpedo boats were sighted leaving Toulon and proceeding to seaward passing very close to HMS Ultimatum. [We have been unable to identify these two 'motor torpedo boats'] At 1000 A/28, two armed trawlers were seen to leave Toulon and then commence to patrol to the east of Cape Cepet. These were the German auxiliary minesweepers M 6004 and M 6005 which left Toulon around this time. At 1100 A/28, a two-funneled merchant vessel was sighted bearing 210° at a range of 6 miles. Course was altered to close. At 1125 A/28, the course of the merchant vessel was estimated to be 335° at 12 knots. HMS Ultimatum went to 80 feet to close as an aircraft was seen patrolling the area shortly before. At 1133 A/28, back at periscope depth the merchant vessel was identified as a German repatriation vessel, thought to be either Djenne (Former French, 8790 GRT, built 1931) or Sinaia (Former French, 8567 GRT, built 1922), although neither vessel was expected. After having closed the land in the vicinity of Cape Sicie the vessel altered course and proceeded to Marseilles. The attack was broken off. At 1220 A/28, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0945 A/28 was received which reported the German hospital / repatriation vessel Djenne being in the vicinity. [The vessel sighted was indeed Djenne which arrived at Marseille around 1420 A/28. M 6004 and M 6005 had been ordered to meet her.] At 1233 A/28, a small naval drifter passed close by HMS Ultimatum proceeding eastwards. At 1305 A/28, an armed trawler was seen bearing 334° patrolling to the south of Cape Cepet. She was dropping listening charges at frequent intervals. The last listening charge was heard to be dropped at 1550 A/28. At 1830 A/28, a lighted ship was seen approaching from seaward bearing 211°. At 1900 A/28, this vessel was identified as being the German hospital / repatriation ship Aquilea (former Italian, 9448 GRT, built 1914). Ten minutes later this ship was seen to be signalling to the shore after which she altered course and proceeded towards Marseilles where she arrived around 0200 A/29 escorted by M 6005. [M 6004 and M 6005 had again left Toulon around 1830 A/28 to meet Aquilea. M 6004 arrived back at Toulon at 2000 A/28. They were apparently not sighted by HMS Ultimatum.] At 2028 A/28, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0952 A/28 was receiving in which area 'SU' was extended to 06°10'E. At 2140 A/28, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 42°57'N, 06°00'E and commenced a battery charge. At 2151 A/28, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1302 A/28 was received which contained the following orders; Leaves patrol proceeding diving by day on 31 October thence proceed on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through positions 42°30'N, 05°21'E at 2330 A/30, 41°53'N, 06°09'E at 0700 A/31, 40°01'N, 05°16'E at 0730 A/1, 38°02'N, 03°18'E at 0230 A/2 to rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 37°04'N. 03°11'E at 0930 A/2. (65) 29 Oct 1943 At 0853 A/29, while patrolling south of Toulon, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a vessel approaching through the Little Pass. Commenced attack. At 0915 A/29, the vessel was identified as a small naval drifter towing a lighter. At 0920 A/29, the attack was broken off as the lighter, a steel construction of about 200 tons, was seen to be empty. The drifter and lighter proceeded to the westward south of Cape Sicie on an estimated course of 284° at 7 knots. [We have been unable to identify this 'naval drifter towing a lighter'.] (27) 30 Oct 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) attacked a German U-boat to the southeast of Toulon, France, in position 43°04'N, 05°57'E. This attack was against U-73 but it inflicted no damage. U-73 moored in Toulon harbour at 0815 A/30 and was apparently unaware of the attack. HMS Ultimatum is often credited to have sunk U-431 in this attack. However U-431 was sunk with all hands on 21 October 1943 in the Mediterranean off Algiers in position 37°23'N, 00°35'E, by depth charges from a British Wellington aircraft (Sqdn. 179/Z). U-431's fate was revised in November 1987 by the Foreign Documents Section (FDS) of the British Ministry of Defence (Naval Historical Branch / NHB). [We could find nothing in German records of depth charges being dropped after this failed attack. Also there is no mention in the KTB (Kriegstagebuch) of U-73 which states she was attacked.] 0707 A/30 - Sighted a 500 tons U-boat bound for Toulon. Closed to attack. 0716 A/30 - Fired three torpedoes from 7000 yards. A fourth torpedo could not be fired as one of the torpedo tube was defective. 0721 A/30 - One torpedo explosion thought to be a hit on the target giving a range of 6600 yards. The U-boats HE ceased and could not be seen when the periscope was raised it is thought the submarine was sunk. 0723 A/30 - Retired from the scene at 80 feet. 0930 A/30 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight except a patrolling aircraft. 0950 A/30 - Went to 80 feet to reload the remaining torpedo. 1015 A/30 - Two sources of HE was reported to be approaching on a steady bearing. No A/S transmissions were heard. A/S vessels were probably hunting, frequently stopping and listening, and then coming in at speed. 1038 A/30 - One vessel passed fairly close to starboard so kept stern in at 180 feet until she had passed clear. 1050 A/30 - All HE had been lost so attempted to come up to periscope depth to have a look but the slightest noise resulted in renewed HE so remained deep instead. 1141 A/30 - A depth charge was dropped a fair distance off. From then until 1409 A/30 twelve patterns were dropped for a total of about thirty depth charges. All a long way off though. 1202 A/30 - Returned to periscope depth to find noting in sight. The reload had also been completed. 1600 A/30 - Left patrol and set course to comply with the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1302 A/28. (27) 2 Nov 1943 Around 0845 A/2, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 19th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers. At 0615 A/2, she made rendezvous with HMS Staffa (T/Lt. R.L. Roat, RNVR) which then escorted her to the harbour. (27) 3 Nov 1943 At 0745 A/3, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Algiers for passage to Malta. She made the passage in convoy KMS 30 together with HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN). [For more info on this convoy, see the event ' Convoy KMS 30 ' for 18 October 1943.] For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during this passage see the map below.
