(27) 6 Nov 1943 At 2240 A/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Malta to join the 10th submarine flotilla. At Malta she was taken in hand for repairs. (27) 21 Nov 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (66) 24 Nov 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was docked in No. 1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. She was docked together with HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN). (66) 4 Dec 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) are undocked. (66) 4 Jan 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) completed repairs at Malta. (67) 5 Jan 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted compass adjustment trials at Malta. (67) 6 Jan 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted independent exercises off Malta. (67) 8 Jan 1944 Around 1000 A/8, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Malta for Beirut where she was to join the 1st Submarine Flotilla. She was routes as follows; Through position 35°49'N, 15°06'E at 1358 A/8 thence proceeding independently on the surface to position 34°43'N, 20°02'E at 2001 A/9 thence diving by day to position 34°15'N, 25°01'E at 2010 A/11th for onward routeing to Beirut which will be signalled by the Capt. S.1. Speed of advance, 200 miles every 24 hours when on the surface and 120 miles every 24 hours when dived by day. In a signal timed 1106 B/8 the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders; From position 34°15'N, 25°01'E proceed on surface at 8 knots through position 34°05'N, 29°59'E at 0232 B/13 to arrive at Beirut at 1031 B/14. She was escorted out until 1313 A/8 by HMS Brave (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.S. Tolliday, RNR). (27) 8 Jan 1944 For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during the passage from Malta to Beirut see the map below. 14 Jan 1944 Around 1115 B/14, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Beirut from Malta. (27) 17 Jan 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted noise trials of Beirut. (67) 19 Jan 1944 Around 0715 B/19, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Beirut for Port Said where she was to be docked for a propeller change. At 1155 B/19, she received a signal ordering her to return to Beirut where she arrived around 1655 B/19. (67) 23 Jan 1944 Around 0715 B/23, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Beirut for Port Said where she was to be docked for a propeller change. (67) 24 Jan 1944 Around 1045 B/24, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Port Said where she was immediately docked in the floating dock. (67) 26 Jan 1944 At 1630 B/26, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was undocked. She then departed Port Said for her 20th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in south-east Aegean. She was clear of the swept channel around 2000 B/26. For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (27) 28 Jan 1944 In the evening HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) makes the northbound passage of the Kaso Strait on the surface. In a signal timed 1953 B/28, the Capt. S.1 ordered her to patrol between Milos and Santorini. Course was set accordingly around 2300 B/28 when clear off Kaso Stait. (68) 30 Jan 1944 At 2123 B/30, south of Milos, in approximate position 36°34'N, 24°37'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a dark shape ahead and up moon. At 2124 B/30, an aircraft was heard very close. HMS Ultimatum dived. At 2125 B/30, while diving the aircraft passed overhead. At 2133 B/30, HE was picked up and also heard A/S transmissions. Closed to attack. It appeared the enemy was moving northwards. At 2138 B/30, A/S transmissions were very loud but nothing could be seen through the periscope. Re-estimated the target was proceeding to the south-east at a speed (by counting the revolutions) of 10 knots. Altered course to 196° and closed at 4 knots. At 2146 B/30, a dark shape was seen bearing 146°. Lt. Kett altered course towards and continued to close.' At 2149 B/30, the gyro compass failed. At 2153 B/30, despite the bright moonlight the identity of the target could not be established. The target was turbine engined so may have been a destroyer or torpedo boat. HE was very loud and it appeared to be very close. At 2155 B/30, numerous red flares were dropped. At 2300 B/30, HMS Ultimatum surfaced. The attack had already been broken off as it was not possible to close the enemy submerged. From signals received from aircraft between 2324 B/30 and 0027 B/31 it was considered that this had been a convoy, of one or two merchant vessels and three escorts proceeding on course 090° at a speed of 12 knots. Most likely HMS Ultimatum had encountered the convoy made up of the German transport Oria (2127 GRT, built 1920, former French Sainte Julienne, former Norwegian Oria). She was escorted by the auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2105, UJ 2106 and UJ 2110. This convoy had departed Piraeus around 1830 A/29 bound for Rhodos via Milos and Stampalia. The convoy had arrived at Milos around 0730 A/30 and departed again around 1515 A/30. In the evening the convoy, at 2120 B/30 the convoy was detected by enemy aircraft so it seems that the aircraft which were noticed by HMS Ultimatum were allied and not German. Shortly afterwards the escort also noticed that an Allied submarine was in the area. UJ 2106 and the Oria proceeded close inshore to Folegandros to evade while the other two UJ boats remained further from the shore to draw the attention of the submarine on them. The Germans were under the impression that this tactic worked. At 0720 A/31, the convoy was attacked by a submarine. This was HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) which had fired three torpedoes but all missed. At 1300 A/31, the convoy anchored off Stampalia. At 0200 A/1, UJ 2105 and UJ 2110 got underway for an A/S hunt. One hour later UJ 2106 and Oria also got underway. Around 1600 A/1, UJ 2106 and Oria entered Rhodos harbour while the other two UJ boats remained outside for an A/S hunt. At 1725 A/1, UJ 2106 departed Rhodos and joined the other two UJ-boats after which they set course for Leros. They arrived at Portolago (Lakki) around 0715 A/2 for convoy duty. (69) 1 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1058 B/1, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN); Assume patrols in approaches to Suda Bay by 0401 B/3 as follows; (1) Ultimatum to the west of a line 27° from position 35°30'N, 24°14'E. (2) Sportsman to the east of this line. (70) 1 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1548 B/1, The Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and RHS Papanikolis (Lt. N. Roussen). (1) Ultimatum is to be south and west of position 36°15'N, 24°25'E by dawn/2 thence proceed to patrol as in my signal timed 1058 A/1. (2) Papanicolis is not to enter her patrol area until after dawn/2. (70) 1 Feb 1944 At 2300 B/1, south of Milos, sighted an enemy vessel bearing 220° at an estimated range of 10 miles. The vessel was to the left of the moon and appeared to be long and low. Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) turned on a parallel course and increased to full speed in an effort to get ahead and up moon of the enemy. At 0030 B/2, the enemy's bearing drew slowly aft. Course was adjusted to close on a 160° track. Speed of the enemy was estimated as being 12 knots. With the very bright moonlight conditions prevailing and a fairly light horizon behind Ultimatum Lt. Kett did not want to close too fast on a broad track until the moon conditions were more favourable. At 0100 B/1, the bearing of the enemy was now steady. Ultimatum was slowly closing the range. At 0130 B/1, the moon set behind a bank of low cumulus cloud leaving a very dark horizon. Visual touch was lost with the enemy. By 0200 B/1, there was still no visual contact so Lt. Kett stopped both motors and generators and set A/S listing watch. At 0205 B/1, faint HE was picked up bearing green 040° and the chase was resumed. By 0240 B/1, the enemy had still not been sighted so once again HMS Ultimatum stopped and A/S watch was set but no HE could be picked up. Also radar did not obtain contact. Ultimatum then proceeded south for 15 minutes but there was no sign of the enemy. At 0300 B/1, the loom of lights was observed from the direction of Suda Bay and it looked like that arrangements had been made for the enemy vessel to proceed straight into harbour. The chase was now broken off as there was no chance at all to catch the enemy in time due to the slow surface speed of the U-class submarines. Course was set for the Anti-Kithera channel, hoping to intercept enemy traffic there. The vessel sighted may have been the small German transport Gisela (211 GRT, built 1939) which was en-route from Piraeus via Milos to Suda Bay. She arrived at Iraklion early in the afternoon of the 2nd most likely she was diverted as the harbour entrance at Suda Bay was closed as it was suspected that the Allies had laid mines in the area. (71) 2 Feb 1944 At 1215 B/2, Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2359 B/1, which contained the following orders; Leave patrol forthwith passing through position 35°54'N, 24°48'E about 1801 B/2 thence Sophrana Island and through Scarpanto Strait to arrive in position 35°37'N, 27°37'E at 0201 B/4. Thence on the surface at 8 knots through position 34°03'N, 31°53'E to arrive at Beirut at 0701 B/6. HMS Ultimatum was recalled from patrol early as apparently some of her patrol orders and arrangements, like bombing restictions, had been compromised. (72) 4 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1954 B/4, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; U-Boat sighted in position 34°13'N, 33°23'E (south of Cyprus) at 0533 B/4. Aircraft and surface forces will be carrying out a search in vicinity during daylight tomorrow. Total bombing restrictions are in force for your route. This must have referred to U-453. The trawler HMS Islay (A/Skr.Lt. O. Jinks, RNR), whaler HMSAS Southern Maid and trawler FFS Reine des Flots were ordered to investigate the area but found nothing. The next day, the 5th, the destroyers HMS Quilliam (Capt. R.G. Onslow, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Queenborough (Cdr. E.P. Hinton, DSO and Bar, MVO, RN) and HMS Raider (Lt.Cdr. K.W. Michell, RN) departed Alexandria around noon for an A/S hunt as a U-boat had been suspected by direction finding at 0515 B/5 in approximate position 34°00'N, 31°00'E. They were recalled on the 6th and returned to harbour around 1540 B/6. (73) 6 Feb 1944 At 0710 B/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 20th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut. (27) 20 Feb 1944 At 1800 A/20, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Beirut for her 21st war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut. She was ordered to patrol in the northern Aegean. She was to pass Kaso Strait during the night of 22/23 February thence proceed to the north east of Sirina and Stampalia and west of Skyros to patrol an area bound by 39°40'N, the coast of Greece, the Skopelos Islands and 24°00'E. The object of the patrol is destruction of Piraeus / Salonika traffic. Allied MTB's and Levant schooner force caiques may also be operating in the patrol area. On completion of the patrol it was the intention to recall you to arrive at Malta on 8 March. For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (27) 23 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1816 A/23, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Unsparing remains north of 39°32'N from dusk/25 until 0001 A/26th and then leaves patrol passing east of Kaloveri Rocks and by the southbound as in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1459 B/2(January) [unable to find this signal and this route] to arrive in position 35°12'N, 26°43'E at 0102 A/29. Further routeing signal will follow. Ultimatum enters patrol area about 2001 A/25 remaining south of latitude 39°32'N until dawn/26. Note all times are zone A (-1). (74) 25 Feb 1944 At 2100 B/25, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) entered her patrol area. She proceeded towards the Skiathos Channel to intercept enemy vessels expected to leave Salonika for Piraeus and also an enemy vessel bound for Salonika. (27) 26 Feb 1944 At 1350 B/26, while patrolling north of Skiathos, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small caique inside the Skiathos Channel. At 1415 B/26, another small caique, of about 15 tons, was seen steering for the Skopelos Channel on course 100°. Lt. Kett did not want to compromise his position so early in the patrol so decided not to attack these small caiques during daylight. (27) 27 Feb 1944 At 0700 B/27, while patrolling north of Skiathos, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small caique leaving the Skopelos Channel steering 015°. At 0815 B/27, two small caiques, probably fishing vessels, entering the Skopelos Channel steering 170°. Several very small fishing boats were observed to enter the Skiathos and Skopelos Channels during the day. (27) 28 Feb 1944 At 1510 B/28, while patrolling north of Skiathos, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small caique heading for the Skopelos Channel. Several very small fishing boats were observed to enter the Skiathos and Skopelos Channels during the day. (27) 29 Feb 1944 At 0815 B/29, while patrolling north of Skiathos in position 39°16'N, 23°25'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small caique bearing 105° at a range of 7 miles. Course of the caique was 190°. (27) 1 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 1631 A/1, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Dolfijn pass through position 39°30'N, 24°01'E at dawn/2 and patrol in an area bounded by latitude 39°40'N, coast of Greece, Skopelos Islands and latitude 24°01'E to intercept Salonika - Piraeus traffic. Ultimatum leaving patrol A.M./2 passing through position 39°40'N, 24°01'E at 0801 A/2 thence north of strait by southbound route and east of Stampalia to arrive in position 35°12'N, 26°43'E at 2301 A/5. (75) 2 Mar 1944 At 0400 B/2, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) left patrol in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1631 A/1. (27) 3 Mar 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) stopped a small caique in position 39°34'N, 25°22'E but then released it after boarding. 0058 B/3 - Sighted a caique. Altered course to close and went to action stations. 0111 B/3 - Opened fire from 1500 yards. After a second round the vessel lowered her sail and exhibited a white light. The target was now seen to be a small sailing boat. Fire was ceased and Ultimatum manoeuvred alongside and a boarding party was put on board. The sailing vessel had a crew of four and the cargo consisted of four bags of potatoes, two bags of onions and four empty drums. The boat was allowed to proceed. (27) 5 Mar 1944 At 0353 B/5, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) fired ten rounds at the Kandeliusa light house for target practice. (27) 7 Mar 1944 At 0245 B/7, south-west of Crete, in position 34°23'N, 23°11'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted the Swedish Red Cross ship Fenris (1904 GRT, built 1941) proceeding on course 136° at 13 knots. (27) 10 Mar 1944 Around 1100 B/10, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 21st war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (27) 25 Mar 1944 At 1310 A/25, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Malta for her 22nd war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the south-west Aegean, south of 36°05'N and west of 25°00'E. She was to enter the Aegean through the Anti-Kithera Channel. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (76) 27 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 2347 A/27, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; The German transports Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) and Gisela (211 GRT, built 1939) are expected to sail from Piraeus to Crete shortly. (77) 28 Mar 1944 In the morning, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) passed eastbound throught the Anti-Kithera Channel, submerged. On completing the passage course was set to patrol in the approaches to Suda Bay. (27) 30 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 1958 A/30 the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; The German transports Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) moved to Mardon. Probably about to sail for Crete. (77) 31 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 1210 A/31, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN); (1) HMS Ultimatum remain east of longitude 24°00'E from dawn/2 to dawn/3. (2) HMS Ultor after passing through Anti-Kithera Channel proceed to patrol area through position 36°05'N, 23°19'E. (3) HMS Sportsman leave patrol P.M./2. Pass through positions 36°11'N, 23°43'E, 35°50'N, 23°46'E to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°30'E at 2130 A/2. Thence dived at 200 feet to position 35°41'N, 23°15'E. (77) 31 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 1750 A/31, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information to HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; Departure from Salamis of the German transports Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) and Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) probably for Crete appears imminent P.M./31. The German tanker Centaur (1267 GRT, built 1915) may possibly sail for Leros. (77) 1 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 0041 A/1, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) of the following; Departure of the German transports Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) for Crete and tanker Centaur (1267 GRT, built 1915) for Rhodes [note: probably Leros was intended] from Salamis imminent. Three caiques ready to leave Suda. (78) 3 Apr 1944 At 0404 A/3, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 35°40'N, 24°16'E and set course to patrol four miles north of Cape Drepano. At 1015 A/3, what is thought to be a motor torpedo boat was seen to round Cape Tripiti and proceed into Suda Bay. The enemy steamed about one mile off shore at an estimated speed of 12 knots. Most likely this was an R-boat (motor minesweeper) of which three were at Suda Bay on this day. [No KTB of the 12th R-boat Flotilla is available for this month so no further details are available.] At 1420 A/3, a schooner rigged caique of about 40 tons was seen to leave Suda Bay. At 1500 A/3, this caique was seen to round Cape Drepano and proceeding to the eastward. At 1600 A/3, it was seen that this caique had a green painted hull with a gun abaft the foremast. She appeared to be flying a Nazi ensign. Lt. Kett did not want to compromise his position by surfacing close to Suda Bay in daylight, he decided to keep the caique in sight until after sunset, then surface and engage her with the gun. The enemy's estimated course and speed were 110° at 3.5 knots. At 1800 A/3, it was realised the caique might be making for Rethymnon. At 1830 A/3, it was seen that the caique was exchanging signals with a shore station at Rethymnon and it was thought she subsequently entered harbour. At 1900 A/3, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°25'N, 24°26'E and despite the bright moonlight the caique was not seen. The caique sighted was most likely the Greek Elly (apparently German controlled) which proceeded from Suda Bay to Rethymnon on this day. (79) 5 Apr 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sank the Greek sailing vessel (in German service) PI 2659 / Agios Georgios with gunfire off Suda Bay, Crete, Greece. The German coastal guns of 10. Battery. M.A.A. 520 opened fire on the submarine which was hit by fragments on the casing. 0400 A/5 - Dived in position 35°38'N, 24°18'E. 0555 A/5- Sighted the mast of a caique bearing 040°. Range was 7 nautical miles. Altered course to intercept. 0642 A/5 - Surfaced in position 35°34'N 24°20'E and opened fire with the 3" gun from 2000 yards. Fired 15 rounds for 10 hits. The target was seen to break up and catch fire aft. The crew of six abandoned ship and pulled away in a small boat. 0645 A/5 - Dived. 0646 A/5 - Shore batteries in the vicinity of Cape Drepano opened fire. Shrapnel was heard to hit the casing. 0555 A/5 - Periscope observation showed that the caique had sunk. Survivors were seen pulling ashore towards Suda Bay at a distance of about 5 miles. Several enemy aircraft were seen patrolling in the area later in the morning. (80) 5 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 2151 A/5, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) off the following; Two F lighters lying close north-west of Monemvasia A.M. today. [This were F 370 and F 497 which were bound from Crete to Piraeus.] The transport Sabine (Former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) has been ready to sail from Salamis for several days. Destination Crete or Salonika. In fact Sabine departed Piraeus for Crete at 2000 A/5 together with the transport Suzanne (German, 552 GRT, former Spanish San Juan II, built 1919). They were escorted by TA 19 (former Italian torpedo boat Calatafini), TA 16 (former Italian torpedo boat Castelfidardo), TA 17 (former Italian torpedo boat former Italian San Martino) and the motor minesweeper R 34. The two transports arrived at Suda Bay at 1900 A/6. The escorts immediately returned to Piraeus. TA 17 had parted company with the convoy to hunt a suspected enemy submarine near Milos and was picked up on the way back to Piraeus by the other escorts. (81) 6 Apr 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sank the Greek sailing vessel (in German service) PI 2326 / Agios Nikolaos with gunfire off Anti-Kithera, Greece. 0408 A/6 - Dived in position 35°54'N, 23°26'E and commenced to patrol towards Patamo Bay (Anti-Kithera Island). 0630 A/6 - Sighted a caique bearing 290°. Range was 6 nautical miles. Closed for gun action. 0651 A/6 - Surfaced in position 35°53'N, 23°20'E and opened fire with the 3" gun from 1000 yards. Fired 11 rounds for 7 hits. the crew of 3 abandoned ship in their small boat. The caique soon sank. 0655 A/6 - Dived. (27) 7 Apr 1944 At 0430 A/7, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 35°36'N, 24°19'E. At 0830 A/7, a two-masted caique was seen coast crawling from the direction of Rethymnon towards Suda. At 0900 A/7, the caique was lost from sight against the land. Periscope visibility was limited due to the haze. At 1045 A/7, the caique was seen again 207° from Cape Drepano, 2 miles. As bigger ships were expected to arrive at Suda Bay soon Lt. Kett decided not to attack this caique. At 1215 A/7, the caique passed out of sight to the north of Cale Maleka. [We have been unable to identify this caique.] At 2135 A/7, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1802 B/7 was received which ordered HMS Ultimatum to remain east of 23.50'E until 1401 B/8 and then to proceed to patrol off St. Nikolo Bay. (27) 9 Apr 1944 At 0354 A/9, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 36°08'N, 23°10'E and patrolled off St. Nikolo Bay. At 0700 A/9, the mast of a caique was seen through the Dragonera Islands. HMS Ultimatum tried to intercept but by 0800 A/9, the caique was no longer seen. At 1900 A/9, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 36°09'N, 23°10'E and set course for the Anti-Kithera Channel to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1809 A/7. (27) 12 Apr 1944 At 1815 A/12, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 22nd war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Foxtrot (T/Lt. O.R. Turville, RNVR) which had joined around 1130 A/12. (27) 18 Apr 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted A/S exercises off Malta with HMS Teazer (Lt.Cdr. A.A.F. Talbot, DSO and Bar, RN) and RHS Navarinon (?). (82) 26 Apr 1944 At 1800 B/26, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Malta for her 23rd war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the South-West Aegean, off the south coast of Morea and west of Kithera and Antikithera. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (27) 28 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 1146 B/28, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Proceed forthwith with despatch to examine Kapsali Bay (Kithera) tonight. HMS Ultimatum examined the Bay very late in the evening but sighted nothing of interest. The following morning, just after daybreak a second examination of the Bay was made and a small caique at anchor was sighted and the wreck a sunken steamer. (83) 29 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 0930 B/29, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); (1) You have freedom of action in your area. (2) Appreciate possible Danube-lighter lying in Kalamata. (78) 1 May 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sank the small Greek sailing vessel MYT 687 / Elevtheria with gunfire off Cape Matapan, Greece in position 36°21'N, 22°35'E. 0445 B/1 - Dived in position 36°36'N, 22°34'E and commenced to patrol at periscope depth. 0900 B/1 - In approximate positon 36°40'N, 22°34'E, sighted two small caiques sailing northwards. 1820 B/1 - Heard an underwater explosion. 2020 B/1 - Surfaced in position 36°25'N, 22°35'E and set course for Cape Matapan. 2141 B/1 - Sighted a dark object. Closed. 2201 B/1 - In position 36°21'N, 22°35'E opened fire on a caique. 2223 B/1 - Took three survivors on board. The caique sank. Set course towards Levadia Point. (27) 2 May 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) shells the harbour of Koroni, Greece. Two sailing vessels were claimed sunk, five were claimed destroyed on the slips and one was claimed to be damaged. Three shells fell in the village, killing one Greek was killed and wounding two more. 0443 B/2 - Dived in position 36°40'N, 22°07'E and patrolled at periscope depth off Koroni. 1000 B/2 - Several small caiques of 10 to 15 tons left Koroni and sailed northwards. A close examination of Koroni harbour revealed three ship building yards with nine large caiques either building or in repair on the slipways. Numerous caiques of various sizes were seen afloat. 1540 B/2 - Adjusted course to investigate Koroni harbour again with a view to bombardment. 1919 B/2 - Surfaced 1400 yards from the breakwater to bombard Koroni harbour where several caiques had been sighted as well as three shipyards that were building caiques. 1937 B/2 - Completed the bombardment. 62 Rounds had been fired. Dived and retired seawards. The next morning the harbour was examined for the results of the bombardment. Two caiques were seen to have sunk, five were destroyed on the slipways and one was seen to be holed and damaged. (27) 4 May 1944 In a signal timed 1031 B/4, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unswerving (T/Lt. M.D. Tattersall, RNVR) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); (1) Unswerving has freedom of action in western Aegean north of lat. 36°26'N, and west of long. 24°30'E. (2) Ultimatum enter Aegean through Antikithera at dawn tomorrow for patrol - North coast Crete south of lat. 36°05'E. (3) Sabine (German, former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) in Piraeus ready to sail destination probably Crete. Bourgas (Bulgarian, 2812 GRT, built 1900) in Piraeus ready to sail destination unknown. Considerable caiques today reported between Port Nikolo, Mirabello and Heraklion.<./br> (4) Estimated HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) westbound in approximate position 35°41'N, 23°05'E at 2005 hours today. (84) 5 May 1944 In a signal timed 1701 B/5, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Unswerving (T/Lt. M.D. Tattersall, RNVR) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; Sabine (German, former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) probably still in Piraeus. May sail for Crete at 2200 hours tonight escorted by three destroyers. (84) 6 May 1944 At 0430 B/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived north of Suda Bay in position 35°49'N, 24°11'E and commenced to patrol at periscope depth towards Suda Bay. At 0506 B/6, a darkened shape was seen bearing 000°. At 0512 B/6, the target was made up to be two patrol caiques and one 'R' boat (motor minesweeper). Estimated range was 7000 yards. The vessels were transmitting on Asdic and apparently engaged in an A/S sweep. At 0517 B/6, four Arado 96 aircraft were seen patrolling low over the area. At 0520 B/6, an aircraft fired a pyrotechnic signal consisting of three red and three white stars. At 0530 B/6, a single bomb or depth charge was dropped fairly close. HMS Ultimatum went to 100 feet. At 0540 B/6, HMS Ultimatum commenced to return to periscope depth. On reaching 40 feet an A/S vessel, whose HE was now very loud, appeared to be in contact and coming in to attack so Lt. Kett returned to 100 feet, increased speed and altered away. At 0545 B/6, no attack had developed so once again commenced to return to periscope depth. Watched the A/S vessels and aircraft sweeping to the southward. At 0620 B/6, the A/S vessels were lost from sight in the haze off Cape Drepano. The Germans claimed that the bomb dropped at 0530 B/6 [0532 B/6 in German records] sank the submarine. The A/S vessels sighted were the auxiliary patrol vessels GK 01 and GK 91 which were escorting the motor sailing vessels SYR 438 / ???? (?) and PI 163 / ???? (?) from Milos to Suda Bay. Apparently GK 91 had sighted the submarine. After two depth charges had been dropped by an aircraft part of the submarine was seen breaking the surface. This was followed by air bubbles for several minutes. After that no contact could be gained on the submarine and it was thought it had been sunk. At 1200 B/6, a large caique of about 100 tons and one coaster, also of about 100 tons, were seen coast crawling from eastwards towards Suda Bay. In view of the possible arrival of Sabine (German, former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) these were not attacked. [We have been unable to identify these vessels or this must have been the two motor sailing vessels listed above but this seems unlikely as, according to German documents, they entered Suda Bay around 0900 B/6 together with GK 01 while GK 91 remained out hunting for the submarine.] At 2035 B/6, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°39'N, 24°15'E. At 2120 B/6, while patrolling on a course of 130° three small vessels appeared out of the fog on bearing 160° (Green 30°) at a range of about 6000 yards. HMS Ultimatum turned towards and closed. At 2127 B/6, the bearing moved to starboard. The estimated course of the enemy was 260° at a speed of 7 knots. They were transmitting on Asdic and steaming in single line ahead. At 2134 B/6, HMS Ultimatum dived. The vessels were made out to be small patrol craft, probably patrol caiques with sails furled. HE was classified as that of petrol engines. At 2200 B/6, the HE faded out on bearing 260°. HMS Ultimatum surfaced. There was nothing in sight. The vessels sighted were most likely the auxiliary patrol vessels GK 01, GK 60 and GK 92 which had departed Suda Bay at 1950 B/6 for Potamo, Anti-Kithera. They were joined later by the landing barge F 123 which departed Chania around 2100 B/6. They arrived at Potamo around 0600 B/7 but not before F 123 had been attacked and damaged by Allied aircraft shortly after midnight on the 7th. One of the crew / troops was killed, nine heavily injured and one lightly injured. (85) 6 May 1944 In a signal timed 2020 B/6, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Unswerving (T/Lt. M.D. Tattersall, RNVR) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; Three destroyers, two escort vessels and Bourgas (Bulgarian, 2812 GRT, built 1900) departed Piraeus 1530 B/6, destination unknown. In a follow up signal timed 2235 B/6 they were also informed of the following; (1) My 2020 B/6, destination Saloniki. (2) Sabine (German, former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) with three destroyers and two escort vessels probably sail today for Crete. The transport Bourgas, small tanker Elli (former Greek, 314 GRT, built 1878) towing the Siebel ferry, SF 265 had departed Piraeus for Saloniki at 2100 B/5. They were escorted by UJ 2105 and UJ 2110. They arrived at Saloniki at 1700 B/8 being delayed by fog while en-route. (86) 9 May 1944 At 0323 B/9, while patrolling off Suda Bay, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted two darkened ships steering south. HMS Ultimatum altered course towards. At 0350 B/9, it was seen that it were enemy patrol craft which were patrolling to the north of Cape Drepano. They were observed to steam northward as far as Cape Tripiti then turn and proceeded south. No Asdic transmissions were picked up. Also no HE was heard. [We have been unable to positively identify these vessels, it seems likely that it were vessels from the Seeverteidigung Kreta, perhaps it were GK 60 and GK 92 proceeding from Chania to Suda Bay. [They had arrived at Chania around 0445 B/8 and the next mention of them is on 10 May when they left Suda Bay in the morning to assist GK 91 hunting HMS Ultimatum. The harbour entrance at Suda Bay had been closed until 2230 B/8 due enemy mining so GK 60 and GK 92 most likely must have departed Chania after this time and thus may well have been the vessels encountered at 0323 B/9.] At 0358 B/9, the navigation lights of as steamer were sighted on bearing 115°. HMS Ultimatum closed on a 120° track. At 0405 B/9, HMS Ultimatum dived in position 35°40'N, 24°16'E and continued to close the illuminated vessel. At 0500 B/9, the vessel was identified as a hospital ship illuminated in accordance with the International regulations. At 0530 B/9, it was seen that the hospital ship remained stopped off the entrance to Suda Bay. Half an hour later the ship entered the Bay. [For the identity of this hospital ship see below.] At 0842 B/9, two unidentified vessels were seen to leave Suda Bay. HMS Ultimatum closed to attack. At 0910 B/9, it was seen that the two vessels were small patrol craft patrolling between Cape Drepano and Paleo Suda Island. At 0940 B/9, the enemy patrol vessels returned to Suda Bay. At 1025 B/9, the enemy patrol vessels were seen once more. Perhaps the patrol vessels sighted this morning were GK 60 and GK 92 yet again. At 1726 B/9, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1345 B/9 was received which stated that the German hospital ship Tübingen (Former French Gouverneur General Tirman, 3509 GRT, built 1930) was at Suda Bay. This was indeed the hospital ship seen entering Suda Bay that morning. She came from Iraklion. At 2035 B/9, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°35'N, 24°17'E. (27) 10 May 1944 In the early morning hours, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was detected by two German A/S vessels to the north of Suda Bay. She was depth charged by them sustaining some damage. After several hours she managed to escape. The attacks were carried out by the German auxiliary patrol vessel GK 91 which was escorting a motorised sailing vessel Ismini (????). Between 0435 B/10 and 0640 B/10 she dropped a total of 20 depth charges. After the 6th pattern had been dropped a slight oil trace was sighted and an can of food with English language printed on it, as well as bread and pieces of wood were picked up. GK 60 and GK 92 were sent from Suda Bay to assist in hunting the enemy submarine. GK 91 and Ismini arrived at Suda Bay around 0700 B/10. 0355 B/10 - Sighted two ships. 0402 B/10 - Dived to carry out an attack. 0408 B/10 - The two ships were thought to be UJ boats (auxiliary submarine chasers) zigzagging on a main course of 130 degrees. Approached at slow speed keeping end on. The vessels were sweeping on Asdic. 0418 B/10 - Both vessels altered towards and appeared to gain contact. Lights were seen flashing aft on both vessels. Went deep and shut off for depth charging. 0421 B/10 - A pattern of five depth charges was dropped close as Ultimatum reached 100 feet causing some damage. Went to 200 feet. 0512 B/10 - After successfully avoiding several attempted attacks a second pattern was dropped causing some more damage. 0530 B/10 - Several bursts of high speed became necessary to assist in depth keeping. Ultimatum was becoming heavy despite constant pumping. Despite large alterations of course both vessels remained in contact but they did not drop depth charges unless they were very certain of their attack. 0640 B/10 - A pattern of five depth charges fell close causing a fire in the motor room besides causing more damage. Increased speed and checked the descent at 400 feet. Returned to 270 feet and restored trim. 0720 B/10 - A pattern of four depth charges was dropped. Some minor electrical damage was sustained. Both vessels were now, for the first time on the port side and by constant altering of course kept them there. 0740 B/10 - The vessels appeared to have lost contact. A third vessel was now also heard transmitting on Asdic. They all failed to regain contact and Ultimatum withdrew to the north-east. 0840 B/10 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. 0915 B/10 - With all repairable defects made good, course was set to patrol off Suda Bay. 1815 B/10 - the hospital ship Tübingen (Former French Gouverneur General Tirman, 3509 GRT, built 1930) was seen leaving Suda Bay and proceeding on a course of 050°. 2048 B/10 - HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°40'N, 24°16'E and patrolled to the northward while charging the battery. 2200 B/10 - Left patrol and set course for the Anti-Kithera Channel in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1047 B/8 which contained the following orders; Leave patrol as necessary to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°29'E at 0505 B/11 thence through the Anti-Kithera Channel. Further onward routing had been signalled by the Capt. S.1 in his signal timed 1515 B/9 which contained the following orders; (1) From position 35°41'N, 23°15'E about 1201 B/11 proceed to rendezvous with escort in position 35°49'N, 15°18'E at 1530 B/13. (2) Dive by day on the 11th thence 8.5 knots. (87) 11 May 1944 At 0455 B/11, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 35°48'N, 23°30'E and proceeded on course 240°. At 0600 B/11, HMS Ultimatum dived to 200 feet to pass under the Anti-Kithera minefield. At 1300 B/11, HMS Ultimatum returned to periscope depth and set course to 270°. At 1955 B/11, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°43'N, 22°34'E. At 2100 B/11, HMS Ultimatum passed her signal timed 2000 B/11 in which she reported being detected off Suda Bay the day before. (88) 13 May 1944 Around 2100 B/13, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 23rd war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was swept in. The escort (identity not known) had joined around 1515 B/13. (27) 19 May 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was docked at the Malta Dockyard in No.1 graving dock. (89) 29 May 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was undocked. [No logbooks are available for the months of June and July 1944. Some details for these months may thus be missing.] (89) 8 Jun 1944 Convoy MKS 52. This convoy departed Port Said on 8 June 1944. On departure from Port Said the convoy was made up of the transports / tankers; Baron Belhaven (British, 6591 GRT, built 1925), Cerion (British, 2588 GRT, built 1938), Clydefield (British (tanker), 7365 GRT, built 1928), Empire Almond (British, 6860 GRT, built 1941), Empire Wordsworth (British (tanker), 9891 GRT, built 1942), Eurybates (British, 6276 GRT, built 1928), Fort Walsh (British, 7126 GRT, built 1943), Mactra (British (tanker), 6193 GRT, built 1936), Murena (Dutch (tanker), 8252 GRT, built 1931), Pierre L'Enfant (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Samariz (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and Samblade (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943). On departure from Port Said the convoy was escorted by sloop HMS Fleetwood (Cdr. W.B. Piggott, DSC and Bar, OBE, RD, RNR) and the corvette HMS Anemone (Lt. J.B. Sparkes, RNR). On 9 June 1944, the following transports / tankers departed Alexandria to join the convoy; Crista (British, 2590 GRT, built 1938), Empire Cobbett (British (tanker), 9811 GRT, built 1942), Empire Nugget (British (tanker), 9807 GRT, built 1942), Empire Spartan (British, 7009 GRT, built 1942), Eridan (French, 9928 GRT, built 1928), Fort Clatsop (British, 7157 GRT, built 1943), Hermelin (Norwegian, 1683 GRT, built 1940) and Noesaniwi (Dutch, 6737 GRT, built 1936). The minesweepers RHS Karteria, RHS Salaminia, RHS Afroussa and RHS Leros also departed Alexandria and joined the convoy for passage westwards. They were escorted by the minesweeper HMS Hazard (Lt.Cdr. L.C. Smith, RN) and the corvettes HMS Bergamot (A/Lt.Cdr. W. McInnes, RNR) and HMS Convolvulus (A/Lt.Cdr. R.F.R. Yarde-Buller, RNVR). On 13 June 1944, the following ships departed Augusta to join the convoy; A.P. Hill (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Andrew Briscoe (American, 7244 GRT, built 1944), Chiswick (British, 6006 GRT, built 1943), Djebel Aures (French, 2835 GRT, built 1929), Empire Gale (British, 7089 GRT, built 1941), Facto (Norwegian, 1522 GRT, 1921), Fort de Douaumont (French, 5266 GRT, built 1918), Fort Gaspereau (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943), Fort Maisonneuve (British, 7128 GRT, built 1942), Fort Pembina (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), George Davis (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Gylfe (Norwegian (tanker), 6129 GRT, built 1930), Hoegh Hood (Norwegian (tanker), 9351 GRT, built 1936), Iceland (British, 1236 GRT, built 1914), James Moore (American, 7177 GRT, built 1943), John Trumbull (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Kepong (British, 1874 GRT, built 1916), King Stephen (British, 5274 GRT, built 1928), Kiungchow (British, 2653 GRT, built 1921), Laurent Meeus (Belgian (tanker), 6429 GRT, built 1930), Lincoln Steffens (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Lochee (British, 964 GRT, built 1937), Ocean Gipsy (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Ocean Rider (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942)), Robert T. Hill (American, 7244 GRT, built 1943), Samhope (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Svanholm (Norwegian, 696 GRT, built 1917), Tabitha Brown (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Tore Jarl (Norwegian, 1514 GRT, built 1920) and Woodbridge N. Ferris (American, 7200 GRT, built 1943). On 14 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Augusta after having parted company with the convoy; Baron Belhaven, Cerion, Crista, Empire Corbett, Empire Nugget, Empire Spartan, Empire Wordsworth, Fort Clatsop, Hermelin, Macrtra, Pierre L'Enfant and Samblade. On 14 June 1944, the following transports arrived at Malta after having been detached from the convoy; Empire Almond and Eridan while the tug Empire Titania (British, 258 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy from Malta as did the submarine HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR). On 14 June 1944, the Djebel Aures arrived at Tunis after having been detached from the convoy. On 15 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Bizerta after having been detached from the convoy; Facto, Murena, Tore Jarl and the four Greek (YMS-class) minesweepers while the following ships joined the convoy coming from Bizerta; Badjestan (British, 5573 GRT, built 1928) and Empire Summer (British, 6949 GRT, built 1941). The rescue tug HMRT Charon also joined the convoy as did the AA cruiser HMS Colombo (Capt. C.T. Jellicoe, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN). On 16 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Bone after having been detached from the convoy; Empire Titania, Fort Gaspereau, Fort Maissoneuve, Kopong, Kiungchow and Ocean Gypsy while the following ships joined the convoy coming from Bone; Empire Ballad (British, 6700 GRT, built 1942) and Hardingham (British, 7269 GRT, built 1942). HMS Ultimatum was also detached and proceeded to Bone. The reason for her proceeding to Bone is not known to us. Normally she would have proceeded direct to La Maddalena. On 16 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Philippeville after having been detached from the convoy as they were straggling; Fort de Douaumont and Svanholm. On 17 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Algiers after having been detached from the convoy; Badjestan, Chiswick, Empire Summer, Fort Pembina, Gylfe, Hardingham, Hoegh Hood, Iceland, Laurent Meeus, Ocean Rider, Samhope and HMS Colombo while the following ships joined the convoy coming from Algiers; Alex (British, 3932 GRT, built 1914), Belgian Trader (Belgian, 2890 GRT, built 1942), Cape Howe (British, 6999 GRT, built 1943), Clausina (British (tanker), 8083 GRT, built 1938), Empire Gareth (British, 2847 GRT, built 1942), Fort Anne (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943), Gouverneur General Lepine (French, 3509 GRT, built 1923) and Ville de Majunga (British (former French), 6174 GRT, built 1931). On 18 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Oran after having been detached from the convoy; A.P. Hill , Andrew Briscoe, Cape Howe, Clydefield, Fort Anne, George Davis, James Moore, John Trumbull, Lincoln Steffens, Robert T. Hill and Tabitha Brown while the transport Sinnington Court (British, 6910 GRT, built 1928) joined the convoy coming from Oran. The convoy arrived at Gibraltar on 20 June 1944. 15 Jun 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Malta to join convoy MKS 52. [For more info on this convoy see the event ' Convoy MKS 52 ' for 8 June 1944.] [As no log of this period is available so her exact movements are uncertain. She sailed to join convoy MKS 52 on passage from Port Said to Gibraltar. She had received orders to leave the convoy near Bone on the 16th and proceed to La Maddalena but it appears that she went to Bone instead, perhaps to remedy to some defects.] (90) 16 Jun 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Bone. (90) 18 Jun 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Bone for La Maddalena where she was to join the 10th submarine flotilla. (90) 20 Jun 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at La Maddalena. (90) 23 Jun 1944 At 1700 B/23, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed La Maddalena for her 24th war patrol (20th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Southern France. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (27) 25 Jun 1944 At 0145 B/25, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) entered area 'ST' which was her patrol area. (27) 26 Jun 1944 At 0435 B/26, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 43°05'N, 06°38'E and commenced to patrol at periscope depth 2 miles to the south of Cape Lardier. At 2030 B/26, a destroyer, UJ-boat and a naval tug (A/S equipped) were seen proceeding eastwards from Bolnes roads. The destroyer and UJ boat were most likely the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 6073 and UJ 6079. At 2105 B/26, an F-lighter was seen close inshore, proceeding on course 070° at a speed of 8 knots. Lt. Kett came to a 100° track closing intending to fire two torpedoes. At 2115 B/26, the attack was broken off as it was not possible to reduce the range below 4000 yards. The chances of hitting and F-lighter did not justify firing torpedoes or compromising the submarines position so early in the patrol. At 2130 B/26, the destroyer and UJ-boat, on reaching Cape Lardier, turned round and proceeded towards Hyeres roads. Both vessels were zigzagging continuously. The F-lighter and A/S tug proceeded eastwards in company. The tug was most likely the L'Obstiné (323 GRT, built 1921) and the brand new landing barge MFP 935. They had sailed from Toulon for St. Tropez at 1715 B/26. They were to sail from St. Tropez at 2300 B/26 for Nice with the German transport Pallas (5260 GRT, built 1925) in tow escorted by the German auxiliary minesweepers M 6020, M 6021 and M 6027. They arrived at Nice at 0930 B/27. At 2200 B/26, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 43°05'N, 06°40'E and pratrolled five miles to the south-east of Cape Camarat. At 2310 B/26, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1255 B/26 was received which granted HMS Ultimatum freedom of action in area's 'ST', 'SU' and 'SW'. (91) 28 Jun 1944 At 0432 B/28, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived south of Toulon Bay in position 42°59'N, 06°01'E and set course to close the Little Pass. At 0900 B/28, two motor torpedo boats (or motor minesweepers) were seen patrolling inside Toulon Bay. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] At 1945 B/28, a destroyer and an UJ boat were observed to leave Toulon and commence an A/S sweep. Both vessels were transmitting on Asdic and carried out a continuous zig-zag. These were most likely the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 6073 and UJ 6079 which left Toulon at 1915 B/28 to hunt a reported submarine in the Nice-Cannes area. At 2000 B/28, two former French Chasseurs joined the A/S sweep. These too were transmitting on Asdic. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] At 2130 B/28, the destroyer and UJ boat proceeded to sweep westward eventually disappearing in the direction 6 miles south of Cape Sicie. The Chasseurs continued to patrol near Little Pass. At 2200 B/28, it was getting too dark to see the enemy against the land so course was set to seaward. At 2336 B/28, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 42°54'N, 06°02'E and commenced to patrol in the path of the moon while charging the battery. (91) 29 Jun 1944 At 0429 B/29, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived south of Toulon Bay in position 43°00'N, 06°01'E and set course to patrol off the Giens Peninsula / Little Pass area. At 1230 B/29, an aircraft report was received which reported a 4000 tons vessel and a 2000 ton vessel in position 43°22'N, 06°59'E proceeding on course 240° at a speed of 9 knots. It was considered likely these ships could be expected to pass through Little Pass shortly after 1530 B/29. Most likely this were the ships reported by the aircraft. At 1605 B/29, a destroyer and UJ boat were seen leaving the Little Pass and proceeding towards Toulon. Both vessels were transmitting on Asdic and carrying out a continuous zig-zag. At 1800 B/29, the destroyer and UJ boat were seen to enter Toulon. This were once again the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 6073 and UJ 6079 returning from A/S operations. At 2110 B/29, four Chasseurs or motor torpedo boats / motor minesweepers were observed to be patrolling to the south of Cape Cepet. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] At 2000 B/29, HMS Ultimatum retired to seaward, surfacing at 2314 B/29 in position 42°57'N, 06°00'E. The course was then set to patrol off Cape Camarat. (91) 3 Jul 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) fired two torpedoes at the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 6073 south of Cannes, southern France. The torpedoes however missed their target. UJ 6073 was on an A/S patrol together with UJ 6079. 0059 B/3 - Sighted a white light flashing morse bearing 340°. 0104 B/3 - Two darkened ships were made out steaming on a course of 200° distance 2000 yards. As the vessels were at close range and were signalling, Lt. Kett thought he might be sighted. He turned his submarine stern on to watch events unfold. 0109 B/3 - The vessels were identified as a destroyer and a UJ boat, and as they showed no sign of taking action against Ultimatum Lt. Kett altered course to attack. 0114 B/3 - In position 43°24'N, 06°57'E fired four torpedoes. 40 seconds later one torpedo was seen to hit causing a column of water rise 50 feet into the air completely enveloping the target (this actually was not a hit, the torpedo exploded prematurely). Six seconds later a second torpedo was heard to explode but this was not observed as the submarine was now diving towards 150 feet. The target is thought to be destroyed. 1115 B/3 - Dived an took evasive action at 150 feet. 0120 B/3 - Faint A/S transmissions picked up astern while at 150 feet. 0123 B/3 - Heard two loud explosion at six second intervals which were considered to be torpedoes exploding against the land. 0200 B/3 - No more Asdic transmissions or HE were heard. Considered returning to periscope depth. 0223 B/3 - A pattern of eight depth charges was dropped but not very close. HMS Ultimatum returned to 150 feet and continued withdrawal from the area. 0320 B/3 - Came to periscope depth. 0340 B/3 - Surfaced and resumed the battery charge. 0415 B/3 - Dived in position 43°17'N, 06°59'E and commenced reloading the torpedo tubes and repair the Asdic training gear which had become defective. Reload and repairs were completed one hour later. 2308 B/3 - Surfaced in position 43°18'N, 06°53'E. 2350 B/3, received the Capt S.10's signal timed 2003 B/3 ordering HMS Ultimatum to leave patrol P.M./4. (27) 4 Jul 1944 At 2100 B/4, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) left patrol to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2003 B/3. (27) 6 Jul 1944 At 0920 B/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 24th war patrol (20th in the Mediterranean) at La Maddalena. (27) 17 Jul 1944 Around 1730 B/17, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed La Maddalena for her 25th war patrol (21st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Lions, in area 'SX'. She was routes as follows; through position 42°15'N, 07°21'E at 2000 B/18 and 42°15'N, 03°53'E at 1201 B/20 to patrol in area 'SX'. For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below. (27) 21 Jul 1944 At 0730 B/21, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) carried out a dummy attack on the Swiss Red Cross ship Albula (1197 GRT, built 1910) which was en-route from Gibraltar to Marseille. At 1659 B/21, HMS Ultimatum received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1659 B/21 giving her freedom of action in areas 'SX' and 'SW'. (27) 22 Jul 1944 In a signal timed 0847 B/22, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Endeavour to intercept Spanish fishing vessel Dot Hermanos believed sailing from Barcelona today to Port Vendres with cargo of Wolfram. Utmost importance to prove this clandestine export. Capture of master should be attempted. In a followup signal timed 1835 B/22, the following orders were signalled to HMS Ultimatum; (1) My 0847 B/22. Intercept outside territorial waters in Gulf of Rosas halfway between La Escala and Punta Palconera between 1200 B/23 and 1400 B/23. Master of Dos Hermanos is party to this arrangement and will co-operate and capture is not essential. (2) Agents expected to be on board and any suspicious passenger should be captured, any manifest or other evidence of nature of cargo should be removed. Essential to produce unassailable evidence of cargo. Vessel is not to be sunk. Report result by W/T. (92) 23 Jul 1944 In a signal timed 0915 B/23, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); (1) My 1835 B/22. Vessel will not sail from Barcelona until afternoon 24th or 25th. Further signal will be made. (2) Master should not be removed. (3) Bombing restrictions have been arranged. (92) 23 Jul 1944 At 1700 B/23, near Cape Creus, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) carried out a dummy attack on the Swedisch Safe Conduct Ship Finn (1664 GRT, built 1920) bound from Marseille to Gibraltar. (27) 24 Jul 1944 At 1600 B/24, while patrolling near the Gulf of Rosas, Spain, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a fishing vessel proceeding on course 030°. HMS Ultimatum closed to investigate. At 1730 B/24, the fishing vessels was identified as the Antonio of Rosas. A small local vessel. Due to the bad visibility it was approach very close before the name of the vessel could be read. Approach was made from ahead and after her identity was established it was seen that she was towing nets. Course was immediately altered away but it appeared HMS Ultimatum fouled her nets. HMS Ultimatum went to 50 feet and retired at 4 knots. At 2030 B/24, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1623 B/24 was received which ordered HMS Ultimatum to patrol in area 'SW'. Course was altered to comply with the orders. At 2207 B/24, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 42°11'N, 03°20'E and course was set for the Cassidaigne Reef. (27) 25 Jul 1944 At 0600 B/25, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) entered her new patrol area. (27) 27 Jul 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) fired four torpedoes at a group of small German vessels. The German landing barge F 811 was hit and sunk south of La Ciotat, France in position 43°03'N, 05°34'E. [We have been unable to identify the other vessels in this convoy (which was numbered 5495). Unfortunately there is no KTB (Kriegstagebuch, logbook) of the German 6th Sicherungsflottille (6th escort flotilla) available after 15 July 1944. This may have been lost during the Allied landings in Southern France in August.] 0050 B/27 - Obtained a radar echo bearing 045°, range 6000 yards. Altered course towards. 0100 B/27 - The bearing was steady, range decreasing, went to action stations. 0117 B/27 - Turbine HE was picked up bearing 050°. Made out dark shapes against the land. 0130 B/27 - The contact was seen to be one escort vessel and four 'F-boats' on an estimated course of 280°, speed 6 knots. 0148 B/27 - Fired three torpedoes at an almost continuous target of 2 'F-boats'. Radar gave a range of 1500 yards. 0150 B/27 - One of the 'F-boats' was seen to explode with a bright yellow flash and a column of sparks and smoke. It was thought one of the other torpedoes hit the other 'F-boat' but this was not the case. 0151 B/27 - The escort was seen to turn towards. 0154 B/27 - An aircraft was seen to fly down the torpedo tracks. Dived and continued withdrawal submerged. 0155 B/27 - Heard breaking up noises. 0247 B/27 - All quiet. Surfaced. Enemy A/S patrols, mostly aircraft, patrolled the area for the remained of the day. (27) 28 Jul 1944 At 0300 B/28, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) left patrol to comply with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1109 B/26. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but it must have contained the orders to leave patrol and when to arrive at La Maddalena and the route which was to be followed.] (27) 28 Jul 1944 Convoy MKS 57. This convoy departed Port Said on 28 July 1944, around 1430 B/28. On departure from Port Said the convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; Algorab (Dutch, 4938 GRT, built 1921), Clan MacNeil (British, 6111 GRT, built 1922), Edward Bruce (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Empire Lord (British, 7359 GRT, built 1944), Fort Prudhomme (British, 7167 GRT, built 1943), Gulf of Mexico (American (tanker), 7807 GRT, built 1917), Katy (Norwegian (tanker), 6825 GRT, built 1931), Marsdale (British, 4890 GRT, built 1940), Megna (British, 6595 GRT, built 1944), Nurani (British, 5414 GRT, built 1941), Phrontis (Dutch, 6616 GRT, built 1926), Rookley (British, 4998 GRT, built 1940), Samdak (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samingoy (British, 7255 GRT, built 1944), Samtruth (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Sarpedon (British, 11321 GRT, built 1923), Suderholm (Norwegian (tanker), 4908 GRT, built 1917) and Thorshavn (Norwegian (tanker), 6869 GRT, built 1930). The French sloop D'Entrecasteaux (?) also took passage in this convoy. She was not part of the escort though as this ship had been sunk at Diego Suarez in 1942 and had been salvaged in 1943 and made fit to proceed under her own power. She was to proceed to Bizerte where she was placed in reserve. She was never fully repaired. On departure from Port Said the convoy was escorted by the sloop HMS Pheasant (Cdr. W.E.J. Eames, DSC, RN), the minesweeping sloops Commandant Bory (?), La Moqueuse (?) and the corvette HMS Saxifage (Lt. D.K.H. Kinloch, RNR). On 29 July 1944, around 1600 B/29, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Alexandria; Bantria (British, 2407 GRT, built 1928), Beaconsfield (British, 4635 GRT, built 1938), City of Dundee (British, 5273 GRT, built 1921), Fort Astoria (British, 7189 GRT, built 1943), Manchester Division (British, 6048 GRT, built 1918), Samfeugh (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and Woodlark (British, 1501 GRT, built 1928). They were escorted by the sloop HMS Bideford (T/A/Lt.Cdr. H.J. Beverley, DSO, DSC, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Alisma (Lt. G. Lanning, RANVR) and HMS Spiraea (Lt. A.H. Pierce, OBE, RNR) which also joined the convoy. At 0438 B/3, the following ships parted company with the convoy to proceed to Augusta; Algorab, Bantria, Beaconsfield, Empire Lord, Fort Astoria, Gulf of Mexico, Phrontis, Samdak, Samfeugh, Samingoy, Samtruth and Thorshavn. They were escorted to Augusta by Commandant Bory and La Moqueuse which did not rejoin the convoy. At 0553 B/3, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Augusta; Arizonan (American, 5816 GRT, built 1920), Baron Maclay (British, 6317 GRT, built 1924), Cartago (American, 4732 GRT, built 1908), Empire Airman (British (tanker), 9813 GRT, built 1942), Empire Gain (British (tanker), 3738 GRT, built 1943), Empire Spey (British, 4292 GRT, built 1929), John Sergeant (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942), Meline (Norwegian (tanker), 6983 GRT, built 1918), Samavon (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Sheaf Crown (British, 4868 GRT, built 1929) and Toltec (Honduran, 5527 GRT, built 1929). At 1110 B/3, the Woodlark parted company with the convoy to proceed to Malta. At 1600 B/4, the Empire Gain, Empire Spey and Suderholm were detached to Bizerta as was the D'Entrecasteaux. The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Bizerta one hour later; Chloris (British, 1171 GRT, built 1910) and Dallington Court (British, 6889 GRT, built 1929). At 0940 B/5, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Bone; Antonia (Dutch (tanker), 3357 GRT, built 1938), Clydebank (British, 5156 GRT, built 1925) and Empire Planet (British, 4290 GRT, built 1923). At 1138 B/6, the following merchant vessels were detached to Algiers; Antonia, Arizonan, Baron Maclay, Chloris, Clydebank, Dallington Court, Empire Airman and Samavon while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Algiers at 1300 B/6; Astrid (Norwegian, 2861 GRT, built 1942), Henry Baldwin (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), James Hoban (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Orpheus (American, 8258 GRT, built 1944), Pierre Soule (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Ponce de Leon (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Smith Thompson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and William H. Wilmer (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943). Also the submarine HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) joined the convoy coming from Algiers. At 1350 B/7, the following merchant vessels were detached to Oran; Cartago, Henry Baldwin, James Hoban, John Sergeant, Katy, Orpheus, Ponce de Leon, Smith Thompson, Toltec and William H. Wilmer while the merchant vessel Atlantic (British, 5414 GRT, built 1939) joined the convoy coming from Oran at 1445 B/7. At 1839 B/8, the following ships were detached to Gibraltar; Astrid, Atlantic, Empire Planet, Megna, Meline and Rookley. Also detached to Gibraltar was HMS Ultimatum. At 1916 B/8, the Mediterranean escort, HMS Pheasant, HMS Bideford, HMS Alisma, HMS Saxifrage and HMS Spiraea was detached to Gibraltar. This was after a new escort had joined up with the remaining ships of the convoy which were now proceeding into the Atlantic together with joiners from Gibraltar. The convoy was now known as convoy 'MKS 57(G)'. This convoy was to make rendezvous with and join convoy SL 166 coming from Freetown. These two convoy were then to proceed in company, as the combined convoy SL 166 / MKS 57(G), to the U.K. (93) 30 Jul 1944 Around 0930 B/30, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 24th war patrol (21st in the Mediterranean) at La Maddalena. (27) 3 Aug 1944 Around 0630 B/3, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed La Maddalena for Algiers. She was to return to the U.K. to refit. She was routed as follows; through position 41°07'N, 07°34'E at 1830 B/3 thence direct to position 36°56'N, 03°03'E at 1100 B/5 to rendezvous with the escort to Algiers. For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during the passage to the U.K. see the map below. (94) 5 Aug 1944 At 1310 B/5, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Algiers. She was escorted in by HMS Hoy (T/Lt. G.H. McNair, MBE, RNVR) which had joined at 1115 B/5 in position 36°56'N, 03°03'E. (94) 6 Aug 1944 At 1030 B/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Algiers for Gibraltar. At sea, at 1220 N/6, and in position 36°53'N, 03°05'E, she joined convoy MKS 57. [For more info on this convoy see the event ' Convoy MKS 57 ' for 28 July 1944.] (94) 8 Aug 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Gibraltar. (94) 9 Aug 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted D/G trials at Gibraltar. (94) 9 Aug 1944 Convoy SL 167 / Combined convoy SL 167 / KMS 58(G). This convoy departed Freetown around 0800 Z/9. On departure from Freetown the convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; Allerton (British, 7195 GRT, built 1941), Appledore (British, 5218 GRT, built 1929), Argyll (British, 4897 GRT, built 1939), Baron Elgin (British, 3942 GRT, built 1933), Charles Schiaffino (French, 3664 GRT, built 1930), Clunepark (British, 3491 GRT, built 1928), Domby (British, 5582 GRT, built 1932), Empire Beauty (British, 7297 GRT, built 1943), Empire Guinevere (British, 7072 GRT, built 1942), Fort Glenlyon (British, 7132 GRT, built 1943), Fort Thompson (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Wellington (British, 7131 GRT, built 1943), Hoggar (French, 5146 GRT, built 1923), Nailsea Moor (British, 4926 GRT, built 1937), Ocean Vanity (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Palembang (Dutch, 7070 GRT, built 1921), Port Darwin (British, 8063 GRT, built 1918), Samdart (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944), Samderwent (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944), Sansu (British, 5446 GRT, built 1939) and Sydland (Swedish, 5134 GRT, built 1920). On departure from Freetown the convoy was escorted by the frigates HMS Odzani (Lt.Cdr. L.A. Sayers, RD, RNR), HMS Aire (A/Lt.Cdr. N.K. Boyd, DSC and Bar, RNR) and the patrol vessel HMS Kilmington (T/Lt. D.D. Humphreys, RNVR). At 1100 Z/12, in position 14°15'N, 17°51'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Dakar; Bactria (British, 2407 GRT, built 1928), Empire Duchess (British, 7067 GRT, built 1943) and Oued Fes (French, 2554 GRT, built 1913). At 1000 Z/13, in position 17°33'N, 17°55'W, the Empire Guinevere parted company with the convoy to proceed to Dakar due to engine trouble. HMS Kilmington escorted her to Dakar and then rejoined the convoy. At 1700 Z/19, in position 33°56'N, 10°30'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Casablanca; Dagrun (Norwegian, 4562 GRT, built 1928) and Empire Planet (British, 4290 GRT, built 1923). They had been escorted to the rendezvous by the patrol vessels L'Emporté and L'Enjoue which subsequently returned to Dakar taking the merchant vessels Charles Schiaffino, Clunepark, Hoggar and Oued Fes with them. They arrived at Casablanca on 20 August. At 0700 Z/20, in position 35°08'N, 10°16'W, the current escort of the convoy, HMS Odzani, HMS Aire and HMS Kilmington parted company to proceed to Gibraltar. They took the merchant vessel Empire Duchess with them. They arrived at Gibraltar on 21 August. At 0800 Z/20, convoy MKS 58(G) joined and merged with convoy SL 166 in position 35°08'N, 10°06'W. The following merchant vessels now joined the combined convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G); Astrid (Norwegian, 2861 GRT, built 1942), Benedick (British (tanker serving as escort oiler), 6978 GRT, built 1928), Boreas (Norwegian, 2801 GRT, built 1920), Capitaine Paul Lemerle (French, 4945 GRT, built 1925), Lagarto (British, 5072 GRT, built 1917), Nolisement (British, 5084 GRT, built 1928), Samshee (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Samthar (British, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and Van de Velde (Dutch, 6389 GRT, built 1919). Also joining were the submarines HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) for onward passage to the U.K. The escort of concoy MKS 58(G) now formed the escort of the combined convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G). This escort was made up of the following warships; frigates HMS Bullen (Lt.Cdr. A.H. Parish, RN), HMS Antigua (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) D.F. White, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Allington Castle (T/A/Lt.Cdr. W.G. Ogden, DSC, RNVR), HMS Hurst Castle (Lt. H.G. Chesterman, DSC and Bar, RNR), HMS Knaresborough Castle (Lt. J.R. Freeman, RNR), HMS Leeds Castle (T/A/Lt.Cdr. W.T. Hodson, DSC, RNVR) and HMS Tintagel Castle (Cdr. G.A. Thring, DSO, RN). At 1500 Z/20, in position 35°45'N, 11°16'W, the rescue ship Copeland (British, 1526 GRT, built 1923) joined the convoy. At 1400 A/21, in position 37°11'N, 13°50'W, the escort carrier HMS Activity (Capt. C. Wauchope, DSC, RN) joined the convoy coming from convoy OS 86 / KMS 60(G). She was escorted to the rendezvous with convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G) by HMS Allington Castle and HMS Leeds Castle which had been detached earlier for this purpose. At 1730 Z/22, in position 39°49'N, 15°48'W, the merchant vessel Samshire (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy coming from the Azores. At 1030 B/27, in position 50°07'N, 05°54'W, the Benedick was detached to convoy OS 87 / KMS 61(G). At 2215 B/27, in position 51°26'N, 04°30'W, HMS Activity was detached to proceed to Greenock. HMS Antigua went with her as escort. At 2222 B/27, in position 51°26'N, 04°30'W, the following merchant vessels were detached to Barry Roads; Baron Elgin and Boreas. At 2258 B/27, in position 51°26'N, 04°41'W, the Nailsea Moor (bound for Loch Ewe for onward routing to a east coast port) was detached to Barry Roads with engine trouble. At 2000 B/28, in position 53°27'N, 04°48'W, the Mersey and Morecambe Bay sections of the convoy parted company. This were the following merchant vessels; Argyll, Bactria, Empire Beauty, Fort Wellington, Nolisement, Ocean Vanity, Palengbang, Samdart, Samshee, Samshire, Sansu and Sydland. At 2100 B/28, in position 53°33'N, 04°49'W, HMS Ultimatum and HMS Ultor parted company to proceed to Rothesay and Holy Loch respectively. HMS Bullen went with them as escort. At 0930 B/29, in position 54°57'N, 05°09'W, the merchant vessel Empire Planet was detached to the Clyde, as was the rescue ship Copeland. The Lagarto (bound for Loch Ewe for onward routing to a east coast port) was also detached to the Clyde due to engine trouble. The remaining ships of the convoy proceeded to Loch Ewe. (95) 10 Aug 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) is wiped (degaussed) at Gibraltar. (94) 11 Aug 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted compass adjustment trials at Gibraltar. (94) 18 Aug 1944 At 1645 B/18, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Gibraltar for the U.K. She was to join convoy MKS 58(G) together with HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN). This convoy was later to merge with convoy SL 167 forming the combined convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G). [For more info on convoy MKS 58(G) see the event ' Convoy MKS 58(G) ' for 18 August 1944.] [For more info on the combined convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G) see the event ' Convoy SL 167 / Combined convoy SL 167 / KMS 58(G) ' for 9 August 1944.] (27) 18 Aug 1944 Convoy MKS 58(G). The remaining merchant vessels of convoy MKS 58 passed Gibraltar into the Atlantic in the evening of 18 August 1944. This were the following merchant vessels; Capitaine Paul Lemerle (French, 4945 GRT, built 1925), Lagarto (British, 5072 GRT, built 1917), Samshee (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Samthar (British, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and Van de Velde (Dutch, 6389 GRT, built 1919). The new escort for convoy MKS 58(G) had joined the convoy at 2000 A/18 relieving the Mediterranean escort which then parted company to proceed to Gibraltar. The new escort was made up of the following vessels; Frigates HMS Bullen (Lt.Cdr. A.H. Parish, RN), HMS Antigua (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) D.F. White, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Allington Castle (T/A/Lt.Cdr. W.G. Ogden, DSC, RNVR), HMS Hurst Castle (Lt. H.G. Chesterman, DSC and Bar, RNR), HMS Knaresborough Castle (Lt. J.R. Freeman, RNR), HMS Leeds Castle (T/A/Lt.Cdr. W.T. Hodson, DSC, RNVR) and HMS Tintagel Castle (Lt. R. Atkinson, DSC and Bar, RNR). With these new escort vessels the following merchant vessels also joined the convoy; Astrid (Norwegian, 2861 GRT, built 1942), Benedick (British (tanker serving as escort oiler), 6978 GRT, built 1928), Boreas (Norwegian, 2801 GRT, built 1920) and Nolisement (British, 5084 GRT, built 1928). The submarines HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) also joined the convoy for onward passage to the U.K. At 0800 Z/20, the convoy made rendezvous with convoy SL 167 coming from Freetown and the combined convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G) was formed. (93) 29 Aug 1944 Around 1115 B/29, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Rothesay from Gibraltar. It was not possible to take HMS Ultimatum in hand for refit before the end of October. Later this was even further postponed until mid-December. (27) 27 Sep 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted D/G trials on the D/G range off Helensburgh. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (12)
17 Feb 1942 At 0012 B/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) altered course to 120° and proceeded at 10 knots to comply with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1151 B/16.
At 0331 B/17, HMS P 34 was in position 34°20'N, 12°30'E and course was altered to 131° for position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
At 0725 B/17, HMS P 34 dived in position 33°56'N, 13°03'E. Course was still 131° but speed (submerged) was only 2 knots.
At 2015 B/17, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued on course 131°.
At 2330 B/17, HMS P 34 was in position 33°33'N, 13°45'E. Course was altered to 163°. (11)

17 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1435 B/17 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Earlier ordered dispositions anticipated the convoy approaching through position 32°48'N, 14°00'E on course 270° or course 290° and possibly course - 263°. Positions may be moved by signal course - 360° or course 180° as necessary according to air reconnaissance. If forced to retire submarines have freedom of movement as follows; P 34 west of 14°05'E. Una between 14°05'E and 14°17'E. P 39 between 14°17'E and 14°28'E. P 38 and Sokol east of 14°28'E. (13)
18 Feb 1942 At 0448 B/18, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E).
At 0700 B/18, HMS P 34 dived and commenced patrolling at periscope depth in the vicinity of position 'X'.
At 2100 B/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled head to sea at slow speed near position 'X'. (11)

19 Feb 1942 At 0430 B/19, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 70 feet in the vicinity of position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). HMS P 34 remained at 70 feet with the Asdic watch closed up due to the weather conditions until 1645 B/19 when she commenced to patrol at periscope depth.
At 2100 B/19, surfaced and proceeded to regain the patrol position and then patrolled in the vicinity of position 'X'. By midnight the weather had moderated considerably. (11)
20 Feb 1942 At 0725 B/20, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived and proceeded to regain position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). Star sighted had been obtained just five minutes before.
At 2100 B/20, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol in the vicinity of position 'X'.

At 2230 B/20, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2207 B/20 was received which cancelled his signal timed 1205 B/20 and ordered the submarines to return to their previous patrol positions. [For the orders contained in the signal timed 1205 B/20 see the other 'event' for 20 February 1942.] (11)
20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1205 B/20 Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

At 2000 B/20 proceed as follows; HMS P 34 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E to Area K off Kerkenah. HMS Una through positions 33°20'N, 13°45'E and 33°20'N, 12°55'E to Area N south of 33°45'W and west of 12°55'E. HMS P 39 was to operate in area L, 32°10'N, 15°00'W - 000° - 40 - 50.
Three more submarines will sail shortly to concentrate again with HMS Una and HMS P 39 in the vicinity of Khoms.
In a signal timed 2207 B/20 they were ordered by Capt. S.10 to return to their previous patrol positions by 0001 B/23. (13)

20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel my signals timed 1125 B/20th to P 38 and 1205 B/20th to Una, P 34 and P 39. Retire at least 50 miles within areas in my signal timed 1435 B/17th and return to the patrol positions just vacated by 0001 B/23. Further order will follow.

The re-deployment / re-positioning of these submarines was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).
At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron.
At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay.

At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23.

At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate.
