She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22.
At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24.

At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy.

Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23.

Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23.

The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24.
The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24. (13)
21 Feb 1942 At 0720 B/21, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived and patrolled in the vicinity of position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). Star sights had been obtained just five minutes before.
At 2000 B/21, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol in the vicinity of position 'X'. (11)

21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 B/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); My signal timed 2207 B/20. Four submarines leave Malta through position 34°30'N, 14°10'E to positions as follows by dawn/23. P 36, P 35, Unbeaten through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E to position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E), position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), position 'S' (32°44'N, 14°47'E) respectively and Upholder direct from first position given to position 'R' (32°50'N, 14°53'E). My signal timed 1435 B/17, amend and to retire from area. P 36 and P 38, between 14°28'E and 14°40'E former south latter north of 32°45'N. East of 14°40'E, Upholder north of 32°48'N, P 35 south of 32°41'N and Unbeaten between those latitudes. Convoy may be expected any time after dawn/23 and if an eastbound convoy is encountered first that proceeding westward to Tripoli is primary target and position must not be compromised before its passage. (13)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (13)

23 Feb 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) fires four torpedoes against an Italian convoy about 80 nautical miles east of Tripoli, Libya in position 32°51'N, 13°58'E. All torpedoes fired missed their targets.

The convoy attacked was made up of the Italian transports Ravello (6142 GRT, built 1941), Unione (6070 GRT, built 1942 and brand new) and Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940). Escort was provided by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Nicolo Zeno, Maestrale, Strale, Premuda, and the Italian torpedo boats Pallade and Calliope. The torpedoes were spotted by an escorting aircraft which machine-gunned them to give the alarm and the convoy altered course to avoid them. Unione was missed ahead and astern by two torpedoes. The destroyer Vivaldi depth charged the submarine.
0400 B/23 - P 34 dived to 70 feet in position 'X' (32°52'N, 14°00'E). Asdic watch was set.
0700 B/23 - Commenced patrolling at periscope depth.

0912 B/23 - Sighted a tanker of about 5000 tons and a small coaster of about 500 tons bearing 175°. They were proceeding on course 280° very close inshore. These were not attacked in view of the expected convoy.

[We have been unable to identify these vessels. The only thing that could be found was the Italian tanker Giulio Giordani (10534 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the Italian destroyer Emmanuele Pessagno which sailed from Brindisi at 2205 B/20 via Corfu (1112 B/21-1335 B/21) and arrived at Tripoli at 1720 B/23. This does, however, not even match the description given by P 34 closely.
1125 B/23 - Sighted two merchant vessels and three destroyers bearing 040°. Enemy course was 250°. Started attack.
1130 B/23 - The convoy appeared to consist of three modern merchant ships of 9000 tons in line ahead. Seven destroyers were seen to be the escort. They were of various types.
1149 B/23 - Fired a salvo of four torpedoes from 4500 yards.
1157 B/23 - Heard three explosions.
1158 B/23 - A counter attack commenced. A total of 57 depth charges were dropped some quite near. P 34 managed to escape.
Between 1408 B/23 and 1508 B/23 - Distant explosions were heard.
1420 B/23 - Both hunting destroyers were now out of sight.
1515 B/23 - Went to 60 feet and commenced to reload the torpedo tubes. Also course was altered to 300° to close the position of the attack.
1700 B/23 - Completed reloading the torpedo tubes.
1745 B/23 - Arrived in the position of the attack. Went to periscope depth. Nothing was seen. Altered course to 360° to clear the area.
2015 B/23 - Surfaced and proceeded on course 339° at 8 knots to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 B/23. [For the text of this signal see the other 'event' for 23 February 1942.]
2342 B/23 - In position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. Altered course to 308° to proceed to area 'K'.

23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25.
HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E.

HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E.

HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E.

HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E.
They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (13)

24 Feb 1942 At 0230 B/24, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 2255 B/23 to the Capt. S.10 which stated the following; Your 2123 B/23. At 0912 B/23 sighted one tanker one small motor vessel close inshore. At 1149 B/23 attacked convoy of three motor vessels of about 9000 tons escorted by seven destroyers proceeding on course 250°. Result uncertain possibly three hits, other explosions heard during morning and afternoon.

This was a response to the signal of Capt. S.10 timed 2123 B/23 which contained the following orders; When safe to do so make short report of what westbound ships were sighted today (23rd). Other submarines are not to report yet. (12)
25 Feb 1942 At 0225 B/25, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol in the vicinity of position 34°37'N, 11°53'E (in area 'K').

26 Feb 1942 At 2015 B/26, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) surfaced and left the area near Kerkennah no.4 buoy to return to Malta as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1240 B/25.
This signal contained the following orders for HMS P 34; Arrive at Marsamxett (Malta) 0715 B/28. Proceed through positions 35°00'N, 12°32'E and 35°36'N, 14°10'E en-route to Malta. (11)
28 Feb 1942 At 0734 B/28, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)

11 Mar 1942 At 1930 B/11, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Calabrian coast from Cape Spartivento.

13 Mar 1942 At 0730 B/13, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol at periscope depth in the vicinity of Cape Spartivento. She had dived to 70 feet at 0513 B/13 with Asdic watch closed up.
At 2035 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced. Course was set to proceed to the vicinity of Punta Stilo to patrol there in accordance with the patrol orders. (11)

14 Mar 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian submarine Ammiraglio Millo (offsite link) in the Ionian Sea off Punta (Cape) Stilo, Calabria, Italy in position 38°27'N, 16°37'E.
0500 B/14 - Dived to 70 feet with Asdic watch set. Commenced to close the land.
0730 B/14 - Commenced to patrol at periscope depth and patrolled in the vicinity of Punta Stilo.
1130 B/14 - Sighted a small merchant vessel close inshore proceeding on course 035°. Turned towards and closed.

1136 B/14 - Sighted a dazzle painted merchant ship of 3000 tons. This looked like, and probably was, an armed merchant cruiser of the Citta di Napoli-class. Another small merchant ship, of about 3000 tons was sighted astern of this suspected armed merchant cruiser. Started attack on the leading ship which was zigzagging up to 80° and at irregular intervals. The other two ships were on a steady course. One aircraft was seen patrolling overhead, escorting this convoy.

Although P 34 closed at high speed, the attack was abandoned at 1222 B/14. The range had never been less than 7500 yards. It was unfortunate the convoy had not been sighted sooner but the visibility towards the land at that time was bad.

The ships sighted were probably the steamers Enrichetta (Italian, 4652 GRT, built 1907) and Pegli (Italian, 1593 GRT, built 1910), the tanker Ennio (Italian, 464 GRT, built 1917) escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Francesco Morosini (2423 GRT, built 1928) which sailed from Messina at 0205 B/14 at 8 knots and arrived at Taranto at 0925 B/15.
1255 B/14 - Sighted the conning tower of a submarine bearing 270°. Range was about 8000 yards. Started attack.
1319 B/14 - Fired four torpedoes from 2200 yards. Two hits were obtained and the submarine was seen to sink.
1325 B/14 - Surfaced and picked up fourteen survivors. These included four officers and ten ratings.
1402 B/14 - Dived and withdrew from the area.
1700 B/14 - Went to 80 feet and commenced to reload the torpedo tubes.

2015 B/14 - Surfaced and proceeded on course 180° at 9.5 knots. Lt. Harrison had decided to return to Malta to land the Italian survivors and embark four new torpedoes. A signal on P 34 returning to Malta was passed at 0015 B/15. On the way back P 34 was ordered to proceed through position 37°13'N, 16°45'E.
16 Mar 1942 At 0750 B/16, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta to land the Italian survivors and to load four new spare torpedoes and provisions. (11)

17 Mar 1942 At 1930 B/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta to resume her patrol. She was now ordered to patrol to the south of the Straits of Messina between positions 37°13'N, 16°02'E and 37°32'N, 15°44'E (preferably in position 'L', 37°36'N, 15°49'E, until 0400 B/20 when she was to move to the position vacated by HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN).

At 1958 B/17, when in position 060° - St. Elmo light - 1 mile a motor torpedo boat was seen laying stopped which did not reply to the challenge. HMS P 34 dived. At 2005 B/17, this motor torpedo boat dropped 12 small charges in the vicinity of P 34. HE was heard on the starboard quarter until 2120 B/17.

At 2123 B/17, HMS P 34 surfaced in position 068° - St. Elmo light - 3.5 miles. On surfacing a motor torpedo boat was seen laying stopped about 800 yards on the starboard beam. P 34 dived again. At 2135 B/17, this motor torpedo boat dropped 14 small charges in the vicinity of P 34. HE was lost at 2358 B/17.
[We have been unable to identify this suspected motor torpedo boat.]
At 0010 B/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 071° at 5 knots with the Asdic watch closed up until 0105 B/17 when speed was increased to 9.5 knots when the battery charge was completed.
No German S-boats (motor torpedo boats) were operating of Malta during this night. Perhaps the motoro torpedo boats encountered were Italian MAS boats.
19 Mar 1942 At 1030 B/19, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'L' (37°36'N, 15°49'E) and commenced in this vicinity on courese 090° - 270°.
At 2100 B/19, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol in the vicinity of position 'L'. (11)
20 Mar 1942 At 0400 B/20, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) set course for position 'M' (37°43'N, 15°33'E) in accordance with orders received.
Around 0800 B/20, she arrived in position 'M' and patrolled at periscope depth on course 090° - 270° through position 'M'.
At 1230 B/20, the masts of a small schooner were sighted bearing 360° at a range of about 6 miles.
At 1305 B/20, a small object was sighted bearing 030° at a range of about 6 miles. This was a small boat which may have been a motor torpedo boat.
Both the schooner and motor torpedo boat were laying stopped or proceeding very slowly. It is considered both were on A/S patrol.
At 1500 B/20, both the schooner and motor torpedo boat passed out of sight in the direction of the Straits of Messina.
At 2100 B/20, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol on course 090° - 270° through position 'M'. (11)
21 Mar 1942 At 0150 B/21, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) picked up HE on bearing 133°.

At 0210 B/21, it was reported that the HE was that of a motor boat but nothing was seen. HMS P 34 dived. The HE was then reported as being from a reciprocating engine doing about 80 revs. This was considered to most likely be from a trawler. The vessel, which could still not be seen, passed across starboard quarter and faded out at 0230 B/21 on bearing 090°. As the battery was full and Allied aircraft were known to be operating in the vicinity Lt. Harrison decided to remain dived.

At 0300 B/21, HE was heard on bearing 020°. This passed down the starboard side and was very probably a destroyer or torpedo boat. Two depth charges were heard to explode at 0430 B/21. After that this HE practically faded out then re-appeared passing up the starboard side, finally fading out on bearing 100° at 0530 B/21.

Perhaps the HE heard was of a ship of the convoy made up of the German transport Brook (1225 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian destroyer Strale and the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari which sailed from Messina at 0015 B/21. At 0428 B/21, the convoy was attacked by Swordfish torpedo bombers, 18 miles south of Capo dell'Armi; two torpedoes missed Strale. At 1200 B/21, the torpedo boat was detached and proceeded to Trapani where she arrived at 0800 B/22. The other two vessels arrived at Argostoli at 0845 B/22-0915 B/22. During the night of 22/23, Brook ran aground in the Argostoli anchorage but managed to free herself without damage. They sailed again at 1745 B/26 and arrived at Navarino at 0744 B/27 and sailed from there at 1930 B/27. They arrived at Benghazi at 0917 B/29-1100 B/29.
At 0700 B/21, HMS P 34 came to periscope depth and continued to patrol in the vicinity of position 'M' (37°43'N, 15°33'E).

At 1020 B/21, a motor torpedo boat was sighted on bearing 260°. This motor torpedo boat was painted white. Range was about 4 miles. The enemy was proceeding on course 020° towards Messina at 20 knots. This was not an A/S vessel.
This was the German S-boat (large motor torpedo boat) S-59 which had departed Augusta at 0830 B/21 for Palermo where she was to undergo an engine refit.
At 2100 B/21, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol through position 'M' on course 090° - 270°. (14)
22 Mar 1942 At 0205 B/22, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) picked up HE bearing 010°. Course was altered towards to investigate but nothing was seen.

At 0210 B/22, HMS P 34 dived. The HE was reported as being from a reciprocating engine doing 80 revs. The source of the HE passed down the starboard side fading out at 0230 B/22 on bearing 050°. This was thought to be coming from the same trawler as on the previous night.
At 0245 B/22, HMS P 34 surfaced.
At 0500 B/22, HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet. Asdic watch was set.
At 0700 B/22, HMS P 34 came to periscope depth, patrolled in the vicinity of position 'M' (37°43'N, 15°33'E).
At 1005 B/22, four distant explosions were heard followed by several single explosions at short intervals.
At 2100 B/22, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled head on to sea at slow speed on course 138° due to the weather conditions. (11)
23 Mar 1942 At 0400 B/23, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet with Asdic watch set.
She remained patrolling at 80 feet after daybreak due to the weather conditions being unsuitable for periscope patrol.
In a signal timed 1744 B/23, from the Capt. S.10, HMS P 34 was informed that she had freedom of action areas 'M' and 'N' after 0800 B/24.
At 2100 B/23, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled at slow speed, head on to sea on course 100°.
At 2230 B/23, HMS P 34 received a signal timed 1941 B/23 from the Capt. S.10 that an enemy 6" cruiser had broken off from an Italian task force and was possibly proceeding single towards Messina.

This probably referred to the Italian light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Francesco Stocco which was returning to Messina and was reinforced by the torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cantore at 1145 B/24. They arrived at Messina at 1345-1354 B/24. (15)
24 Mar 1942 At 0400 B/24, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch closed up.
At 0800 B/24, it was decided to remain at 80 feet as the weather conditions were not suitable for periscope depth patrol.
At 1048 B/24, HE was picked up bearing 035°. Went to periscope depth but sighted no surface vessel. An aircraft was seen patrolling though.

At 1100 B/24, a puff of smoke was sighted on bearing 076° but nothing could be seen of a vessel. Not even with 10 feet of periscope. HE was lost at 1105 B/24 and at 1115 B/24 the patrolling aircraft approached, so P 34 went back to 80 feet.

This vessel could not have been the expected cruiser as it was leaving the Straits of Messina instead of approaching but it may possibly have been a destroyer or torpedo boat proceeding to seaward to rendezvous with the reported cruiser.

At 1715 B/24, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1426 B/24 was received which contained the following orders for P 34; P 34 is to leave patrol P.M./25 to position 37°00'N, 15°40'E to arrive at Malta A.M./27. (15)

25 Mar 1942 At 0130 B/25, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the signal timed 0040 B/25 from the Capt. S.10. This signal contained the following information. Two enemy cruisers at 2350 B/24 in position 36°25'N, 16°48'E, course 330° speed 15 knots. (this position was amended later in the signal timed 0121 B/25, to 36°45'N, 16°45'E). Albacores will attack around 0300 B/25.

No enemy cruiser(s) was / were at sea on this day. These were the actually the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade which sailed from Messina at 0625 B/24-0635 B/24 to search for survivors of the Italian destroyer Scirocco which had foundered. Folgore was attacked by Albacore torpedo bombers at 0308 B/25 in 36°50’ N, 16°40’ E and returned to Messina at 1008 B/25. Pallade followed at 1348 B/25. The search had yielded no result. The Italian hospital ship Arno (7785 GRT, built 1912) found eight survivors from Lanciere, an Italian seaplane picked up another seven survivors from the Italian destroyer Lanciere and a second seaplane found two survivors from Scirocco.
HMS P 34 kept patrolling in position 'M' (37°43'N, 15°33'E). The weather had considerably improved but it did not yet permit Asdic watch being set on the surface.
At 0550 B/25, HMS P 34 dived to 70 feet with the Asdic watch closed up.
At 0700 B/25, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth in the vicinity of position 'M'.

At 0940 B/25, the masts, bridge and funnel of a Spica-class torpedo boat were sighted bearing 180°. Enemy course was 290°. The torpedo boat passed out of sight at 1010 B/25 on bearing 240° but was heard on Asdic until 1200 B/25 when the HE faded out on bearing 347°. This torpedo boat maintained a steady speed and is not considered to have been carrying out an A/S sweep.
This was most probably Pallade (see above).
At 2040 B/25, HMS P 34 surfaced and left patrol proceeding on course 160° at 8.5 knots.
27 Mar 1942 At 1025 B/27, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)

6 Apr 1942 At 2115 B/6, HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol along the Calabrian coast and then proceed to patrol off Capo Santa Maria di Leuca to operate against traffic from Taranto to the Adriatic.
Lt. Coombe had taken command of HMS P 34 vice Lt. Harrison for this patrol in order to give Lt. Harrison a rest.
At 2214 B/6, in position 35°56'N, 14°38'E a motor torpedo boat was sighted bearing 280° at a range of 2000 yards. HMS P 34 dived.
[We have been unable to identify this supposed motor torpedo boat.]
At 2300 B/6, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded.
7 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1131 B/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN);
HMS Una was to patrol in position 37°42'N, 15°37'E from 2301 B/7 to 0531 B/8. HMS P 34 was to patrol in position 37°00'N, 16°14'E from 0259 B/8 to 0731 B/8.
In a follow up signal timed 2135 B/7, the Capt. S.10 informed both submarines that Wellington aircraft from Malta will search and attack enemy shipping after midnight.

This was to try to intercept the BETA-convoy which was made up of the German transport Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare which sailed from Naples at 0130 B/7-0208 B/7. The Italian torpedo boat Orsa had sailed from Messina at 1745 B/5 for an A/S sweep and attacked a submarine at 2010 B/5 in position 200° - Cape Spartivento - 3 miles and claimed it sunk. She was then ordered to reinforce the escort of the Ankara. At 0415 B/7, the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza departed from Messina to join Ankara. At 0800 B/7, the Italian torpedo boat Lince sailed from Messina to reinforce the escort and joined at 1301 B/7, followed by Orsa at 1336 B/7 and shortly after by Giuseppe Dezza. Ankara, Antoniotto Usodimare, Giuseppe Dezza and Orsa arrived at Messina at 1728-1750 B/7. Lince and two MAS boats were on an A/S search, the torpedo boat returning to Messina only at 0300 B/8. Ankara, Antoniotto Usodimare and Lince sailed again at 2152 B/7-2330 B/7. At 0022 B/8, Ankara and Antoniotto Usodimare accidentally collided in position 37°55'20"N, 15°25'10"E. The three ships returned to Messina at 0422 B/8-0426 B/8. Ankara, escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince, sailed from Messina at 2150 B/8. At 0837 B/10, the Italian torpedo boat Pallade met them to pilot them in. They arrived at Benghazi at 1145 B/10. (16)
8 Apr 1942 At 0205 B/8, HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) arrived in position 37°00'N, 16°14'E. Asdic watch was set and P 34 commenced to patrol in the vicinity.
At 0303 B/8, a distant explosion was hard resembling that of a torpedo was heard to the north-west. Visibility was now only two cables.
At 0540 B/8, HMS P 34 dived in position 37°00'N, 16°15'E and continued to patrol in the vicinity.
At 0730 B/8, course was set for Punta Stilo.
At 1152 B/8, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1046 B/8 was received in which P 34 was ordered to proceed direct to the area off Cape Santa Maria di Leuca off the Gulf of Taranto.
At 2050 B/8, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 034° at 9 knots. (11)

8 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1046 B/8, HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) was ordered to remain on patrol as in Capt. S.10's singal timed 1131 B/7 until A.M. on the 9th. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was ordered to proceed direct to the area off Cape Santa Maria di Leuca off the Gulf of Taranto. (16)
10 Apr 1942 At 0535 B/10, HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) dived in position 39°35'N, 18°15'E.
At 0800 B/10 the Cape Santa Maria di Leuca lighthouse was sighted and course was altered to close the coast.

At 1037 B/10, in position 39°41'N, 18°18'E, the funnel and masts of a merchant ship were sighted bearing 340° at a range of 7 miles. It was very close to the shore. Course was altered to 050° and P 34 ran in to close the enemy's track if he was coming east. A small motor fishing boat was seen on the bearing steering east and also very close to the shore.
At 1046 B/10, only smoke could be seen and this too soon disappeared. Apparently the enemy was proceeding westwards.

The vessel sighted was most probably the steamer Doris Ursino (Italian, 891 GRT, built 1886) which sailed from Brindisi at 0710 B/9 and arrived at Otranto at 1500 B/9 and Gallipoli at 1430 B/10 and sailed from there at 0645 B/11 and reached Taranto at 1250 B/11 for eventual passage to Palermo.
At 1105 B/10, in position 39°43'N, 18°19'E a small steam trawler was sighted bearing 070°. Enemy course was 300°.

Between 1115 B/10 and 1155 B/10, the small motor fishing boat previously sighted came out to seaward and circled very close to P 34. This vessel was only about 20 tons in size and about 60 feet long. It had two masts, an amidships cabin and was driven by a high revving diesel or petrol engine. No armament was seen.
At 1900 B/10, HMS P 34 commenced to withdraw to seaward for the night and to charge.
At 2100 B/10, HMS P 34 surfaced. (11)
11 Apr 1942 At 0400 B/11, HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) commenced to close the coast near Cape Santa Maria di Leuca.
At 0520 B/11, HMS P 34 dived in position 39°36'N, 18°24'E.
At 1010 B/11, while in position 39°40'N, 18°28'E the same fishing vessel as the day before was seen bearing 310° at a range of 8000 yards.

At 1205 B/11, in position 39°44'N, 18°30'E, some was sighted bearing 350°. The source of the smoke was very close to the shore and was coming southwards. Course was altered to 290° and P 34 ran in at high speed.

At 1300 B/11, when within three miles from the shore the smoke was observed to be coming from a small steam fishing smack. HMS P 34 patrolled all afternoon three miles from the shore but noting further was seen.
At 1700 B/11, HMS P 34 commenced to withdraw to seaeward for the night and establish patrol in what was hoped to be the route taken by ships bound for the Gulf of Patras.
At 2135 B/11, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled on courses 290° - 110°. (11)
12 Apr 1942 At 0400 B/12, HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) commenced to close the coast.
At 0515 B/12, HMS P 34 dived in position 39°42'N, 18°39'E.
At 0630 B/12, two distant explosions were heard.

At 0924 B/12, in position 39°45'N, 18°29'5"E, HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) was shaken by a violent explosion. The submarine was blown to the surface. The fore hatch lifted and admitted a quantity of water. All lights in the after part of the ship were extinguished. Three depth gauges in the control room were put out of action and considerable other damage was done. P 34 obviously had a lucky escape after hitting a mine. This was most likely a mine laid by the Italian minelayer Barletta in June 1940 (minefield 4 AS).

P 34 altered course to get clear of the coast to make good defects and to establish if she could remain on patrol. In the afternoon most damage had been repaired and it was decided to remain on patrol.
Between 1125 B/12 and 1255 B/12, twenty-six distant explosions were heard.

At 1311 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1147 B/12 was received which ordered HMS P 34 to patrol in position 39°07'N, 18°16'E until 0900 B/13 and then to proceed to position 40°00'N, 17°15'E to intercept enemy heavy units. Course was altered to 197° to comply with this order.
In the afternoon most of the minor damage was repaired. One cell container of No.2 battery was dound to be cracked and necessitated the isolation of two cells in each battery.

At 2115 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2020 B/12, which ordered HMS Torbay (Cdr. A.C.C. Miers, DSO and Bar, RN) to take part in the interception of the enemy heavy units. Also in this signal the second position in which HMS P 34 was to patrol in (40°00'N, 17°15'E) was changed to 40°02'N, 17°15'E.
At 2200 B/12, HMS P 34 surfaced and set course to 207° at 8 knots. (11)
13 Apr 1942 At 0020 B/13, HMS P 34 arrived in position 39°07'N, 18°16'E and commenced patrolling on course 020° - 200° while charging
At 0515 B/13, HMS P 34 dived and continued patrolling in the vicinity of the patrol position.
At 0900 B/13, HMS P 34 altered course to 320° to proceed to position 40°02'N, 17°15'E.

As it was likely that enemy 'heavy units' might be met Lt. Coombe decided that the depth settings of the torpedoes were to be altered. It was then found out that due to the explosion of the previous day the torpedoes could not be retracted from the tubes nor that they could be fired. The defects could not be repaired at sea.
At 0925 B/13, twelve distant explosions were heard.
At 1500 B/13, six distant explosions were heard.
At 1528 B/13, Lt. Coombe reluctantly decided to abandon the patrol and return to Malta as the top stops and spring catches of the torpedo tubes could not be cleared. Course was altered to 214°.

At 2016 B/13, while in position 39°12'N, 18°04'E, HE was picked up on bearing 328°. This was subsequently identified as coming from a destroyer which was closing at 14 knots. This destroyer came out of the Gulf of Taranto and passed close to HMS P 34 and eventually faded away towards Messina.
At 2136 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced on continued on course 214° at a speed of 9 knots.

At 0006 B/14, passed her signal timed 1528 B/13 in which she reported that she was returning to Malta due to the damage sustained. This signal was acknowledged by the Capt. S.10 in his signal timed 0155 B/14. (11)
16 Apr 1942 Around 1945 B/16, HMS P 34 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (11)

29 Apr 1942 At 0430 B/29, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for Alexandria. Due to heavy enemy air attacks it had been decided to retire the small number of submarines currently based at Malta to Alexandria.
HMS P 34 was to leave Malta through the north-east swept channel.
En-route to Alexandria she was to pass through positions 34°55'N, 20°05'E and 32°09'N, 28°16'E thence to the end of the Alexandria searched channel around 0530 C/8. She was to remain dived by day.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (17)

7 May 1942 At 0030 C/7, a fire occurred in the motor room of HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN). This resulted in the complete stoppage of all machinery and plunging the submarine in darkness. At 0140 B/7, HMS P 34 was able to proceed at reduced speed on one generator and one battery section. (17)
8 May 1942 At 0330 B/8, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived near the end of the searched channel to Alexandria.
At 0450 B/8, recognition signals were exchanged with HMS Hasty (Lt.Cdr. N.H.G. Austen, RN) which was on patrol.
At 0530 B/8, HMS P 34 entered the searched channel.
At 1037 B/8, HMS P 34 secured alongside HMS Medway (Capt. Philip Ruck-Keene, RN) in Alexandria harbour. (17)
11 May 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Alexandria for Port Said.
12 May 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at Port Said from Alexandria for docking and repairs. [The dates of docking are unknown to us as the is no log available for this month.]
31 May 1942 With her repairs completed, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Port Said for Alexandria.
2 Jun 1942 At 0725 B/2, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at Alexandria from Port Said. En-route a deep dive trial had been carried out. (18)
4 Jun 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Alexandria. (18)

6 Jun 1942 Around 1825 B/6, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Alexandria for her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to give cover during convoy operations to Malta by forming a patrol line with HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) (cover of Operation VIGOROUS).
HMS P 34 was to take up position 'C' (35°50'N, 19°02'E) in the patrol line.
14 Jun 1942 At 0230 C/14, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'C' (35°50'N, 19°02'E) and commenced to patrol in the vicinity.
At 0540 C/14, HMS P 34 dived to 80 with with the Asdic watch closed up.
At 0700 C/14, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol in the vicinity of position 'C' while at periscope depth.
Between 1640 C/14 and 1655 C/14, a series of about 20 underwater explosions were heard.
At 2219 C/14, HMS P 34 surfaced and altered course to 230° for position 'C 1' (35°30'N, 16°03'E) in accordance with the operation orders. (17)

15 Jun 1942 At 0117 C/15, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2321 C/15 which ordered the four submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla to return to their positions in the A - D patrol line.
At 0400 C/15, aircraft flares were sighted to the northward. Shortly afterwards HMS P 34 stopped and set the Asdic watch but no HE was picked up.
At 0500 C/15, HMS P 34 proceeded on course 078° at 2 knots.
At 0540 C/15, HMS P 34 dived to periscope depth.

At 0615 C/15, HMS P 34 sighted two large ships with escort vessels on bearing 348°. Air air attack on these ships was in progress and the ships were manoeuvring all over the place. P 34 was unable to obtain a favourable firing position. Two large units (battleships) with destroyers were seen proceeding on course 090° while three other units (cruisers) with destroyers were proceeding on course 180°. A large column of smoke remained in the vicinity of the air attack. [This must have been from the damaged Italian heavy cruiser Trento.]
Of the ships sighted above, Force 1 was sighted first then Force 2 (see below, 'Italian naval movements to counter operation VIGOROUS' and 'Italian naval movements to counter operation HARPOON').
At 0623 C/15, an attack was started on a formation of three cruiser escorted by four destroyers. Enemy course was 180° at 20 knots. The speed was obtained by Asdic.

P 34 was inside the port screen of the cruisers and about 1200 yards off track when at 0637 C/15 the enemy altered course to 90° to port which put HMS P 34 right ahead at 1500 yards range. P 34 went to 80 feet. On regaining periscope depth P 34 was right astern of the enemy formation at a range of 5000 yards. The enemy altered course back to 180° and reduced speed to about 12 knots.

At 0700 C/15, the two battleships, with their escorting destroyers, five in number, were now seen to be steering 180° at reduced speed at a range of 10000 yards. The battleships were thought to be Littorio-class vessels. P 34 closed until 0715 C/15 by when it was quite clear that there was no chance of attack. These battleships later joined up with the three cruisers and the whole force then proceeded to the south-eastward.

At 0715 C/15, course was altered to 330° to close the smoke which was hanging around in the vicinity of the dawn air attack. The source of the smoke was later identified as a Trento-class cruiser lying stopped and obviously badly damaged, with flares coming out of the forward funnel and on fire in the vicinity of 'X' turret. Two destroyers were circling round her making a continual smoke screen. As the cruiser was definitely out of action incapable of movement it was decided to pass an enemy report on the enemy battlefleet to the C-in-C Mediterranean and return later to sink the cruiser if still necessary.
At 0915 C/15, HMS P 34 commenced to withdraw to pass an enemy report.

At 1012 C/15, a loud underwater explosion was heard. On coming to periscope depth a large column of smoke about 1000 feet high was seen and also the bow of the cruiser was seen sticking out of the water. The cruiser was seen to sink at 1015 C/15 in position 35°53'N, 18°57'E. The cruiser sank as a result of torpedo hits by HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN). P 35 had gone deep after firing to evade possible enemy counter measures but Lt. Harrison in P 34 sat more or less front row and was able to confirm that the cruiser had sunk.
At 1223 C/15, HMS P 34 surfaced and passed her enemy report.
At 1230 C/15, HMS P 34 dived again and proceeded on course 150°.

At 1348 C/15, a T-class submarine was sighted bearing 296° at a range of about 2000 yards proceeding on course 090°. This submarine dived almost immediately and was probably obtaining a sun sight. [This was most likely HMS Taku (Lt.Cdr. J.G. Hopkins, RN) which dived at 1352 C/15.]
At 1502 C/15, HMS P 34 altered course to 330° to regain patrol position 'C' (35°50'N, 19°02'E).
At 1744 C/15, HMS P 34 surfaced and altered course to 122° in accordance with the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1515 C/15.
At 1803 C/15, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) was seen on the surface on bearing 160° at a range of 2 miles. They got in touch through V/S and discussed the situation.
At 1820 C/15, a signal was received in which the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1515 C/15 was cancelled.
P 34 altered course to 098° at 8 knots in order to comply with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1315 C/15 which ordered HMS P 34 to patrol in position 35°35'N, 19°19'E.

At 1825 C/15, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1625 C/15 was received which ordered to patrol position to be moved 050° - 20 miles. Five minutes later course was altered to 068° to comply, proceeding to position 35°37'N, 19°37'E.
At 2105 C/15, HMS P 34 dived for a formation of aircraft.
At 2130 C/15, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 122° at 8 knots.
Italian naval movements to counter operation VIGOROUS.

Force 1: battleships Littorio and Vittorio Veneto, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Bersagliere, Mitragliere, Legionario (equipped with the German Dete Gerät Radar), Freccia and Folgore, sailed from Taranto at 1230 B/14-1330 B/14. Force 2: Heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento (3rd Cruiser Division), light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi and Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta (8th Cruiser Division), screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Geniere and Corazziere, sailed from Taranto at 1152-1246 B/14. They were to take position 15 miles ahead of the Littorio-group. They came under several air attacks. At 0200 B/15, the Littorio-group was attacked by five or six torpedo bombers but managed to avoid them. At 0515 B/15, the Gorizia-group was under attack and Trento was hit by a torpedo and at 0800 B/15, she was taken in tow by a destroyer. At 0816 B/15, Littorio was hit by a bomb on “A” turret, one man was killed and twelve wounded. At 0845 B/15, she avoided an attack by five torpedo bombers. At 0910 B/15, Trento was hit forward by one or two torpedoes from HMS P 35, causing her ammunition to explode. She sank immediately with the loss of 402 officers and ratings. The destroyers Antonio Pigafetta and Saetta which had sailed from Brindisi at 1330 B/14-1342 B/14, picked up 602 survivors and brought them to Messina at 1955-2055 B/15. The Italian hospital ships Aquileia and Città di Trapani were sailed to search for survivors. At 2340 B/15, Littorio was hit by a torpedo on the starboard bow and took 1,000 tons of water. Force 1 returned to Taranto at 1609 B/16-1709 B/16, except Mitragliere which returned at 1935 B/16. Force 2 returned to Taranto at 1639 B/16-1745 B/16, except Camicia Nera which had sighted a submarine at 0700 B/16 in position 38°36'N, 19°39'E and returned only at 1900 B/16.

Italian naval movements to counter operation HARPOON. Force 3: Light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia, Eugenio Montecuccoli (7th Cruiser Division), screened by the destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Ascari and Vincenzo Gioberti (which turned back because of defects and arrived at Naples at 0430 B/15), Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello and Nicolò Zeno (Vivaldi and Zeno were hit and proceeded to Pantelleria), which sailed from Palermo at 2030 B/14 and returned to Naples at 1030 B/16. The destroyer Premuda sailed from Trapani at 1810 B/14 to join them and returned to Trapani at 2055 B/15. The torpedo boats Polluce and Castore sailed from Trapani at 2000 B/14 to cover the passage of the 7th Division and returned at 0645 B/15. (17)
16 Jun 1942 At 0005 C/16, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 35°47'N, 19°37'E and commenced to patrol in the vicinity.

At 0440 C/16, HMS P 34 altered course to 274° to return to patrol position 'C' (35°50'N, 19°02'E). This was in accordance with orders contained in the orders in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2321 C/15 which ordered the four submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla to return to their positions in the A - D patrol line.
At 0540 C/16, HMS P 34 dived to 80 with with the Asdic watch closed up.
At 0700 C/16, HMS P 34 came up to periscope depth and continued her passage to position 'C'.

At 1315 C/16, a hospital ship was sighted bearing 320° at a range of 8 miles. Enemy course was 180°. The hospital ship was apparently searching in the vicinity in which the Italian cruiser Trento sank. She finally disappeared at 1500 C/16 proceeding to the north.
The Italian hospital ships Aquileia (9448 GRT, built 1914) and Città di Trapani (2467 GRT, built 1929) had been sent to the scene so it must have been one of these two.
At 2225 C/16, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolling in the vicinity of position 'C' where she also patrolled the following day. (17)

17 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1833 C/17, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to proceeded towards Taranto with despatch and that additional orders would follow.
In a follow up signal timed 2015 C/17 the following orders were signalled; My 1835 C/17 adjust speed and time leaving present patrol area so as to arrive by dawn 20th.

In yet another signal timed 2101 C/17 the following orders were signalled; Establish patrol by dawn 20th as follows; HMS Thorn in the vicinity of position 40°04'N, 17°13'E HMS Thrasher in the vicinity of position 40°07'N, 17°26'E. HMS Porpoise in the vicinity of position 35°01'N, 16°29'E. 10th Submarine Flotilla was to patrol line; 4 - 39°47'N 17°17'E - 7 miles - 055°. HMS Una in position 'A', 39°47'N, 17°17'E. Remainder in present relative positions. [thus HMS P 31, HMS P 34 and HMS P 35 in positions 'B', 'C' and 'D' respectively.] Proceed direct to new position diving at dawn 20th on line 247° from Cape Maria di Leuca. (19)

18 Jun 1942 At 0156 C/18, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2101 C/17. Course was set for the Gulf of Taranto at 8 knots in accordance with the orders contained in this signal. [For the text of the signal see the event for 17 June 1942.]
At 0613 C/18, HMS P 34 dived to periscope depth, continuing on course 346° for the Gulf of Taranto.
At 2140 C/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued on course 346° at 10 knots. (17)

19 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1109 C/19, the Capt. S.1, ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in position 'C' and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to return to Alexandria forthwith through following positions to arrive as stated; HMS Porpoise through positions 35°22'N, 18°11'E and 32°21'N, 28°01'E to arrive at dawn on the 22nd. HMS Thorn through positions 37°21'N, 20°30'E, 35°42'N, 21°57(?)'E and 32°08'N, 28°57'E to arrive at dawn on the 25th. The four boats of the 10th submarine flotilla were to cross Latitude 34.59'N in the following Longitudes; HMS Una in 21°28'E, HMS P 31 in 22°04'E, HMS P 34 in 22°40'E and HMS P 35 in 23°18'E. [More positions to proceed through are given in the signal but it contains several errors]. The four boats were to arrive off the boom before 2030 C/27 or after 0730 C/28. (19)

19 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2059 C/19, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN); Establish patrol line forthwith 5 - 38°16'N, 19°47'E - 8 - 150° in the order HMS Una, HMS P 31, HMS P 34, HMS P 35, HMS Thrasher. HMS Una in position 38°16', 19°47'E. HMS Thorn proceeding as ordered in my signal timed 1109 C/19 is not to cross 37°N before 2200 C/20.

In a signal timed 2205 C/19, the above listed submarines were informed of the following by Capt. S.1; On reaching position in my 2059 C/19 proceed submerged at 3 knots on course 145° until 2130 hours. HMS Thrasher then return to patrol area keeping at least 10 miles to north east above course, and the four boats of the 10th S/M Flotilla proceed in accordance with my 1109 C/19. It is important that no submarine will be seen on the surface on the 20th.

In a signal timed 2259 C/19, the Capt. S.1 informed the submarines listed above of the following; My 2059 C/19. It is important that error in longitude should be reduced to a minimum. HMS Thrasher is to obtain accurate land fix at dawn, dive to position ordered to replace by 1200 hours or earlier if possible and then establish communication by SS/T to enable the other submarines to adjust their position. (19)

20 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 0401 C/20, the Capt. S.1, sent the following orders to HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); My 2059 C/19 shift patrol line 22 miles from 267°. My 2205 C/19 for 145° read 134°. HMS Thorn conform.

In a follow up signal timed 0455 C/20 sent by the Capt. S.1 to HMS Thorn, HMS Una and HMS P 31 and repeated to the other submarines the following orders were given; My 0401 C/20, HMS Thorn patrol along a line between positions 37°22'N, 20°15'E and 36°50'N, 20°59'E. HMS Una and HMS P 31 return to position 37°55'N, 19° 35'E and then steer 134°.

In a signal timed 1211 C/20, the following orders were sent by Capt. S.1; HMS Thorn, HMS Una, HMS P 31, HMS P 34 and HMS P 35 return to Alexandria. Expected in accordance with previous orders. HMS Thrasher return to patrol area through 37°56'N, 20°03'E. (20)

21 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2135 C/21, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the four submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Area 33°00'N, 47°00'E, 23°14'E - 310° - 65 miles is not to be crossed during night 22nd/23rd. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) pass to southward of area. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passing to the south and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to the north of Gavdo Island. (20)

23 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1117 C/23, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Proceed to following positions from whence you are expected end of searched channel at the approx. time given but submarines are not to arrive off the boom after 2030 hours or before 0730 hours. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) through position 32°19'N, 29°08'E to arrive at (the end of the searched channel) 1401 C/27. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) through position 31°39'N, 29°02'E to arrive at 0459 C/28. HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) through position 31°53'N, 28°55'E to arrive at 0801 C/28. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) through position 32°01'N, 28°59'E to arrive at 0501 C/29. (20)
24 Jun 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) makes a torpedo attack on German U-boat U-77 south of Crete in position 34°20'N, 24°09'E. All torpedoes fired however missed their target.
It was however thought that the target had been sunk but this was not the case.

The Germans reported hearing two heavy detonations at 0708 B/24 in quadrant CO 5365 which is centred in position 34°21'N, 24°18'E [note the one hour time zone difference]. The Germans did not know the cause of these explosions but suspected they had been been attacked by an Allied submarine although no torpedo tracks had been sighted. U-77 was shaken but sustained no damage.
0510 C/24 - Dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch closed up.
0600 C/24 - Commenced to patrol at periscope depth.
0735 C/24 - Heard HE bearing 045°.
0740 C/24 - Sighted a German U-boat bearing 060° at long range. Enemy course was 140°, speed 10 knots by Asdic. Started attack.
0800 C/24 - In position 34°20'N, 24°09'E fired four torpedoes from 7000 yards.

0807 C/24 - Heard a loud explosion. HE of the target stopped immediately. Nearly two minutes later a second loud explosion was heard. This was thought to be a internal explosion on board the U-boat. It is thought the U-boat had been hit and sunk.
1255 C/24 - Went to 80 feet and commenced to reload the torpedo tubes.
1500 C/24 - The reload was completed. Came to periscope depth.
2158 C/24 - Surfaced and proceeded on course 117° at 8 knots. (21)

27 Jun 1942 At 1500 C/27, in approximate position 32°31'N, 28°32'E, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a German U-boat at a range of 9000 yards. P 34 closed submerged at high speed but the U-boat dived before a favourable attack position could be obtained.

However, there were no U-boat in this area (U-372 and U-453 were more than 60 miles to the south). This may well have been HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) which was also returning to Alexandria. (17)
28 Jun 1942 At 1030 C/28, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Alexandria. (17)
29 Jun 1942 At 2045 C/29, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Alexandria for Haifa.
For the daily positions of HMS P 34 during this passage see the map below.
3 Jul 1942 At 1515 C/3, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at Haifa from Alexandria. (22)

17 Jul 1942 At 2000 C/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Haifa for Malta. While en-route she was ordered to patrol to the west of Crete making this her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean).

22 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1744 C/22, the Senior Officer Submarines Alexandria, sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Patrol in the vicinity of position 34°10'N, 23°30'E until 0600 C/24 then proceed through positions 35°34'N, 23°20'E, 36°50'N, 21°24'E thence through position 35°21'N, 16°19'E to arrive at last position noon/29. (23)

23 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1852 C/23, the Senior Officer Submarines Alexandria, sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); My 1744 C/22. Cancel movement ordered after 0600 C/24. Patrol in the vicinity of position 34°20'N, 22°10'E.

In a signal timed 2000 C/23, the Senior Officer Submarines Alexandria, sent the following info to HMS P 34; At 1630 C/23, aircraft reported three merchant ships and one destroyer in position 35°40'N, 23°25'E on course 180° at 10 knots.

This most likely referred to the Italian transports Città di Alessandria (Italian, 2498 GRT, built 1930) and Città di Savona (Italian, 2500 GRT, built 1930) which were en-route from Suda Bay (departed 0830 B/23-0908 B/23) to Tobruk (arrived 1640 B/24-1700 B/24). They were escorted by the German destroyer ZG 3 / Hermes and the Italian torpedo boat Saggittario.

The two escorts did not enter harbour but turned back, the German destroyer returning to Suda while Sagittario was ordered to reinforce the escort of the Italian tanker Rondine (6468 GRT, built 1924) screened by the torpedo boat Lince (they had sailed from Suda at 2300 B/23. Sagittario met them at 2355 B/25, and they anchored in Tobruk at 1120 B/25-1140 B/25.

In a follow up signal from the Senior Officer Submarines Alexandria timed 2019 C/23 the following orders were passed to ; My 2000 C/23. Aircraft searching from 2300 C/23 to 0400 C/24 using homing procedure on 320 K/cs, dropping flares over enemy every hour and half hour from 0100 B/24 [corrupt group] at about 0030 B/24. Aircraft call sign K Z 7 suffixed C J or S. (23)

24 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1000 C/24, the Senior Officer Submarines Alexandria, informed HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) that an enemy transport and an escorting destroyer had been sighted in the early morning hours in position 35°15'N, 23°25'E on course 175°, speed 10 knots.

This signal was repeated to all submarines on patrol. [HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) and RHS Nereus (Lt.Cdr. A. Rallis, HN), though none would be in a position to intercept.]

This signal most likely referred to the tanker Rondine (Italian, 6468 GRT, built 1924) that had left Suda Bay around 2350 B/23 for Tobruk where she arrived around 1200 B/25. She was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Lince which was reinforced by the Italian torpedo boat Saggitario around 2100 C/24. (24)

24 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 2322 C/24, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta, sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); My 1852 C/23. Leave patrol position A.M./26 and proceed direct through position 35°21'N 16°19'E to arrive noon/29. (23)
31 Jul 1942 At 1230 B/31, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 0955 C/31. (25)
Convoy WS 21S and the concentration of the escort forces

Convoy WS 21S departed the Clyde on 2 August 1942. The convoy was made up of the following ships; American freighters; Almeria Lykes (7773 GRT, built 1940), Santa Elisa (8379 GRT, built 1941), British freighters; Brisbane Star (12791 GRT, built 1937), Clan Ferguson (7347 GRT, built 1938), Deucalion (7516 GRT, built 1930), Dorset (10624 GRT, built 1934), Empire Hope (12688 GRT, built 1941), Glenorchy (8982 GRT, built 1939), Melbourne Star (11076 GRT, built 1936), Port Chalmers (8535 GRT, built 1933), Rochester Castle (7795 GRT, built 1937), Waimarama (12843 GRT, built 1938), Wairangi (12436 GRT, built 1935), and the American tanker; Ohio (9264 GRT, built 1940).

These ships were escorted by light cruisers HMS Nigeria (Capt. S.H. Paton, RN, flying the flag of the Rear-Admiral 10th C.S., H.M. Burrough, CB, DSO, RN), HMS Kenya (Capt. A.S. Russell, RN) and the destroyers HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN), HMS Venomous (Cdr. H.W. Falcon-Stewart, RN), HMS Wolverine (Lt.Cdr. P.W. Gretton, OBE, DSC, RN), HMS Malcolm (A/Cdr. A.B. Russell, RN), HMS Amazon (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy) Lord Teynham, RN), HMS Derwent (Cdr. R.H. Wright, DSC, RN) and HMS Zetland (Lt. J.V. Wilkinson, RN).

A cover force made up of departed Scapa Flow on the same day. This force was made up of the battleships HMS Nelson (Capt. H.B. Jacomb, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral E.N. Syfret, CB, RN) and HMS Rodney (Capt. J.W. Rivett-Carnac, DSC, RN). They were escorted by the destroyers HMS Ashanti (Cdr. R.G. Onslow, DSO, RN), HMS Eskimo (Cdr. E.G. Le Geyt, RN), HMS Somali (Cdr. E.N.V. Currey, DSC, RN), HMS Tartar (Cdr. St.J.R.J. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN), HMS Pathfinder (Cdr. E.A. Gibbs, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Penn (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, RN) and HMS Quentin (Lt.Cdr. A.H.P. Noble, DSC, RN). They were to rendez-vous with convoy WS 21S at sea on 3 August. HMS Penn was delayed by a defect and after topping off with fuel at Moville, Northern Ireland overtook the force and joined at sea.

The aircraft carrier HMS Victorious (Capt. H.C. Bovell, CBE, RN, flying the flag of Rear Admiral A.L.St.G. Lyster, CB, CVO, DSO, RN) and the light cruiser HMS Sirius (Capt. P.W.B. Brooking, RN) meanwhile had already left Scapa Flow on 31 July 1941 to rendez-vous with the convoy. They were escorted by the destroyers HMS Intrepid (Cdr. C.A.deW. Kitcat, RN), HMS Icarus (Lt.Cdr. C.D. Maud, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. C.H. Campbell, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Foresight (Lt.Cdr. R.A. Fell, RN). These ships were joined at sea on 1 August 1942 by the aircraft carrier HMS Argus (Capt. G.T. Philip, RN), loaded with spare fighter aircraft for the operation, and her two escorts the destroyers HMS Buxton (Lt.Cdr. I.J. Tyson, RD, RNR) and HMS Sardonyx (Lt.Cdr. A.F.C. Gray, RNR). HMS Argus and her two escorting destroyers had departed the Clyde on 31 July. HMS Buxton later split off and proceeded towards Canada and HMS Sardonyx proceeded to Londonderry.

The last ships to take part in the operation to depart the U.K. (Clyde around midnight during the night of 4/5 August) were the aircraft carrier HMS Furious (Capt. T.O. Bulteel, RN), loaded with Hurricane fighters for Malta, and her escorts, the light cruiser HMS Manchester (Capt. H. Drew, DSC, RN) and the Polish destroyer ORP Blyscawica (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) L. Lichodziejewski). They were joined at sea, around dawn, by HMS Sardonyx coming from Londonderry. The destroyers parted company around midnight during the night of 5/6 August. They arrived at Londonderry on 7 August. HMS Furious and HMS Manchester then joined convoy WS 21S around midnight of the next night but HMS Manchester parted company shortly afterwards to proceed ahead of the convoy and fuel at Gibraltar.

On 1 August 1942 the aircraft carrier HMS Indomitable (Capt. T.H. Troubridge, RN), light cruiser HMS Phoebe (Capt. C.P. Frend, RN) and the destroyers HMS Laforey (Capt. R.M.J. Hutton, RN), HMS Lightning (Cdr. H.G. Walters, DSC, RN) and HMS Lookout (Lt.Cdr. A.G. Forman, DSC, RN) departed Freetown to proceed to a rendez-vous position off the Azores.

On 5 August 1942, the aircraft carrier HMS Eagle (Capt. L.D. Mackintosh, DSC, RN), light cruiser HMS Charybdis (Capt. G.A.W. Voelcker, RN) and the the destroyers HMS Wrestler (Lt. R.W.B. Lacon, DSC, RN), HMS Westcott (Cdr. I.H. Bockett-Pugh, DSO, RN) and HMS Vansittart (Lt.Cdr. T. Johnston, RN) departed Gibraltar also to the rendezvous position off the Azores.

The convoy conducted maneuvering and AA exercises with the escorts between the Azores and Gibraltar during the period of 6 to 9 August. (Operation Berserk). Also dummy air attacks were carried out by aircraft from the carriers.
Passage of the Straits of Gibraltar and organization of escort forces.
The convoy then passed the Straits of Gibraltar during the night of 9/10 August 1942 in dense fog but despite this the convoy was detected by German and Italian spies and reported.

After passing the Straits of Gibraltar the convoy was organized as follows; The actual convoy was protected a large force of warships until the whole force would split up before entering the Sicilian narrows after which ‘Force X’ under command of Rear-Admiral Sir H.M. Burrough, CB, DSO, RN was to accompany the convoy to the approaches to Malta where they would be met by the Malta Minesweeping Flotilla, which was then to sweep the convoy into the harbour. Force X was made up of the following ships: Licht cruisers: HMS Nigeria (flagship), HMS Kenya,, HMS Manchester. AA cruiser: HMS Cairo (A/Capt. C.C. Hardy, DSO, RN). Destroyers: HMS Ashanti, HMS Fury, HMS Foresight, HMS Icarus, HMS Intrepid, HMS Pathfinder and HMS Penn. Escort destroyers: HMS Derwent, HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN), HMS Bramham (Lt. E.F. Baines, RN), HMS Ledbury (Lt.Cdr. R.P. Hill, RN) and HMS Wilton (Lt. A.P. Northey, RN). Also the rescue tug HMRT Jaunty was to be part of this force.

After the escort was to be split up cover was provided by ‘Force Z’ under Vice-Admiral E.N. Syfret, CB, RN. This force was made up of the following ships: Battleships: HMS Nelson (flagship) and HMS Rodney. Aircraft carriers: HMS Victorious, HMS Indomitable and HMS Eagle. Light cruisers: HMS Phoebe, HMS Sirius and HMS Charybdis. Destroyers: HMS Laforey, HMS Lightning, HMS Lookout, HMS Eskimo, HMS Somali, HMS Tartar, HMS Quentin, HMS Ithuriel (Lt.Cdr. D.H. Maitland-Makgill-Crichton, DSC, RN), HMS Antelope (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Sinclair), HMS Wishart and HMS Vansittart. Escort destroyer: HMS Zetland. Also attached were the aircraft carrier HMS Furious (for Operation Bellows, the launching of Hurricane fighters for Malta. HMS Furious only carried four Albacore aircraft for A/S searches after the Hurricanes had been launched) and the ‘spare’ destroyers HMS Keppel (Cdr. J.E. Broome, RN), HMS Malcolm, HMS Venomous, HMS Vidette (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Walmsley, DSC, RN), HMS Westcott, HMS Wolverine, HMS Wrestler and HMS Amazon. These ‘spare’ destroyers were to take the place of destroyers in the screen ‘Force Z’ if needed, escort HMS Furious during her return passage to Gibraltar after she had completed Operation Bellows and / or strengthen the escort of ‘Force R’.

Then there was also ‘Force R’, the fuelling force. This force was made up of the following ships: Corvettes: HMS Jonquil (Lt.Cdr. R.E.H. Partington, RD, RNR), HMS Spiraea (Lt.Cdr. R.S. Miller, DSC, RNR), HMS Geranium (T/Lt. A. Foxall, RNR) and HMS Coltsfoot (T/Lt. the Hon. W.K. Rous, RNVR). Rescue tug: HMS Salvonia. RFA tankers: RFA Brown Ranger (3417 GRT, built 1941, Master D.B.C. Ralph) and RFA Dingledale (8145 GRT, built 1941, Master R.T. Duthie).

Before we give an account of the passage of the main convoy we will now first describe the operations taking place in the Eastern Mediterranean (Operations MG 3 and MG 4), the launching of the Hurricane fighters for Malta by HMS Furious (Operation Bellows) and the return convoy from Malta (Operation Ascendant) as well as on submarine operations / dispositions.
Diversion in the Eastern Mediterranean.

As part of the plan for Operation Pedestal the Mediterranean Fleet had to carry out a diversion in the Eastern part of the Mediterranean. Before we go to the operations in the Western Mediterranean we will first give an account of the events in the Eastern Mediterranean.

It was at this time not possible to sent any supplies from Egypt to Malta as all supplies and forces were much needed for the upcoming land battle at El Alamein it was agreed that ‘a dummy convoy’ would be sent towards Malta with the object of preventing the enemy to direct the full weight of their air and naval power towards the Western Mediterranean.

In the evening of 10 August 1942 a ‘convoy’ (MG 3) of three merchant ships departed Port Said escorted by three cruisers and ten destroyers. Next morning one more merchant ship departed Haifa escorted by two cruisers and five destroyers. The two forces joined that day (the 11th) and then turned back dispersing during the night. The Italian fleet however did not go to sea to attack ‘the bait’.

The forces taking part in this operation were: From Port Said: Merchant vessels City of Edinburgh (8036 GRT, built 1938), City of Lincoln (8039 GRT, built 1938) and City of Pretoria (8049 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the light cruisers HMS Arethusa (Capt. A.C. Chapman, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral I.G. Glennie, RN), HMS Euryalus (Capt. E.W. Bush, DSO, DSC, RN), the AA cruiser HMS Coventry (Capt. R.J.R. Dendy, RN) and the destroyers HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN), HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Pakenham (Capt. E.B.K. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Paladin (Cdr. A.F. Pugsley, RN) and the escort destroyers HMS Dulverton (Lt.Cdr. W.N. Petch, OBE, RN), HMS Hurworth (Lt.Cdr. J.T.B. Birch, RN), HMS Eridge (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, DSC, RN), HMS Hursley (Lt. W.J.P. Church, DSC, RN), HMS Beaufort (Lt.Cdr. S.O’G Roche, RN) and HMS Belvoir (Lt. J.F.D. Bush, DSC and Bar, RN).

From Haifa: Merchant vessel Ajax (7797 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the light cruisers HMS Cleopatra (Capt. G. Grantham, DSO, RN, flagship of Rear-Admiral P.L. Vian, KBE, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Dido (Capt. H.W.U. McCall, RN), the destroyers HMS Sikh (Capt. St.J. A. Micklethwait, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Zulu (Cdr. R.T. White, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Javelin (Cdr. G.E. Fardell, RN) and the escort destroyers HMS Tetcott (Lt. H.R. Rycroft, RN) and HMS Croome (Lt.Cdr. R.C. Egan, RN).

After dark on 11 August 1942 the force turned back and the City of Pretoria returned to Port Said escorted by HMS Eridge and HMS Hursley. The City of Edinburgh, escorted by HMS Beaufort and HMS Belvoir proceeded to Haifa. The City of Lincoln escorted by HMS Dulverton and HMS Hurworth proceeded to Beirut and finally the Ajax, escorted by HMS Tetcott and HMS Croome returned to Haifa. HMS Dido had to return to Port Said with hull defects. She was escorted by HMS Pakenham, HMS Paladin and HMS Jervis.

HMS Cleopatra, HMS Arethusa, HMS Sikh, HMS Zulu, HMS Javelin and HMS Kelvin then proceeded to carry out another diversion (Operation MG 4). They bombarded Rhodes harbour and the Alliotti Flour Mills during the night of 12/13 August but did little damage. On the way back HMS Javelin attacked a submarine contact in position 34°45’N, 31°04’E between 0654 and 0804 hours. She reported that there was no doubt that the submarine was sunk but no Axis submarines were operating in this area so the attack must have been bogus. This force returned to Haifa at 1900 B/13.
Operation Bellows.

During operation Bellows, the aircraft carrier HMS Furious, started 37 Spitfire which were to proceed to Malta, when south of the Balearic Islands. The Admiralty had decided to carry out this operation at the same time as Operation Pedestal.

HMS Furious remained with the convoy until 1200 B/11. She then launched the Spitfires for Malta in 5 batches between 1230 and 1515 hours. During these flying off operations she acted independently with the destroyers HMS Lookout and HMS Lightning. After having launched the last batch of Spitfires she briefly re-joined to convoy until around 1700 hours when she split off and set course for Gibraltar escorted by the destroyers HMS Malcolm, HMS Wolverine and HMS Wrestler. These were joined shortly afterwards by HMS Keppel and HMS Venomous.

Around 0100 B/12, HMS Wolverine, rammed and sank the Italian submarine Dagabur which was trying to attack HMS Furious. Around 0200 hours, HMS Wolverine reported that she was stopped due to the damage she had sustained in the ramming. HMS Malcolm was detached to assist her.

At 1530 B/12, the destroyer HMS Vidette joined the screen. The force then entered Gibraltar Bay around 1930 B/12. The damaged HMS Wolverine arrived at Gibraltar at 1230 B/13 followed by HMS Malcolm around 1530 B/13.
Operation Ascendant

On 10 August 1942 the empty transports Troilus (7648 GRT, built 1921) and Orari (10107 GRT, built 1931) departed Malta after dark for Gibraltar. They were escorted by the destroyer HMS Matchless (Lt.Cdr. J. Mowlam, RN) and the escort destroyer HMS Badsworth (Lt. G.T.S. Gray, DSC, RN). They first proceeded to the south of Lampedusa, then hugged the Tunisian coast as far as Galita Island. Near Cape Bon they encountered the Italian destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello that was laying a minefield. They had a brief gunfight but this was soon ended as both sides were thinking the enemy was Vichy-French. The remained of the passage to Gibraltar was uneventful and the convoy arrived at Gibraltar shortly before noon on 14 August 1942.

Submarine operations / dispositions. Eight submarines took part in the operation; these were HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN), HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN). Two of these were to carry out normal dived patrol to the north of Sicily, one off Palermo, the other off Milazzo which is futher to the east. The other six submarines were given alternative patrol lines south of Pantelleria, one od which they were to take up at dawn on 13 August 1942, according to the movements of enemy surface ships that might threathen the convoy from the westward. When the convoy had passed the patrol line, which it should have done by that time, the submarines were to proceed on the surface parallel to the convoy as a screen and to dive away clear of the convoy at noon. It was expressly intended that they should be seen on the surface and reported by enemy aircraft in order to deter enemy warships from attacking the convoy.

Enemy warships did go to sea but as soon as it was clear that the enemy ships could not reach the convoy the sunmarines were ordered to dive and retire. These six sumarines had no contact with the enemy. One of the the two submarines off the north coast of Sicily, HMS P 42, managed to torpedo two Italian cruisers near Stromboli on the morning of 13 August 1942.
Now we return to the main convoy to Malta.
Passage eastwards after passing the Straits of Gibraltar.
10 and 11 August 1942.

After passing through the Straits of Gibraltar in the early hours of 10 August 1942, in dense fog, the convoy was first sighted by an Italian passenger aircraft, which sighted the convoy in the afternoon of the same day. German reconnaissance aircraft started shadowing the convoy from dawn on the 11th, and thereafter they or Italian aircraft kept the convoy under continuous observation, despite the effort of the fighters from the carriers to shoot them down or drive them off. At 1315 hours, HMS Eagle, was hit an sunk by torpedoes from the German submarine U-73 which had penetrated the destroyer screen. At that moment there were thirteen destroyers in the screen, the remainder was away from the main convoy, escorting HMS Furious during the flying off operations of the Hurricane fighters for Malta or oiling from and screening ‘Force R’ which was several miles away. Between 1430 B/10 and and 2030 B/11 no less then three cruisers and twenty-four destroyers fuelled from the two oilers of ‘Force R’.

At the time of the torpedoing of HMS Eagle the convoy was in four columns, zigzagging at 13 knots, with the heavy ships stationed close round it and a destroyer screen ahead. HMS Eagle was on the starboard quarter of the convoy. She was hit on her starboard side by four torpedoes which had dived through the destroyer screen and the convoy columns undetected and then torpedoed and sank the Eagle in position 38°05’N, 03°02’E (Another source gives 03°12’E but this might be a typo). The carrier sank quickly in about 8 minutes, 926 of her crew, including the Commanding Officer, were rescued by the destroyers HMS Laforey and HMS Lookout and the rescue tug HMS Jaunty. At the time of her sinking, HMS Eagle had four aircraft on patrol. These landed on the other carriers. All other aircraft were lost with the ship. The survivors picked up were later transferred to the destroyers HMS Keppel, HMS Malcolm and HMS Venomous that were to escort HMS Furious back to Gibraltar. The tug HMS Jaunty that had been involved in picking up survivors was never able to rejoin the convoy due to her slow speed.

Late in the afternoon air attacks were expected so Vice-Admiral Syfret ordered the destroyer to form an all-round screen. Indeed the air attacks started around sunset, 2045 hours. The last destroyers had just returned from oiling from ‘Force R’. The enemy aircraft that were attacking were 36 German bombers and torpedo aircraft, Ju 88’s and He 111’s, most of which attacked the convoy but a few attacked ‘Force R’ to the southward. The Junkers arrived first, diving down from 8000 feet to 2000 / 3000 feet to drop their bombs. They claimed to have hit an aircraft carrier and one of the merchant ships. Then the Heinkels attacked, they claimed to have torpedoed a cruiser but during the attacks no ship was hit. The British fighter cover was unable to attack / find the enemy in the failing light. Four enemy aircraft were claimed shot down by the ships AA fire but it appears only two JU 88’s were in fact shot down.
12 August 1942

At 0915 B/12 another wave of German aircraft attacked the convoy. Some twenty or more JU 88’s approached the convoy out of the sun ahead. They were intercepted by fighters about 25 miles from the convoy. About a dozen got through to the convoy, making high-level or shallow dive-bombing attacks individually but without any result. Eight German aircraft were claimed to be shot down by the fighters and two more by AA guns from the ships. The fighters meanwhile were also busy dealng with shadowers, three of which are claimed to have been shot down before the morning attack. Around this time destroyers were also busy with numerous submarine contact which were attacked by depth charges.
Around noon the enemy launched heavy air attacks from the Sardinian airfields. Seventy aircraft approached which were heavily escorted by fighters. They attacked in stages and employed new methods.

First ten Italian torpedo-bombers were each to drop some sort of circling torpedo or mine a few hundred yards ahead of the British force, while eight fighter bombers made dive-bombing and machine-gun attacks. The object at this stage was clearly to dislocate the formation of the force and to draw anti-aircraft fire, making the ships more vulnerable to a torpedo attack which soon followed with over forty aircraft. They attacked in two groups, one on either bow of the convoy. The next stage was a shallow dive-bombing attack by German aircraft, after which two Italian Reggiane 2001 fighters, each with a single heavy armour-piercing bomb were to dive bomb on one of the aircraft carriers, whilst yet another new form of attack was to be employed against the other carrier, but defects in the weapon prevented this attack from taking place.

The enemy attack went according to plan besides that the torpedo attack was only made half an our after the ‘mines’ were dropped instead of five minutes. British fighters met the minelaying aircraft, they shot down one of them as they approached. The remaining nine aircraft dropped their ‘mines’ at 1215 hours in the path of the force, which turned to avoid the danger. The mines were heard to explode several minutes later. Only three of the fighter-bombers of this stage of the attack appear to have reached as far the screen, but HMS Lightning had a narrow escape from their bombs.

The torpedo-aircraft appeared at 1245 hours. Their number were brought down a bit due to British fighters. The remaining aircraft, estimated at 25 to 30 machines, attacked from the port bow, port beam and starboard quarter. They dropped their torpedoes well outside the screen some 8000 yards from the merchant ships which they had been ordered to attack. The force turned 45° to port and then back to starboard to avoid the attack.

In the next stage, around 1318 hours, the German bombing attack, the enemy scored their one success. These aircraft were also intercepted on their way in but about a dozen of about twenty aircraft came through. They crossed the convoy from starboard to port and then dived to 3000 feet. They managed to damage the transport Deucalion which was leading the port wing column. More bombs fell close to several other ships.

Finally, at 1345 hours, the two Reggiane fighters approached HMS Victorious as if to land on. They looked like Hurricanes and HMS Victorious was at that time engaged in landing her own fighters. They managed to drop their bombs and one hit the flight deck amidships. Fortunately the bomb broke up without exploding. By the time HMS Victorious could open fire both fighters were out of range.

The Deucalion could no longer keep up with the convoy and was ordered to follow the inshore route along the Tunisian coast escorted by HMS Bramham. Two bombers found these ships late in the afternoon, but their bombs missed. At 1940 hours, however, near the Cani Rocks, two torpedo aircraft attacked and a torpedo hit the Deucalion. She caught fire and eventually blew up.

The convoy passed some 20 miles north of Galita Island and spent the afternoon avoiding enemy submarines which were known to be concentrated in these waters. There were innumerable reports of sightings and Asdic contacts and at least two submarines proved dangerous. At 1616 hours, HMS Pathfinder and HMS Zetland attacked one on the port bow of the convoy and hunted her until the convoy was out of reach. HMS Ithuriel, stationed on the quarter, then attacked, forced the enemy to surface and finally rammed her. She proved to be the Italian submarine Cobalto.
