Meanwhile HMS Tartar, on the starboard quarter, saw six torpedoes fired at close range at 1640 hours, and the next destroyer in the screen, HMS Lookout sighted a periscope. Together they attacked the submarine, continuing until it was no longer dangerous. There was no evidence this submarine was sunk.

At 1750 hours, HMS Ithuriel, which was on her way back to the convoy after sinking the Italian submarine Cobalto was attacked by a few dive-bombers, when still a dozen miles astern of the convoy. At this time the convoy came under attack by aircraft stationed on Sicily. This force numbered nearly 100 aircraft. Ju.87 dive-bombers as well as Ju.88’s and SM-79’s all with a strong escort of fighters. The enemy started attacking at 1835 hours, the bombers attacking from both ahead and astern which last was the direction of the sun. The torpedo aircraft came from ahead to attack on the starboard bow and beam of the convoy.

The Italian SM-79’s torpedo bombers dropped their torpedoes from ranges of about 3000 yards outside the destroyer screen, and once again the convoy turned away to avoid them. However the destroyer HMS Foresight was hit by a torpedo and disabled. The bombers chose HMS Indomitable as their main target. She was astern of HMS Rodney at the time on the port quarter of the convoy. Four Ju.88’s and eight Ju.87’s came suddenly out of the sun and dived steeply towards HMS Indomitable from astern. Some of the Ju.87 came down to 1000 feet and the carrier received three hits and her flight deck was put out of action. Her airborne fighters eventually had to land on HMS Victorious. HMS Rodney meanwhile had a narrow escape when a bomber attacked from ahead. One enemy aircraft was claimed to have been shot down by AA fire from the ships while the fighters claimed nine more although there were about twice as much enemy fighters in the air then British.

HMS Tartar took the damaged HMS Foresight in tow and proceeded westward for Gibraltar. Next day, as they were shadowed by enemy aircraft, and enemy submarines were known to be in the area, it was decided to scuttle the cripple before both ships might be lost. HMS Tartar then torpedoed HMS Foresight a few miles from Galita Island.
Passage through the narrows, 12-13 August 1942, and the loss off HMS Manchester.

These last air attacks took place about 20 nautical miles west of the Skerki Channel and at 1900 hours, when the attacks were clearly over, Vice-Admiral Syfret turned away with ‘Force Z’. It was now up to Rear-Admiral Burrough with ‘Force X’ to take the convoy to Malta.

At 2000 hours, when the convoy was changing it’s formation from four to two columns, the convoy was attacked by Italian submarines. The submarine Dessiè attacked a freighter with four torpedoes and claimed three hits. The sound of the torpedo hits was however not caused by her attack but by an attack by the Axum which hit three ships, HMS Nigeria, HMS Cairo and the tanker Ohio.

HMS Nigeria had to turn back to make for Gibraltar escorted by the escort destroyers HMS Derwent, HMS Wilton and HMS Bicester. Rear-Admiral Burrough transferred his flag to the destroyer HMS Ashanti. The stern of HMS Cairo had been blown off and she had to be sunk as she was beyond salvage with both engines also out of action. She was scuttled by HMS Pathfinder. The Ohio meanwhile managed to struggle on.

At this time the convoy was still trying to form up the the submarine attacks messed things up and right at thus time the convoy was once more attacked from the air in the growing dusk at 2030 hours. About 20 German aircraft, Ju-88’s made dive bombing and torpedo attacks, hitting the Empire Hope with a bomb and the Clan Ferguson and Brisbane Star with torpedoes. The first of these ships had to be sunk (by HMS Bramham, the second blew up but the last eventually reached Malta. Soon after this attack, at 2111 hours, HMS Kenya was torpedoed by the Italian submarine Alagi. She was able to evade three of the four torpedoes but was hit in the bow by the fouth. She was however able to remain with the convoy.

The situation was then as follows. HMS Kenya and HMS Manchester with two merchant ships, and with the minesweeping destroyers HMS Intrepid, HMS Icarus and HMS Fury sweeping ahead, had passed the Skerki Channel and were steering to pass Zembra Island on the way to Cape Bon. HMS Ashanti, with Rear-Admiral Burrough on board was fast overhauling these ships. The other two destroyers HMS Pathfinder, HMS Penn and the escort destroyer HMS Ledbury, were rounding up the remaining nine merchant ships. The escort destroyer HMS Bramham was also catching up after having escorted the single Deucalion until she sank.

On learing about the fate of HMS Nigeria and HMS Cairo, Vice-Admiral Syfret detached HMS Charybdis, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali to reinforce Rear-Admiral Burrough. It would take these ships several hourse to catch up with the convoy.

The main body of the convoy passed Cape Bon around midnight. Fourty minutes later enemy Motor Torpedo Boats appeared and started to attack. Their first victim was HMS Manchester which was torpedoed at 0120 B/13 by the Italian MS 16 or MS 22. She had to be scuttled by her own crew. Many of her ships company landed in Tunisia and were interned by the Vichy-French but about 300 were picked up by destroyers (first by HMS Pathfinder, and later by HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali. These last two destoyers then set off towards Gibraltar.)

Four and possibly five of the merchant ships were also hit by the Motor Torpedo Boats. These were the Wairangi, Rochester Castle, Almeria Lykes, Santa Elisa and probably the Glenorchy. They were attacked between 0315 and 0430 hours about 15 nautical miles south-east of Kelibia whilst taking a short cut to overhaul the main body of the convoy. Four were lost, only the Rochester Castle survived and she managed to catch up with the main body of the convoy at 0530 hours. The Glenorchy was sunk by the Italian MS 31, the other four, of which the Rochester Castle survived as mentioned earlier, were hit by the German S 30 and S 36 as well as the Italian MAS 554 and MAS 557.

Shortly before 0530 hours HMS Charybdis, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali had joined the main body of the convoy making the force now two cruisers and seven destroyers with the transports Rochester Castle, Waimarama and Melbourne Star. The damaged tanker Ohio was slowly catching up. With her was the escort destroyer HMS Ledbury. Astern of the main body was the Port Chalmers escorted by the destroyer HMS Penn and the escort destroyer HMS Bramham. The destroyers recued the crew of the Santa Elisa when the passed by the abandoned ship which was afterwards finished off by a German bomber. The Dorset was proceeding without escort and lastly the damaged Brisbane Star was still keeping close to the Tunisian coast independently, intending to steer towards Malta after nightfall.

At 0730 hours, Rear-Admiral Burrough, sent back HMS Tartar and HMS Somali to Kelibia to assist HMS Manchester and then go to Gibraltar. When they arrived they found out that the Manchester had been scuttled several hours earlier so they rescued those of her crew that had not reached the shore yet and then made off to Gibraltar as ordered. Besides crew of the Manchester they also picked up survivors from the Almeria Lykes and Wairangi.

The next encounter with the enemy was an air attack on the main body of the convoy at 0800 hours by German bombers. About 12 Ju.88’s made a shallow diving attack coming down from 6000 feet to 2000 feet to drop their bombs. Two dived on the Waimarama hitting her several times and she blew up immediately, one of the bombers was even destroyed in the explosion. HMS Ledbury saved some of her crew out of the blazing sea. At 0925 hours, when the Ohio, Port Chalmers and Dorset where with the main body again, a few Ju.87’s escorted by Italian fighters attacked. They dived down to 1500 to 1000 feet. HMS Kenya leading the port column, and the Ohio last ship but one in the starboard column, had narrow escapes. One of the enemy aircraft crashed on board the Ohio just after having released it’s bomb after being damaged by gunfire from the Ohio and HMS Ashanti. Another aircraft was claimed to have been shot down by fighters from Malta that had been patrolling overhead since daybreak.
Arrivals at Malta 13-15 August 1942.

At 1050 hours, about 20 bombers, mostly Ju.88’s with a few Ju.87’s, came in to attack. Target was the Ohio and she received four or five near misses and her engines were disabled. At the same time the Rochester Castle in the port column was near-missed and set on fire but she continued with the convoy. The Dorset which was astern of her was hit and stopped. The convoy went on leaving the Dorset behind with the Ohio and two destroyers.

At 1125 hours the last air attack on the main body took place. Five Italian SM.79’s attacked with torpedoes and almost hit the Port Chalmers as the torpedo got stuck in the paravane. Further attacks on the main body were held of by fighters from Malta. At 1430 hours, four minesweepers from Malta joined the main body of the convoy, these were HMS Speedy (Lt.Cdr. A.E. Doran, RN, with the group’s commander A/Cdr. H.J.A.S. Jerome, RN on board), HMS Hebe, HMS Rye and HMS Heyte. Also with them were seven Motor Launches; ML 121, ML 126, ML 134, ML 135, ML 168, ML 459 and ML 462. HMS Rye and two of the ML’s were sent towards the damaged Ohio which was ‘vital for Malta’, according to A/Cdr. Jerome.

At 1600 hours, Rear-Admiral Burrough, set course to the west with his two cruisers and with five destroyers. The Port Chalmers, Melbourne Star and Rochester Castle arrived in Grand Harbour around 1800 hours with the force of A/Cdr. Jerome. The Rochester Castle was by that time very low in the water, she had just made it into port on time.

Out were still the Ohio, Dorset and the Brisbane Star. The valuable Ohio had been helpless with HMS Penn and HMS Bramham. When HMS Rye arrived at 1730 hours, HMS Penn took the Ohio in tow. Meanwhile HMS Bramham was sent to the Dorset but soon afterwards German bombers came again and the ships were attacked repeatedly until dark. Both merchantman were hit around 1900 hours and the Dorset sank.

At daylight on the 14th HMS Ledbury arrived to help bringing the Ohio to Malta. HMS Speedy also soon arrived on the scene with two ML’s. The rest of his force he had sent to search for the Brisbane Star. At 1045 hours, enemy aircraft made their last attempt, causing the parting of the tow. Fighter from Malta shot down two of the attackers. The tow was passed again and the slow procession went on and in the morning of the 15th the vital tanker finally reached Malta.

The Brisbane Star had by then also arrived. She left the Tunisian coast at dusk on the 13th. Aircraft had attacked her unsuccessfully and one of the attackers was shot down by a Beaufighter escort that had been sent from Malta. She arrived at Malta in the afternoon of the 14th.
Italian surface ships to operate against the convoy ?

The convoy had experienced the violence of the enemy in every shape except that of an attack by large surface ships. Yet Italian cruisers and destroyers had been at sea to intercept and attack it. Two light cruiser had left Cagliari in the evening of 11 August 1942 and the heavy cruisers Gorizia and Bolzano from Messina, and a light cruiser from Naples had sailed on the morning of the 12th. That evening reconnaissance aircraft reported one heavy and two light cruisers with eight destroyers about 80 nautical miles to the north of the western tip of Sicily and steering south. It would have been possible for this force to meet the convoy at dawn on the 13th so the shadowing aircraft was therefore ordered in plain language to illuminate and attack. This apparently influenced the Italians as they had limited air cover and they turned back at 0130 B/13 when near Cape San Vito. At 0140 hours the aircraft reported that it had dropped its bombs but no hits had been obtained. Similar orders were signalled, in plain language, to relief shadowers and to report the position of the enemy force to the benefit of imaginary Liberator bombers in case the Italians would change their minds and turn back. They however held on to the eastward.

The submarine HMS P 42 sighted them around 0800 B/13 off Stromboli and attacked with four torpedoes claiming two hits. She had in fact hit the heavy cruiser Bolzano which was able to proceed northwards and the light cruiser Muzio Attendolo which managed to reach Messina with her bows blown off. The other cruisers went to Naples. Following the attack P 42 was heavily depth charged by the destroyers but managed to escape.

In fact the following Italian ships had been at sea; heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trieste, Bolzano, light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia Raimondo Montecuccoli, Muzio Attendolo. They were escorted by eleven destroyers; Ascari, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Corsaro, Fuceliere, Geniere, Legionaro, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani, Grecale and Maestrale.
The return to Gibraltar.

The British ships returning to Gibraltar had better fortune. Having left the convoy off Malta in the afternoon of the 13th, they rounded Cape Bon around 0130 B/14 and from that point until past Zembra Island they successful ran the gauntled of E-boats laying in wait.

at 0450 B/14, near the Fratelli Rocks, a submarine fired torpedoes at HMS Ashanti from the surface. She was nearly rammed by HMS Kenya, which was next astern of the ‘flagship’ (Rear-Admiral Burrough was still in HMS Ashanti). The inevitable shadowers arrived soon after daylight to herald their air attacks that began at 0730 hours. They lasted until around 1315 hours. German bombers came in first with three attemps by a few Ju.88’s. This was followed by a more severe attack with about 30 bombers, Ju-88’s and Ju-87’s between 1030 and 1050 hours. An hour later 15 Savoia high-level bombers attacked followed until 1315 hours by torpedo-carrying Savoia’s. Around 20 aircraft attacking single or in pairs. Also aircraft are though to be laying mines ahead. Several ships were near missed, but no further damage was sustained. After these attacks the British were left alone and in the evening they joined ‘Force Z’.

Vice-Admiral Syfret had gone as far west as 01’E where he ordered the damaged carrier HMS Indomitable to proceed to Malta with HMS Rodney and a destroyer screen made up of HMS Ithuriel, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon, HMS Westcott, HMS Wishart and HMS Zetland. He then turned back to the east to make rendez-vous with Rear-Admiral Burrough. HMS Rodney, HMS Indomitable, HMS Ithuriel, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon, HMS Westcott, HMS Wishart and HMS Zetland arrived at Gibraltar in the evening of the 14th.

A few hours before they arrived the damaged HMS Nigeria and her escort had also entered port, as had HMS Tartar, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali. On her way back HMS Nigeria had been attacked by torpedo-bombers and a submarine but she had not been hit.
Conclusion.

Out of the fourteen ships that had sailed only five arrived ‘safe’ at Malta. This was not a very high score also given the very heavy escort that had been provided also taken in mind that an aircraft carrier, a light cruiser, an AA cruiser an a destroyer had been lost and two heavy cruiser had been damaged. But the convoy had to meet very heavy air attacks by over 150 bombers and 80 torpedo aircraft, all in the space of two days. Also these aircraft were protected by fighter in much greater strength that the carriers and Malta could provide. And there were also the enemy submarines and E-boats.

The spirit in which to operation was carried out appears in Vice-Admiral Syfret’s report: ‘ Tribute has been paid to the personnel of His Majesty’s Ships, both the officers and men will desire to give first place to the conduct, courage, and determination of the masters, officers, and men of the merchant ships. The steadfast manner in which these ships pressed on their way to Malta through all attacks, answering every maneuvering order like a well trained fleet unit, was a most inspiring sight. Many of these fine men and their ships were lost. But the memory of their conduct will remain an inspiration to all who were privileged to sail with them.
Movements of the Italian Fleet during operation Pedestal.

The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli (7th Cruiser Division, Divisional Admiral Alberto Da Zara) escorted by the destroyers Ascari, Alfredo Oriani (leader) and Vincenzo Gioberti (10th Destroyer Squadron), sailed from Cagliari at 1605 B/13-1625 B/13. They were sighted by HMS P 211 at 2020 B/13 in position 38°42'N, 10°29'E. They were attacked by HMS P 43 at 1840 B/13 and missed in position 38°46.5'N, 09°49'E. They arrived at Palermo at 0726 B/14-0826 B/14.

At 1910 B/14-2006 B/14, the squadron sailed from Palermo escorted by Alfredo Oriani, Ascari, Ugolino Vivaldi, Vincenzo Gioberti and Lanzerotto Malocello. The destroyer Premuda sailed from Trapani at 1745 B/14 to join them. After midnight, Vincenzo Gioberti had turn back due to defects and returned to Palermo at 0500 B/15.

At 0532 B/15, in position 190° - Punta Sciaccazza (Pantelleria) - 18 miles, The Italian squadron sighted two enemy cruisers and several destroyers of the Tribal and J/K/N-classes, bearing 220°, course 135°, 15 knots, at a range of 22000 metres. At a distance from them was a convoy consisting of a 12000-ton troop transport, a tanker and three merchant ships of about 6,000 tons, escorted by four Tribal-class destroyers. Immediately, Eugenio di Savoia catapulted a RO 43 seaplane and at 0536 B/14 (it later crashed at sea, the pilot was recovered by a MAS boat but the navigating officer was missing), Raimondo Montecuccoli also launched a RO 43 seaplane but it was forced by engine defects to abort the mission and landed in Sicily. The two cruisers increased speed to 28 knot and a couple of minutes later to 32 knots and opened fire with their 152mm/53 guns, with the third salvo straddling the enemy. The British gunfire soon straddled the Italian cruiser. At 0543 B/15, five enemy destroyers closed to attack, the leading destroyer was of the Tribal-class, followed by a J/K/N-class, the other three could not be identified. At 0546 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia opened fire on the leading destroyer with her main armament and as the range has now decreased, the 100mm/47 also joined. The Italian destroyers also opened fire at 0551 B/15 at a range of 18000 metres (their extreme range). Three minutes later, they were ordered to attack the convoy which was observed to turn back while enemy destroyers laid a smoke screen. At 0555 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia was hit by an enemy round on the 70mm armoured belt on the starboard side, two ratings were killed and eight were wounded. In the meantime, at 0559 B/15, the Italian destroyers had closed the enemy convoy at 5800 metres and the enemy destroyers at 5000 metres. Ugolini Vivaldi and Lanzerotto Malocello each launched two torpedoes. At 0605 B/15, a merchant vessel appeared to be hit by a torpedo. Seven minutes later, an enemy destroyer was hit and the explosion was followed by a dense smoke, she withdrew from the action. At 0600 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia fired one torpedo. At 0609 B/15, the Italian cruisers resumed their fire on the enemy cruisers at a range of 17000 metres but observation was difficult due to the smoke from the enemy ships. At 0620 B/15, the destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi was hit by a round in the forward engine room which had to be evacuated, ten men were killed and eight wounded. Admiral da Zara ordered the destroyers to give her assistance. Alfredo Oriani and Ascari were unable to do so due to the enemy fire. Only Lanzerotto Malocello stood by the stricken destroyer, and laid smoke to cover her. At 0645 B/15, the enemy destroyers were now at 4500 metres and Ugolino Vivaldi fired two torpedoes at the destroyer with the letters H 68 clearly seen on her bows [This was HMS Foresight]. Shortly after, she managed to increase speed at 13 knots. The enemy cruiser was firing at a range of 19000 metres at the Italian destroyers which could not reply due to the distance. At 0659 B/15, the leading enemy cruiser appeared to have been seriously hit, and the escorting destroyers covered her with smoke. By 0717 B/15, the enemy formation had completely disappeared from view, but a big detonation was observed. At 0735 B/15, Ugolino Vivaldi sighted Premuda, Ascari and Alfredo Oriani and the former joined to escort her. At 0757 B/15, a violent fire developed in Ugolino Vivaldi's forward engine room the forward magazine had to be flooded, and ammunition thrown overboard as a precaution. At 0919 B/15, Lanzerotto Malocello took her damaged sistership in tow and they proceed to Pantelleria. The enemy appeared briefly at 0801 B/15, and the cruisers resumed fire for a minute before contact was definitely lost. At 0935 B/15, an enemy bomber of the Beaufort type attacked Eugenio di Savoia, one torpedo missed astern and the other sank in mid-course. In the meantime, the cruisers were still in action with the enemy and, at 1156 B/15, a large explosion was observed with a white column of smoke of over 1000 metres in height believed to be from an enemy ammunition ship. At 1223 B/15, next to the enemy merchant ship on fire, two destroyers were observed and twelve minutes later, fire was opened on one of them at the range of 19500 metres. It was certainly hit and masked by smoke from the other destroyer. Another two destroyers were then sighted next to another merchant ship on fire and fire was opened on one of them at 1259 B/15 at a range of 18000 metres. The destroyer blew up at 1325 B/15 and sank rapidly. In the meantime, Alfredo Oriani and Ascari opened fire at a tanker at a range of 12000 metres which was at repeatedly hit at 1302 B/15-1304 B/15 and set afire. Italian bombers. At 1343 B/15, Alfredo Oriani launched a torpedo at the tanker, but it was only a muffled hit. At 1423 B/15-1437 B/15, six enemy bombers attacked Alfredo Oriani and Ascari; they were not hit. In the action, Eugenio di Savoia fired 838 rounds of 152mm rounds, 690 rounds of 100mm, 978 rounds of 37mm and 973 rounds of 13.2mm, Raimondo Montecuccoli fired 704 rounds of 152mm rounds, 368 rounds of 100mm rounds and 567 rounds of 37mm. The cruisers came under air attack at 1428 B/15 by enemy bombers and at 1600 B/15 by three torpedo bombers. One of the latter was shot down by Italian fighters. Shortly after, the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Premuda joined the cruisers. Premuda was detached and reached Trapani at 2105 B/15. The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli, escorted by the destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Lanzerotto Malocello and Ascari reached Naples at 1000 B/16-1154 B/16.

The tribulations of Ugolino Vivaldi. She was proceeding to Pantelleria, towed by Lanzerotto Malocello and escorted by Premuda. At 0927 B/15, the towing cable broke and Ugolino Vivaldi ordered Premuda to take over the towing and, nine minutes later, four Swordfish bombers attacked Lanzerotto Malocello and Ugolino Vivaldi, but they managed to avoid their torpedoes. At 0948 B/15, another Beaufort bomber missed astern the destroyer Ascari. At 1003 B/15, the destroyers Ascari and Alfredo Oriani rejoined the cruisers. At 1019 B/15, Premuda managed to pass a tow line to Ugolino Vivavli which was still on fire and were proceeding to Pantelleria, fifteen minutes later, they were sighted at long range by the Italian cruisers. At 1218 B/15, two minesweepers from Pantelleria joined them and were asked to provide all the available fire extinguishers and at 1400 B/15, Ugolino Vivaldi dropped anchor at Scauri (Pantelleria). At 1640 B/15, she ordered Premuda and Lanzerotto Malocello to leave the formation and rejoin the 7th Cruiser Division. At 2000 B/15, The damaged destroyer was joined by the tug Salvatore I and the fire was finally extinguished an hour later. At 0728 B/16, Ugolino Vivaldi departed from Pantelleria towed by Salvatore I, but the tug had engine defects and could not proceed at more than 5 knots. They were escorted by the torpedo boats Castore and Polluce and the tug Terracina and reached Trapani at 0154 B/17. (26)

11 Aug 1942 At 1400 B/11, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was to provide cover during 'Operation Pedestal' between Pantelleria and Malta.
HMS P 34 and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) were swept out until around 1625 B/11, by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). (27)
11 Aug 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 34 during her 13th war patrol see the map below.
12 Aug 1942 At 0523 B/12, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet with Asdic watch set.
At 0600 B/12, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth while proceeding towards her patrol position at 2 knots.
At 0620 B/12, course was altered for patrol position 'V' (35°55.5'N, 12°25.5'E).
At 1700 B/12, HMS P 34 arrived in position 'V' and commenced to patrol 2 miles either side of the patrol position on courses 270° - 090°.
At 2115 B/12, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued to patrol 2 miles either side of position 'V'.
At 2200 B/12, very distant flares and gunfire were seen to the northward. (27)

12 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 2219 B/12, the Capt. S.10 ordered the submarines in the patrol line; HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) to take up positions on patrol line D as had been specified in their patrol orders. (28)

13 Aug 1942 At 0005 B/13, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2219 B/12 in which she was ordered to proceed to position 'V 2' (36°19'N, 12°29.5'E) on patrol line D. Course was altered to 010°, speed 7 knots to comply with the orders received.
At 0355 B/13, HMS P 34 arrived in position 'V 2' and commenced to patrol two miles on either side of this position on courses 090°-270°.
At 0407 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0345 B/13 was received which stated that the Pedestal convoy would be two hours behind schedule.
At 0520 B/13, HMS P 34 dived to 80 with the Asdic watch set.
At 0600 B/13, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth in the vicinity of position 'V 2'.

At 0745 B/13, a large merchant ship of about 12000 tons was sighted bearing 030° at a distance of about 7 miles. This vessel, though considerably out of position, was one of the expected convoy. Later this ship was seen to change course to rejoin the convoy having become aware of the convoy's position by enemy action.
At 0805 B/13, several underwater explosions were heard and five minutes later a large column of white smoke about 1000 feet high was seen on bearing 200°. Obviously a ship of the convoy had been hit.
At 0815 B/13, a twin engined aircraft, which was on fire, was seen to fall into the sea in position 36°20'N, 12°29'E.

At 0820 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced in position 36°21'N, 12°30'E and proceeded on course 100° at 9 knots in accordance with the operation orders. The bridge and funnels of a destroyer were sighted bearing 180°. This was one of the convoy's escorts.
At 0825 B/13, HMS P 34 dived for an Italian aircraft, thought to be a Savoia SM 85.
At 0830 B/13, HMS P 34 returned to periscope depth. The aircraft was still in the vicinity and appeared to be searching.
At 0845 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced and again proceeded on course 100° at 9 knots.
At 0903 B/13, HMS P 34 once again dived to 80 feet for an Italian aircraft again thought to be a Savoia SM 85. This aircraft definately sighted the submarine as it turned directly towards.
At 0924 B/13, underwater explosions were heard.
At 0928 B/13, HMS P 34 returned to periscope depth. Two JU 87's were seen approaching so went deep again.
At 0935 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 100° at 9 knots.

At 0950 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0826 B/13 was received which contained the following orders; Enemy cruisers retired. Dive immediately course 040° convoy escort will attack submarines south of 36°10'N after 0900 B/13.

from 1008 B/13 onwards, intermittent underwater explosions were heard. These were only intese for a short period around 1045 B/13. During this period several enemy aircraft were sighted, mostly dive bombers.

At 1725 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1227 B/13 was received which contained the following orders for HMS P 34; Proceed through position 36°24'N, 11°59'E to area 'H', west of 12°01'E and between 35°50'N and 36°45'N. (off the Gulf of Hammamet).

At 2130 B/13, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 180° at 6 knots to get clear of HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) with which S.S./T. contact had showed that at 2043 B/13 she was bearing 290° at only 3300 yards.
At 2200 B/13, HMS P 34 altered course to 264° to proceed towards the Gulf of Hammamet.

At 2250 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1723 B/13 was received which contained the following orders; Convoy Operation not yet completed. HMS P 34, HMS P 44, HMS P 46 and HMS Utmost are to keep north-east of the line Gozo - Pantellaria until further orders. Course was set to 060° to comply with the orders received. (27)

13 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 0345 B/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) were informed of the following; Convoy two hours late. Delay easterly movement on the surface until 0800 B/13 and dive at 1400 B/13.

In a signal timed 0826 B/13, the Capt. S.10 informed the above submarines of the following; Enemy cruisers retired. Dive immediately course 040° convoy escort will attack submarines south of 36°10'N after 0900 B/13. (28)
14 Aug 1942 At 0108 B/14, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet when an aircraft dropped a flare right overhead.
At 0225 B/14, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 060° at 4 knots.
At 0235 B/14, another aircraft flare was dropped overhead and once again HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet surfacing again an hour later when she proceeded on course 360° at 4 knots.
At 0520 B/14, HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet commencing to patrol at periscope depth near position 36°40'N, 13°00'E, 40 minutes later.

At 1400 B/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1029 B/14 was received which contained the following orders for her; Proceed as in my signal timed 1227 B/13 to operate temporarily west of 12°01'E, between 36°45'N and 36°20'E.
At 1410 B/14, course was altered to 252° to comply with the above orders.
At 2120 B/14, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued on course 252° but now at a speed of 7 knots while charging. Speed was increased to 9 knots two hours later. (29)

15 Aug 1942 At 0515 B/15, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch closed up. At 0530 B/15, P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth. Pantelleria Island was in sight and HMS P 34 patrolled on course 270° about 20 miles to the south of Pantelleria.
At 1815 B/15, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1121 B/15 was received in which P 34 was ordered to patrol in position 36°33'N, 11°50'E. Course was altered to 073°.
At 2120 B/15, HMS P 34 surfaced and set course 063° to proceed to the ordered patrol position. (29)

15 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1625 B/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN); One merchant ship and one destroyer in position 38°43'N, 12°56'E, course 205°, speed 15 knots at 1615 B/15. Aircraft may attack before 0200 B/16.

In a follow up signal timed 2231 B/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to P 34 and P 44; One merchant ship and two destroyers in position 37°25'N, 11°47'E, course 180°, speed 15 knots at 2200 B/15.
[For the composition and further movements of this convoy see the event for 16 August 1942.] (28)
16 Aug 1942 At 0020 B/16, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 36°33'N, 11°50'E and commenced to patrol in the expected line of advance of an enemy convoy.

The expected convoy was made up of the Italian transport Rosolino Pilo (8326 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Maestrale and Vincenzo Gioberti which sailed from Naples for Tripoli at 0615 B/15-0700 B/15. When it was learned that an important enemy convoy had sailed from Gibraltar (operation PEDESTAL), they were ordered to Trapani where they arrived at 0414 B/16-0605 B/16. They finally sailed from Trapani at 0530 B/17-0635 B/17. They were attacked by four Beaufort torpedo bombers from 86 Squadron (RAF), Rosolino Pilo was hit by a torpedo at 1550 B/17 in position 35°55'N, 11°49'E; Vincenzo Gioberti was machine-gunned and had about 50 wounded, including her Captain. She turned back and arrived at Trapani at 2200 B/17. Two MAS boats sailed from Pantelleria to assist the freighter. Maestrale was ordered to turn back and arrived at Trapani at 0316 B/18. The tug Montecristo had been ordered to take her in tow. Rosolino Pilo was finished off by a torpedo from HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) at 0009 B/18. There were no casualties. She carried 4000 tons of stores for the Germans with a civilian crew of 50 and a military crew of 32 Italians and 39 Germans, and 110 German passengers. She had already been abandoned by her passengers and crew before the submarine attack, except her captain and four crew. Members who had remained on board and were evacuated shortly before she sank. Montecristo picked up the survivors.

At 0120 B/16, the generators were stopped and Asdic watch was set. Although visibility was good and HE conditions perfect nothing was seen or heard of the expected convoy and it is considered that the convoy could not have passed within 10 miles of the patrol position.
At 0528 B/16, HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch still closed up.
At 0600 B/16, HMS P 34 commenced to patrol at periscope depth in the vicinity of position 36°33'N, 11°50'E.
At 1330 B/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0930 B/16 was received which ordered HMS P 34 to operate in the original patrol area.
At 2133 B/16, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded on course 270° to patrol in the vicinity of the Kurba Bank. (29)
17 Aug 1942 At 0300 B/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived in position 36°26'N, 11°11'E and commenced to patrol in the vicinity of that position.

At 0400 B/17, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0242 B/17 was received which ordered HMS P 34 to patrol in position 36°34'N, 11°53'E (south-south-west of Pantelleria), by 1001 B/17. Course was set to 076° to comply with these orders. Speed was increased to 9 knots.
At 0520 B/17, a large amount of oil fuel was sighted in the vicinity of position 36°30'N, 11.30'E.
At 0600 B/17, HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet continuing on course 076° at 3 knots.
At 1200 B/17, HMS P 34 was in position 36°37'N, 11°50'E. Course was altered to 150° to proceed to the ordered position.

At 1310 B/17, the bridges and funnels of two destroyers were sighted on bearing 050°. These destroyers were escorting a merchant vessel. Range was about 6 to 7 miles. Enemy course 180°. Position of the convoy was 36°38'N, 12°00'E, just to the south of Pantelleria. It was quite clear the convoy was going to pass out of range. The nearest range obtained was about 10000 yards. The merchant vessel was seen to be a modern 8000-tons one, she was also camouflaged. An air escort of at least six aircraft was patrolling in the area. The convoy was last seen at 1845 B/17 steering 180°.

At 1700 B/17, smoke was sighted bearing 180°. This turned out to be a destroyer, probably one of the convoy escort, returning, steering 360° at 25 to 30 knots. The enemy passed at a range of about 5000 yards. His smoke was last seen to the northward at 1725 B/17.
At 1950 B/17, another destroyer passed steering 005° at between 25 to 30 knots.
At 2053 B/17, HE was picked up on bearing 290°. This disappeared to the eastward.
At 2130 B/17, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded towards the Gulf of Hammamet to patrol in the vicinity of the Kurba Bank. (27)

18 Aug 1942 At 0010 B/18, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) observed a large explosion to the southwards. This was considered to be a merchant vessel blowing up after having been torpedoed by either the RAF or a submarine [in fact this was HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) handywork]. The explosion was in the position where flares had been dropped at 2230 B/17.

At 0246 B/18, a darkened shape was seen bearing 090° which was proceeding towards but not at high speed as no bow wave was seen. P 34 dived to avoid being sighted. This was almost certainly a destroyer but no attack developed and the enemy apparently had not seen the submarine.
At 0430 B/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and continued her passage.
At 0535 B/18, HMS P 34 dived with the Asdic watch set.
At 0615 B/18, periscope depth patrol was commenced patrolling in the vicinity of the Kurba Bank.

At 1245 B/18, two puffs of smoke were seen to the north-west, very close inshore. The originators of the smoke were never sighted but it is considered they were most likely Vichy-French coastal traffic.

At 1400 B/18, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1140 B/18 was received which ordered her to also patrol in the southern limit of the patrol area (where P 44 had been patrolling). The patrol area now thus also included Kerkennah and Lampion.
The 2130 B/18, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded to the southward on the estimated Kuriat - Kelibia traffic route with the intention to patrol near Kuriat the following day. (27)

19 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1140 B/19, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Leave patrol as necessary through positions 35°13'N, 12°31'E and 35°37'N, 14°08'E to R/V with minesweeper at end of the searched channel at 0959 B/23. (28)

20 Aug 1942 At 0200 B/20, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) which had patrolled on the estimated route between Kuriat and Kelibia since 2130 B/19, altered course to 245° to proceed towards Sousse.
At 0530 B/20, HMS P 34 dived to 60 feet with the Asdic watch closed up.

At 0630 B/20, she came to periscope depth and continued on towards Sousse. The mine detector unit was run all the time. During the forenoon the anchorage off Sousse was investigated in the hope that Italian phosphate ships might be at anchor but nothing was sighted and HMS P 34 retired to seaward.
At 1513 B/20, course was set to 210° to proceed towards Monastir to investigate. No shipping was subsequently seen there either and once again P 34 retired to seaward.

At 1620 B/20, in position 35°53'N, 10°47'E, a small vessel was seen bearing 145° steering 270°. A motor torpedo boat appeared to be astern of the vessel and it was accordingly decided that it was a small but valuable ship, although the course she was steering indicated otherwise. An attack was started and P 34 closed at high submerged speed.

At 1630 B/20, the vessel was identified as the Vichy-French Optimiste (????) towing a large sized motor launch. The attack was broken off. The vessel was clearly marked with French national colours on the bridge, funnel and ship's sides.
At 2030 B/20, HMS P 34 surfaced and patrolled on the suspected convoy route between Kuriat and Kelibia. (27)
21 Aug 1942 At 0010 B/21, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) altered course to 123° to patrol in the vicinity of Lampedusa on the Pantelleria - Kerkennah convoy route.
At 0530 B/21, HMS P 34 dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch closed up. One hour later periscope depth patrol was commenced in the vicinity of position 35°31'N, 11°57'E.
At 2130 B/21, HMS P 34 surfaced and proceeded towards Kerkennah.
At 2300 B/21, HMS P 34 set course to return to Malta in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1140 B/19. (27)
23 Aug 1942 At 0825 B/23, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was off the end to the searched channel and sighted her minesweeper escort.
At 0850 B/23, HMS P 34 surfaced and closed the minesweeper which turned out to be HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN).
At 0900 B/23, HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN) also surfaced and in company the three ships proceeded up the searched channel to Malta.
At 1205 B/23, HMS P 34 ended her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. Only vessels out of range were observed. (27)
2 Sep 1942 At 1530 B/2, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the West coast of Greece.

However on making a trial dive shortly after 1800 hours, the D.S.E.A. escape hatch was found to be leaking badly and the problem could not be found. During a second trials dive the problem was just as bad as during the first trials dive. Also it had been noticed that the port propeller shaft was very noisy at slow speeds and at 1823 B/2, Lt. Harrison decided to return to Malta to have the defects rectified.
At 2000 B/2, HMS P 34 was back at Malta.
3 Sep 1942 At 0630 B/3, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), having completed her repairs, sailed again to resume her patrol.

On making a trials dive the D.S.E.A. hatch was found to be satisfactory but the port propeller shaft was still very noisy at slow speed. It was decided to continue on patrol in the hopes the problem to the port shaft would rectify itself.
She was to pass through position 35°22'N, 15°05'E around 0430 B/4 and then proceed towards position 38°23'N, 19°45'E (west of Cephalonia). (27)

4 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1639 B/4, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN); P 34 proceed direct to arrive in position 37°55'N, 19°57'E by 0301 B/6 thence to centre of area ?Z [????]. P 43 proceed direct to arrive in position 36°06'N, 20°48'E by 2259 B/6. Passage to be made on the surface during daylight as necessary to comply. Further instructions will be signalled by Capt. S.1. (30)

5 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1615 C/5, sent the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Cancel the previous orders. Proceed to area 'S', southern half. Diving by day. further instructions will follow.
On receipt of this signal HMS P 34 was in position 37°19'N, 18°38'E. Course was set to 105° to comply with the orders received.

At 2104 B/5, course was changed to 145° to comply with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1707 C/5 which had contained the following orders; My 1615 C/5. Proceed through positions 36°58'N, 19°15'E and 36°30'N, 20°42'E, thence operate in the south-west corner of area 'S'. (30)

6 Sep 1942 At 2150 C/6, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1850 C/6 in which she was ordered to patrol in position 35°48'N, 21°37'E. Course was altered to 127° and speed was increased to 8 knots to comply with these orders.

At 2350 C/6, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2115 C/6 was received moving the patrol position 318° - 40 miles. Course was altered to 103° and speed was reduced to 6 miles to comply with these orders. (27)

6 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1850 C/6, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders; Proceed forthwith diving by day to following positions; HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), 35°48'N, 21°37'E HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN), 35°15'N, 21°00'E HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), 35°05'N, 21°51'E HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN) 33°33'N, 21°42'N HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, DSO, RN) only to be in position 32°31'N, 23°34'E by dawn September 8th.

In a signal timed 2047 B/6, they were informed that three merchant ships and ten destroyers had been reported at 1740 B/6 in position 38°46'N, 20°13'E proceeding on course 170° at 10 knots. They were also informed that aircraft may attack the enemy convoy north of Navarino tonight.

In a signal timed 2115 C/6, further instructions were sent by Capt. S.1. HMS P 34, HMS P 43 and HMS Una move to intercept from positions ordered in my 1850 C/6 as follows; HMS P 34, 318° - 40 miles. HMS P 43, 024° - 32 miles. HMS Una, 250° - 18 miles. Be in new positions by dawn 7th Sept. HMS Traveller, ignore my 1850 C/6 and be in position 32°45'N, 20°35'E by 0001 C/8.

These submarines were to operate against an Italian convoy operation; At 0036 B/6-0150 B/6, convoy 'N', consisting of the transports Sestriere (Italian 7991 GRT, built 1942) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere (escort leader), Lampo, Legionario (equipped with new German DETE Gerät) and the Italian torpedo boats Pegaso and Partenope sailed from Brindisi.

At 0012 B/6-0205 B/6, convoy 'P', consisting of transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Manara (Italian 7124 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the italian destroyers Fuciliere (escort leader), Bombardiere, Corsaro, Freccia, Camicia Nera, Geniere and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade sailed from Taranto.

The two convoys were to merge for defensive purposes, but then to split, this time the three Italian ships proceeding to Benghazi and Ankara to Tobruk. At 0100 B/6, the torpedo boat Procione sailed from Piraeus to reinforce the convoy. At 1040 B/6, the two convoys merged and the destroyer Aviere assumed overall command. The combined convoy was now known as convoy 'L'.

At 1525 B/6, Procione joined convoy and was thus present during air attack when Manara was torpedoed at 1530 B/6. The air attack was carried out by fourteen to sixteen torpedo bombers, Manara was hit in the stern section and taken in tow by Freccia and escorted by Legionario and Procione. They proceeded to Corfu. At 1855 B/6, Legionario left them to rejoin the convoy. At 1003 B/7, Manara was brought to the Bay of Arilla (near Cape Cavobianco, Corfu).

At 1810 B/6, the convoy split. Ankara escorted by the destroyers Fuciliere (which resumed duty as escort leader), Geniere, Lampo, Bombardiere and the torpedo boat Partenope slowed down at 10 knots to distance itself from Ravello and Sestriere escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Legionario, Corsaro, Camicia Nera and the torpedo boats Pallade and Pegaso, which forged ahead. The Ankara section was now known as Convoy 'P', the Ravello section as Convoy 'N'.
At 0449 B/7, Legionario rejoined the Ankara convoy.

At 0547 B/7, the Ankara convoy sighted the Ravello/Sestriere convoy and at 0737 B/7, the two convoys merged again and were now known, once again as convoy 'L', with Aviere once more assuming overall command.

At 0927 B/7, Lampo reported torpedo tracks in position 36°10'N, 21°05'E (this was the attack by HMS P 34) and Sestriere signalled she had avoided torpedoes from the port side by turning to starboard. Lampo was ordered to hunt the submarine. At 1355 B/7, she signalled that the A/S search had not yielded result and that she expected to rejoin the convoy at at about 1900 B/7. She caught up at 1738 B/7.
At 1810 B/7, three enemy aircraft attacked the combined convoy which opened up with antiaircraft fire. Bombs fell near Ankara.

At 1941 B/7, the convoy split again, Fuciliere assuming command of convoy 'P' which proceeded to Tobruk. Lampo had been assigned to join the escort of the Benghazi convoy, but as it had been reduced to two ships, Fuciliere requested from Aviere that she reinforce the Ankara convoy and this was granted.
Convoy 'N' was now formed of Ravello and Sestriere escorted by Aviere, Corsaro, Legionario, Camicia Nera, Pegaso and Pallade.

At 0216 B/8, in position 33°30'N, 22°43'E, a low-flying aircraft dropped two or three bombs which narrowly missed Fuciliere (2 metres!) causing massive damage, the shock disabled her gyro compass and her radio but she managed to make 12 knots and remained with the convoy. At 0302 B/8, Fuciliere, by visual signal, ordered Geniere to take over command of the convoy. At 0353 B/8, Fuciliere left the convoy, to make for Crete. Partenope had also turned north to escort her but Fuciliere ordered her to rejoin the convoy. At 0850 B/8, Bombardiere informed Fuciliere that she had been ordered by Geniere to assist her. Fuciliere replied that she was in urgent need of a towing. Bombardiere was proceeding at 25 knots but had trouble locating Fuciliere as the latter could only give a very approximate position due to the breakdown of her gyrocompass. She finally found her at 1830 B/8 near Sphakia and at 2355 B/8 had taken the stricken vessel in tow. At 0729 B/9, Geniere rejoined them. At 0849 B/9, they dropped anchor at Castel Selino (SE Crete) and at 1230 B/9, the Italian destroyer Nicolò Zeno arrived to assist them. At 1810 B/9, the four destroyers left for Navarino where they arrived at 0905 B/10-0919 B/10.

In the meantime, at 0636 B/8, the Ankara convoy was joined by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Brioni (1987 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the torpedo boat Orione which had sailed from Suda at 1330 B/7. Ankara, Brioni, Geniere, Lampo, Partenope and Orione arrived at Tobruk at 1315-1428 B/8. Geniere sailed at 1701 B/8 for Taranto but was later diverted to assist Fuciliere (see above).

Convoy 'N' arrived at Benghazi at 1021 B/8-1321 B/8. Ravallo, Sestriere, Legionario and Pallade entered harbour while Aviere, Corsaro and Camicia Nera remained outside, awaiting instructions from Supermarina, while Pegaso was detached to assist Fuciliere but eventualle Pegaso was recalled and entered Benghazi at 2000 B/8. (31)

7 Sep 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) fired four torpedoes at an enemy convoy in the central Mediterranean about 45 nautical miles south-west of Schiza Island, Greece in position 36°17'N, 21°03'E. All torpedoes fired missed their target.

The convoy consisted of the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, 1941), Sestriere (Italian, 7991 GRT, 1942) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, 1937) escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Legionario, Fuciliere, Bombardiere, Geniere, Camicia Nera, Corsaro, Lampo and the torpedo boats Pegaso, Partenope and Pallade. Sestriere reported to have been missed by the torpedoes, which tracks had also been sighted by Lampo which was ordered to hunt the attacking submarine. She later signalled the results of this hunt as negative.
0207 B/7 - Arrived in position 36°18'N, 21°04'E and commenced patrolling in the vicinity of this position.
0520 B/7 - Dived to 80 feet with the Asdic watch set.
0550 B/7 - Commenced to patrol at periscope depth.
0823 B/7 - Sighted various aircraft to the north-westward. These were Ju 88's and Cant's. Some of these were seen to drop bombs. Most likely these aircraft were escorting the expected convoy.
0835 B/7 - Sighted masts and funnels of the approaching convoy bearing 305°.

0840 B/7 - Started attack on the convoy which was soon seen to be made up of three large merchant ships in line ahead. Many destroyers formed the escort. Several aircraft were also overhead. The leading merchant vessel was an older type of about 6000 tons, the other two were modern ones of 9000 tons.

0921 B/7 - In position 36°17'N, 21°03'E fired four torpedoes at the rear ship from 7000 yards. Seventeen seconds after firing the first torpedo there was a very heavy explosion just ahead of P 34. The first torpedo must have prematured after running off its safety range.
0927 B/7 - Heard a torpedo exploding giving a running range of 8000 yards corresponding with the range on firing of 7000 yards.
0936 B/7 - A counter attack commenced by three destroyers using hydrophones. Several patterns of depth charges were dropped rather close but to shallow. P 34 was at 120 feet at the time.
1030 B/7 - An Asdic fitted destroyer took over the hunt from the other three.
1056 B/7 - The destroyer passed overhead and dropped a pattern of four depth charges very close causing some damage.
1226 B/7 - After hearing no HE and no Asdic transmissions for a while started to return to periscope depth.
1236 B/7 - Head Asdic transmissions on the starboard beam.
1238 B/7 - At periscope depth. Saw a destroyer at a range of 2500 yards on our starboard beam. Went deep again. The destroyer was in Asdic contact.

1258 B/7 - The destroyer passed overhead and dropped a pattern of 5 depth charges which were again very close causing some more damage. This was followed by another pattern a bit further off about one minute later. P 34 remained deep for the remainder of the afternoon.
1700 B/7 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. During the counter attack a total of 83 depth charges had been counted.

1715 B/7 - Went ahead on the port shaft. This resulted in clouds of black smoke which soon filled the submarine. A fire had started, as a result of damage from the depth charging, when starting up the motor so the port motor was placed out of action.
1744 B/7 - Surfaced for 5 minutes to clear the boat of the smoke.
1830 B/7 - Commenced to reload the torpedo tubes.
2030 B/7 - All tubes were now reloaded. One torpedo was found to be defective.
2124 B/7 - Surfaced and set course to return to Malta as a result of the damage inflicted by the depth charging. It was not possible to transmit a signal as the W/T was out of action. (27)

7 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 0956 C/7, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the following submarines; HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, DSO, RN), HMS Traveller (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). On completion of attacks submarines should if possible follow up convoys to finish off damaged ships. This should be a standard practice, A/S measures permitting.
In a follow up signal timed 1015 C/7, these submarines were informed that Allied aircraft had been ordered not to attack submarines unless positively identified as enemy in areas 'K', 'B' and 'T'.

In a signal timed 1514 B/7 and amended in a signal timed 1619 B/7, these submarines were informed by Capt. S.10 that three transports and nine destroyers had been sighted at 1450 B/7 in position 35°30'N, 21°40'N, on course 170° at 10 knots.
In a signal timed 2001 B/7, the Capt. S.10 informed these submarines that aircraft from Malta hope to attack the enemy convoy about midnight tonight. (31)

7 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1837 C/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). If nothing has been sighted by dark proceed patrol area 'K' in sectors from position (A) 35°42'N, 23°28'E as follows: HMS Una, 170° to 215°. HMS P 43, 215° to 235°. P 34, 235° to 270°. Except when in pursuit of enemy submarines should keep to their sector, P 34 and Una not to approach within 5 miles of position (A) and P 43 within 10 miles of position (A). Attention drawn to my signal timed 1015/7th [see above]. (31)

8 Sep 1942 At 2145 B/8, in position 36°10'N, 18°50'E, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a hospital ship on bearing 160° proceeding in the direction of the Straits of Messina on course 320°.
This was the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) which departed from Tobruk at 1700 B/6 via Derna (0800 B/7-1830 B/7) and arrived at Naples at 0940 B/10. (27)

9 Sep 1942 At 0010 B/9, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) finally was able to pass her signal timed 2105 B/8 which contained the following; At 0926 B/7 in position 36°17'N, 21°03'E obtained one hit on rear 9000 ton ship of the convoy. Port main motor out of action. Am returning to Malta through positions 36°16'N, 18°56'E and 35°22'N, 15°05'E to arrive at 0959 B/13.
This signal was acknowledged by the Capt. S.1 in his signal timed 0412 C/9.
In a signal timed 1041 B/9, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 34 to proceed further through positions 35°12'N, 15°01'E and 35°12'N, 14°09'E on her way to Malta. (27)
13 Sep 1942 At 1230 B/13, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 0930 B/13. (27)
24 Sep 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Malta. (32)

25 Sep 1942 At 1445 B/25, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was to proceed to Gibraltar as she was to return to the U.K. to refit. En-route she was ordered to patrol of the south-west coast of Sardinia, south of 39°30'N.
She departed Malta in company with HMS Una (Lt. C.P Norman, RN) and HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN). They were escorted out until around 1700 B/25 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN).
27 Sep 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) made the westbound submerged passage though the Italian declared mine area to the south / south-west of Sicily. (27)
30 Sep 1942 From 30 September to 2 October 1942, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) patrolled off the south-west coast of Sardinia but nothing at all was sighted. (27)

3 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0330 B/3, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) reported the following to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.); My position 39°01'N, 06°58'E, speed of advance 105 miles per day.
In a reply, timed 0536 A/3, this signal was acknowledged. P 34 was ordered to proceed through position 39°01'N, 06°01'E and 38°52'N, 02°57'E. Further orders / routing would follow.

In a signal timed 1036 A/3, F.O.C.N.A. signalled the following; (1) My 0536 A/3, Proceed dived by day through positions 38°52'N, 02°57'E at 0030 A/5 and 180° - Gape de Gata - 10 miles at 1600 A/7 thence arriving Gibraltar 1500 A/8 proceeding on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion on 8th. (2) Submerged bombing restrictions arranged 20 miles either side route from 35 miles ahead to 35 miles astern estimated position 1036 A/3. (3) Total bombing restrictions arranged on 8th. (4) Report if unable to remain inside bombing restrictions. (33)

4 Oct 1942 At 1750 B/4, in position 39°00'N, 03°30'E, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted the upperworks of a merchant ship bearing 300°. Course of this ship was 180°. Range 10 miles. This was most likely a Vichy-French vessel proceeding to Algiers. (27)

6 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 2101 A/6 (passed at 2257 A/6), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) reported the following to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.); Your 1036 A/3, My position 37°21'N, 00°24'W. E.T.A. 0730 A/9.
In a reply timed 0906 A/7, F.O.C.N.A. replied as follows; Your 2101 A/6 acknowledged. Do not cross 05°00'W before 0700 A/9. Arrive at Gibraltar at 0900 A/9. (27)

8 Oct 1942 At 1225 B/8, in position 36°14'N, 04°04'W, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a merchant vessel proceeding on course 080° at 8 knots. This was considered to be most likely a Spanish vessel. (27)

9 Oct 1942 As HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) approached Gibraltar, she was detected by the RDF station on the Rock. As she was west of 05°W and submarines had been instructed not to cross the 5°W meridian before 0700 A/9, the destroyer HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN) was sent to intercept. HMS P 34 had meanwhile dived due to heavy fog which was fortunate. Even more fortunate was that the Asdic set of HMS Wishart was out of action. Soon after diving a ship was heard to pass overhead and this was soon seen to be a British destroyer.
At 0650 A/9, HMS P 34 surfaced and identified herself to HMS Wishart which was about 4 miles away.
Around 0930 A/9, P 34 ended her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. (27)
18 Oct 1942 At 1430 A/18, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K.

She was to pass through positions 225° - Cape St. Vincent - 20 at 2100 A/19, thence diving by day through position 39°01'N, 12°01'W at 0800 A/21, 43°01'N, 12°58'W at 1900 A/23 towards 49°05'N, 12°10'W.

26 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 2212 A/26, the Capt. S.5 sent to the following orders to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Rendezvous with the trawler HMS Egilsay (T/Lt. W.E. Belgrave, RNVR) at 0800 A/29 in position 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles for onward escorted surface passage to Portsmouth.

29 Oct 1942 At 0800 A/29, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was in position 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles. In the bad weather conditions, an easterly gale with bad visibility, her escort, HMS Egilsay (T/Lt. W.E. Belgrave, RNVR) was not sighted.

HMS P 34 remained in the vicinity of the rendezvous position. At 1530 A/29, the weather had moderated and P 34 closed the land and confirmed her position and then carried out a search for the trawler which was finally sighted at 1800 A/29. Course was then set for Portsmouth.
30 Oct 1942 At 1755 A/30, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Gosport / Fort Blockhouse). (27)
31 Oct 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was docked in AFD 2 at Gosport (Fort Blockhouse). (34)
2 Nov 1942 HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was undocked. (35)
16 Nov 1942 Around 1345 B/16, P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) departed Gosport (Fort Blockhouse) for Plymouth where she was to refit at the Devonport Dockyard. (35)
17 Nov 1942 Around 1020 A/17, HMS P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) arrived at Plymouth.
She was escorted in by HMS Lincolnshire (A/Skr.Lt. S.L. Larner, RNR) which had joined around 0730 A/17.
As she could not be accepted for refit immediately she was to participate for several days in special RDF trials. (35)
18 Nov 1942 P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) participated in special RDF trials with aircraft off Plymouth. (35)
19 Nov 1942 P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) participated in special RDF trials with aircraft off Plymouth. (35)
20 Nov 1942 P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) participated in special RDF trials with aircraft off Plymouth. (35)
21 Nov 1942 P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) participated in special RDF trials with aircraft off Plymouth. (35)
23 Nov 1942 P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) participated in special RDF trials with aircraft off Plymouth. (35)
24 Nov 1942 P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) participated in special RDF trials with aircraft off Plymouth. (35)
25 Nov 1942 P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) participated in special RDF trials with aircraft off Plymouth. (35)
26 Nov 1942 P 34 (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) participated in special RDF trials with aircraft off Plymouth. (35)
11 Mar 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted a basin dive trial at the Devonport Dockyard. (36)
18 Mar 1943 With her refit at the Devonport Dockyard completed, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted D/G trials and post refit trials off Plymouth. (36)
19 Mar 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted RDF trials off Plymouth with HMS Ruby (T/Lt. C.F. Unwin, RNVR) and aircraft.. (36)
22 Mar 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted exercises off Plymouth. (36)
28 Mar 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted exercises off Plymouth. (36)

1 Apr 1943 Around 0930 A/1, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Plymouth for Holy Loch. She was to rendezvous around 1400 A/1 with HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) off the Scilly Islands for onward passage north through the Irish Sea.
was escorted from Plymouth to the rendezvous by HMS Pearl (T/Lt. G.K. Hill, RNVR). (37)
4 Apr 1943 Around 0045 B/4, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) arrived at Holy Loch to commence a post refit training period.
En-route from Plymouth she had suffered from engine defects.
[No log for April 1943 is available for HMS Ultimatum. Therefore some details for this month will be missing.] (38)

20 Apr 1943 HMS Oberon (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, DSC, RN) served as target in the Clyde area for members of the C.O.Q.C. (Commanding Officers Qualifying Course) with were embarked in HMS Sealion (Lt. D.S.M. Verschoyle-Campbell, DSC, RN).
Upon completion of these exercises HMS Oberon served as target for HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) which was also conducting exercises in the Clyde area. (39)

30 Apr 1943 Around 1605 B/30, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Holy Loch for Scapa Flow. Passage north through the Minches was made together with HMS Truculent (Lt.Cdr. R.L. Alexander, RN), HNoMS Ula (Lt. R.M. Sars) and HrMs O 10 (Lt.Cdr. Baron D.T. Mackay, RNN). They were escorted by HMS Dornoch (Lt. H.E. Jackson, RN).
2 May 1943 Around 1340 B/2, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) arrived at Scapa Flow to participate in A/S and attack exercises. (40)

3 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted attack exercises off Scapa Flow. Practice attacks were made on HMS Malaya (Capt. J.W.A. Waller, RN) and HMS Suffolk (Capt. R. Shelley, CBE, RN) which were both also conducting exercises in company with each other. The destroyer HMS Tuscan (Lt.Cdr. G.I.M. Balfour, RN) and escort destroyers HMS Blankney (Lt.Cdr. D.H.R. Bromley, RN) and HMS Brecon (Lt.Cdr. T.D. Herrick, DSC and Bar, RN) were escorting the battleship and cruiser. (40)
4 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted attack exercises off Scapa Flow. (40)
5 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted A/S exercises off Scapa Flow with HMS Onslow (Capt. J.A. McCoy, DSO, RN) and HMS Tyrian (Cdr. C.W. Greening, RN). (40)

5 May 1943 HMS Suffolk (Capt. R. Shelley, CBE, RN) conducted gunnery exercises at Scapa Flow. These were followed by underway refuelling exercises. She first fuelled the destroyer HMS Onslow (Capt. J.A. McCoy, DSO, RN) and later HMS Active (Lt.Cdr. P.G. Merriman, RN).
She then conducted an exercises with a submarine, which must have been HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR).

HMS Suffolk then left Scapa Flow for exercises at sea from 5 to 7 May. In the afternoon of the 6th she was joined by her sister ship HMS Berwick (Capt. H.J. Egerton, RN) and both cuisers then conducted exercises until returning to Scapa Flow in the morning of the 9th. (41)
6 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted attack exercises off Scapa Flow. (40)

8 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted A/S exercises off Scapa Flow, first with HMS Tumult (Lt.Cdr. N. Lanyon, RN) and HMS Tyrian (Cdr. C.W. Greening, RN) and later with HMS Brecon (Lt.Cdr. T.D. Herrick, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Brissenden (Lt. D.C. Beatty, RN). (40)

12 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted A/S exercises off Scapa Flow, first with HMS Tumult (Lt.Cdr. N. Lanyon, RN) and ORP Slazak (Lt.Cdr. R. Nalecz-Tyminski, ORP) and later with HMS Charlestown (Lt. W.F.B. Webb, DSC, RN) and HMS Chiddingfold (Lt. T.M. Dorrien-Smith, RN). (40)

13 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted A/S exercises off Scapa Flow, first with HMS Eclipse (Lt.Cdr. E. Mack, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Cleveland (Lt. J.K. Hamilton, RN), and later with HMS Onslow (Capt. J.A. McCoy, DSO, RN) and HMS Tyrian (Cdr. C.W. Greening, RN). (40)

15 May 1943 Around 2200 B/15, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Scapa Flow for Lerwick. At sea she joined HMS Tuna (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS Seadog (Lt. C.R. Pelly, RN) and their escort HMS Bryony (T/Lt. T. Hand, RNR).
Earlier in the day, HMS Ultimatum had carried out gunnery exercises off Scapa Flow. (40)
16 May 1943 At 1255 B/16, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) arrived at Lerwick from Scapa Flow. (40)
18 May 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) conducted A/S exercises off Lerwick with aircraft. (40)
21 May 1943 At 0600 B/21, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Lerwick for her 16th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Norwegian Sea for an anti-Uboat patrol.
For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below.

24 May 1943 At 1708 B/24, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) received the signal timed 1447 B/24 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) in which it was reported that there were two German U-boats within 120 miles of Ultimatum's position.
HMS Ultimatum at that time was in approximate position 64°32'N, 05°20'W.
This most likely referred to the German submarines U-467 and U-488.

The exact position of the German U-boat U-467 is not known but it looks likely that she was already passed HMS Ultimatum. U-467 had departed Bergen on 20 May 1943 for the North Atlantic but was sunk on 25 May 1943 to the south of Iceland in position 62°25'N, 14°52'W by a Fido homing torpedo from a US Catalina aircraft. There were no survivors.

U-488 had departed Kristiansand (South) on 20 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. At 1600 B/24, she was in German naval grid AE 6735 (centred in 64°21'N, 08°52'30"W) which was a little over 90 miles from HMS Ultimatum's position. This too was already past the patrol position of HMS Ultimatum.

The was also U-193 which had departed Bergen for the North Atlantic on 22 May and at 1600 B/24 was in German naval grid AF 4947 (centred in 63°57'00"N, 000°37'30"E) which was about 160 miles from HMS Ultimatum's position and not yet past her. (42)

26 May 1943 At 1706 B/26, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) received the signal timed 1443 B/26 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) in which it was reported that there was a German U-boat within 99 miles of Ultimatum's position. At 1517 B/26, HMS Ultimatum was in position 64°48'N, 05°14'W.

This most likely referred to the German submarine U-667 which had departed Kristiansand (South) on 22 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. At 1600 B/26, U-667 was in German naval grid AF 4586 (centred in position 64°39'00"N, 000°22'30"W) which was about 120 miles from HMS Ultimatum's position. (43)

31 May 1943 At 1830 B/31, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) received the signal timed 1646 B/31 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) in which it was reported that there were two German U-boats within 120 miles of position 64°59'N, 05°01'W.
This most likely referred to the German submarines U-341 and U-535.

U-341 had departed Kristiansand (South) on 27 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. At 1600 B/31 she was in German naval grid AE 6694 (64°39'00"N, 004°37'30"W) which was a little over 20 miles from the position reported.

U-535 had departed Kiel on 25 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. Her exact position is not known as she was lost on 5 July 1943 during her first war patrol. There were no survivors. which had departed Kristiansand (South) on 22 May 1943 for the North Atlantic. At 1600 B/26, U-667 was in German naval grid AF 4586 (centred in position 64°39'00"N, 000°22'30"W) which was about 120 miles from HMS Ultimatum's position. (44)
1 Jun 1943 At 0400 B/1, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) left patrol to return to Lerwick in accordance with the Capt. S.9's signal timed 0931 B/31. (27)
3 Jun 1943 At 1100 B/3, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) ended her 16th war patrol at Lerwick. (27)

4 Jun 1943 At 2200 B/4, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Lerwick for Holy Loch. She made the passage together with HMS Tuna (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS Stubborn (Lt. A.A. Duff, RN). They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN).
The next morning, at 0942 B/4, they were joined by HMS Syrtis (Lt M.H. Jupp, DSC, RN) which came from Scapa Flow. (45)
6 Jun 1943 At 2030 B/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) arrived at Holy Loch from Lerwick. (45)

14 Jun 1943 At 2000 B/14, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Holy Loch for Scapa Flow. She made the passage together with HMS Truant (Lt.Cdr. J.G. Hopkins, RN). They were escorted by HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). (45)
16 Jun 1943 At 1250 B/16, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) arrived at Scapa Flow after having parted company with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) at 1155 B/16. (45)
19 Jun 1943 At 1015 B/19, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Scapa Flow for Blyth.
At sea, at 1225 B/19, she joined HMS Satyr (Lt. T.S. Weston, RN) and her escort HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR) that were en-route to Dundee coming from Lerwick.
HMS Ultimatum was escorted to the rendezvous by HMS Lord Lloyd (T/Lt. M.W. Bond, RNVR). (45)
20 Jun 1943 Around 1700 B/20, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) arrived at Blyth. (45)
21 Jun 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) proceeded from Blyth to Wallsend where she was to undergo repairs at the Swan Hunter shipyard. (45)
26 Jun 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) was docked at Wallsend. (45)
15 Jul 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was undocked. (46)
5 Aug 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) proceeded from Wallsend to Blyth. (47)
6 Aug 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted exercises off Blyth. (47)
7 Aug 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted exercises off Blyth. (47)
9 Aug 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted exercises off Blyth. (47)

11 Aug 1943 Early in the evening, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR), HMS Una (Lt. W.G. Meeke, DSC, RN) and HMS Uther (Lt. P.S. Beale, RN) departed Blyth for Holy Loch. They were escorted by HMS ML 300 (T/Lt. F.C.R. Piesse, RNVR) until Dundee where HMS Loch Monteith (T/Lt. K.W. Richardson, RNR) took over the escort. (47)
14 Aug 1943 At 1430 B/14, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Holy Loch from Blyth. (47)

16 Aug 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Kingfisher (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) G.H. Gandy, RN) serving as target. Also gunnery exercises were carried out. (47)

17 Aug 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Scott (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Sharpey-Schafer, RN) serving as target. Also gunnery exercises were carried out. (47)

17 Aug 1943 HMS Varangian (Lt. J. Nash, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Scott (Lt.Cdr. J.M. Sharpey-Schafer, RN), HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville). (48)

20 Aug 1943 At 1906 A/20, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) departed Holy Loch for her 17th war patrol. She was to patrol in the Bay of Biscay (A/S patrol) and to proceed to Gibraltar upon completion of this patrol.

Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS Truant (Lt.Cdr. J.G. Hopkins, RN), Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville) and Minerve (Lt. H.F.D. Simon-Dubuisson). They were escorted by HMS Kingston Amber (T/Lt. Richard Adams, RNR).
At 0130 A/20, HMS Ultimatum parted company with the other submarines and HMS Kingston Amber in position 180° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles.
HMS Ultimatum was to proceed through positions 'UA' (49°01'N, 07°30'W), 'UB' (48°01'N, 07°30'W) thence direct to anti U-Boat patrol in zone 'J 7'.

HMS Ultimatum was to leave patrol to proceed through position 'UC' (45°01'N, 06°01'W) at 0800 A/31. Thence by route 'Gold' to 07°00'W, thence inside 100-fathom line to pass through position 'ZZ' 43°01'N, 09°45'W at 0400 A/3 for onward passage to Gibraltar.

22 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 2206 A/22, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); Cancel previous instructions. Prom position 'UB' (48°01'N, 07°30'W) HMS Ultimatum proceed direct diving by day to patrol in zone 'J 10'. From position 'CA' (48°10'N, 07°01'W) Curie proceed direct diving by day to patrol in zone 'J 11'. (50)

24 Aug 1943 At 1850 A/24, in approximate position 46°58'N, 07°15'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted a southbound sailing vessel. Investigation showed this to be a tunny fishing vessel which was indeed fishing at the moment.
At 2245 A/24, a darkened ship was sighted. An attack was started.
At 2300 A/24, this vessel was identified as a tunny fishing vessel thought to be the same one seen at 1850 A/24. (51)
27 Aug 1943 At 0130 A/27, in approximate position 45°42'N, 05°42'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted a distant white light. Ultimatum closed to investigate.
At 0213 A/27, the white light was identiefied as a tunny fishing vessel which was proceeding under sail. Ultimatum altered course away and resumed patrol.
At 0715 A/27, a sailing vessel was sighted which was closed. Again this was a French tunny fishing vessel.
At 0837 A/27, HMS Ultimatum resumed patrol. (51)

28 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1216 A/28, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); You are to leave patrol after dark on 30th August and 31st August respectively and proceed dived by day through position 'UA' 46°01'N, 07°01'W to position 'UB' 46°01'N, 12°54'W thence route 'Green' to cross 43°01'N at 0300 A/5 and 0300 A/6 respectively. Daily advance 90 miles. (50)

29 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1028 A/29, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); Curie and HMS Ultimatum proceed after dark today to position 46°48'N, 04°48'W and position 04°15'N, 05°15'W respectively. Leave patrol to comply with my 1216 A/28.
In a signal timed 1033 A/29, the following was signalled to these two submarines; Route 'Green' is course 180° from position 46°01'N, 12°54'W. (50)
30 Aug 1943 At 0848 A/30, in approximate position 46°17'N, 05°14'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted smoke and topmasts of two vessels bearing 116°. Course was altered to investigate.

At 0902 A/30, the vessels appeared to be three trawlers hull down steaming in a single line ahead on an estimated course of 220°. All three were making large quantities of smoke. HMS Ultimatum continued closing at speed.

At 0910 A/30, the trawlers, which could barely be seen through the periscope due to the heavy swell, appeared to have altered course to 180° and may have stopped. Two sailing fishing vessel were now also in sight close to the trawlers.

At 0915 A/30, visibility was decreasing owing to rain and only a high vertical column of smoke was visible. HMS Ultimatum continued to close the sailing vessels which were eventually identified as tunny fishermen with side booms rigged.
At 1005 A/30, HMS Ultimatum resumed patrol at periscope depth. (51)

31 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1529 A/31, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signalled the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville); (1) Cancel route given in para. 1 of my 1216 A/28. (2) Ultimatum passes through position 'UM' 46°00'N, 07°50'E at 2000 A/31 thence 180° to join route 'Gold' in position 44°00'N. 07°50'E thence by route 'Gold' to cross 43°00'N at 2200 A/3. (3) Curie joins route 'Gold' in 46°30'N at 0200 A/1 thence by route 'Gold' to cross 43°00'N at 0500 A/5. (4) Friendly aircraft may operate with flares. They will be informed of your movements. (50)
31 Aug 1943 At 0159 A/31, in position 46°10'N, 05°55'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted a darkened ship. An attack was started.
At 0210 A/31, the vessel was identified as a sailing vessel which was showing a dimmed stern light. HMS Ultimatum altered course away and continued her passage. (51)
1 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 0911 A/1, the Admiralty informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) that several U-boats were reported in her immediate vicinity.
HMS Ultimatum was at that time in approximate position 44°50'N, 07°55'W.
[There were quite a number of U-boat in and off the Bay of Biscay on this day but none in the immediate vicinity of HMS Ultimatum.] (52)

1 Sep 1943 In a signal timed the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar, set the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR); Following is onward route after crossing 43°00'N at 2300 B/3. Proceed though position 38°50'N, 10°01'W. at 1715 B/6th. Then though positions 'U' (36°51'N, 09°13'W at 01(?50)B/8, 'V' (36° 37'N, 07°24'W at 0200 B/9, 'X' 35°53'N, 06°15'W at 1140 B/9, 'T' (35°49'N, 06°06'W) at 1230 B/9, thence by Q.L.A. 87 [no idea what this means !] to Gibraltar.
