Speed of advance, diving by day, 88 miles per day. Do not cross Meridian 07°00'W before 0630 B/9 but thereafter proceed on surface at your discretion at 9 knots. Submerged bombing restrictions arranged until 0200 B/9 thereafter total bombing restrictions 10 miles either side of your route from 35 miles ahead, to 35 miles astern of your estimated position. Report if unable to keep within your bombing restrictions. Note all times mentioned are zone minus 2 (B). (53)

3 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 0954 B/3, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville) were informed of the following by the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar; U-boat attacked by aircraft in position 42°32'N, 10°09'W at 0545 B/3. Enemy course had been 090°, speed 10 knots.
This referred to U-600 which had been attacked by Wellington 'J' of 179 Squadron of the RAF. (53)

5 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1022 B/5, the Flag Officer in Command Gibraltar, informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and Curie (Lt. P.M. Sonneville) of the following; Aircraft sighted a submarine in position 41°14'N, 11°02'W. Enemy course was 020° at 14 knots at 0059 B/5. This submarine was not attacked.
It was good that this 'enemy submarine' was not attacked as it was in fact HMS Ultimatum.
The patrol report of Ultimatum gives the following (note the one hour time zone difference); 0006 A/5 - An aircraft burning a blue searchlight illuminated the submarine. Dived to 90 feet.
0020 A/5 - Came to periscope depth. Set radar watch.
0032 A/5 - Surfaced and continued passage while maintaining radar watch. Resumed zigzagging. (54)
9 Sep 1943 Around 1550 A/9, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 17th war patrol at Gibraltar. (27)
16 Sep 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted exercises with the harbour defences of Gibraltar. (55)
18 Sep 1943 At 1615 B/18, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Gibraltar for her 18th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Lions.
Between 1645 B/18 and 2300 B/18 exercises were carried out on completion of which HMS Ultimatum proceeded on patrol.

HMS Ultimatum was to proceed through positions 'P' (180° - Cape Calaburras - 10 miles) at 0220 B/19, 'Q' (180° - Cape de Gata - 10 miles) at 1630 B/19 and 'R'(135° - Cape Palos - 10 miles) at 0430 B/20. Further routing was ordered as follows by the Capt. S.8.; Position 38°32'N, 00°36'E at 1500 B/20, 39°47'N, 01°11'E at 0115 B/21, 42°08'N, 04°54'E at 0430 B/22, thence diving by day through 42°30'N, 05°30'E to patrol between 05°00'E and 06°30'E North of 42°30'N. Do not patrol inside 100 fathom line unless absence of mined is indicated by movements of shipping. Land targets in France were not to be attacked.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Ultimatum during this patrol see the map below.

19 Sep 1943 At 1200 B/19, in position 36°31'N, 02°39'W, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted a Spanish merchant vessel proceeding on course 260°. HMS Ultimatum altered course away to avoid being sighted.
At 2030 B/19, in approximate position 36°49'N, 01°35'W, a lighted merchant vessel was sighted on which a dummy attack was started.
At 2045 B/19, this vessel was seen to wearing most likely a Portugese ensign. She was also showing a white flag with a red cross. Course of this vessel was 240°.
At 2300 B/19, the Swiss merchant vessel Alqula (????) was sighted steering 240°. She was wearing a lighted national flag and the name 'Alqula' was illuminated beneath the funnel. (27)

21 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1630 B/21, the Capt. S.8 sent the following to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Your patrol area has been extended to 07°00'E. The south-east limits being the line joining 43°00'N, 07°00'E and 42°30'N, 06°30'E. A profitable area for patrol may be in Gulf of Frejus South of Cape Camarat and South of Hyeres Islands. Enemy radar stations are known to be situated in Hyeres Islands and one near Cape Sicie. (56)

25 Sep 1943 At 0340 B/25, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2328 B/24 [or 2338 B/24] which contained the following orders; After 0001 B/27 the eastern limit of your patrol area is 06°01'E. Aréthuse (Lt. J.M. Gouttier) will be operating to the eastward of you. (27)

27 Sep 1943 At 1045 A/27, south of Toulon, in approximate position 42°56'N, 06°01'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted smoke, funnel and mast of a vessel leaving Toulon. This vessel was subsequently identified as an armed trawler which proceeded eastwards out of range. The vessel was painted light grey with a black top to a long thin funnel. A small calibre gun, approximately 3", was mounted on the forecastle. No A/S impulsed was heard.

Perhaps this was the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2216 which conducted a trial off Toulon on this day. On her return to harbour (see below) this vessel was seen to be probably towing a gunnery target which if this was indeed UJ 2216 would make perfect sense as this vessel besides the trial, also carried out gunnery exercises.

At 1326 A/27, while patrolling 4 miles south of Cape Sicie, smoke was sighted to the westward. HMS Ultimatum turned towards and ran in to attack. During the approach gun flashes were observed from the shore and splashes 40 to 50 feet high were seen to the eastward. No gunnery target could be made out. When the land and target had been closed to within 4000 yards it was seen to be a small drifter towing a lighter. Estimated speed of the enemy was 5 knots steering just a quarter of a mile from the shore. Considered that the size of the target did not justify firing a torpedo and divulging the submarine's position so Lt. Kett resumed patrol south of Cape Sicie. The drifter and it's tow were seen to be steaming on towards Toulon.
This was most likely the German auxiliary netlayer Netztender 44 towing a lighter which had departed Marseille at 0910 B/27 and arrived at Toulon at 1645 B/27.

At 1630 A/27, the armed trawler, that had been seen in the morning, was seen to return to harbour. It may have been towing a gunnery target, although the range and swell prevented accurate observation. (57)

29 Sep 1943 At 1338 A/29, off Cassidaigne Reef (south-east of Marseille), in approximate position 43°07'N, 05°30'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a motor torpedo boat bearing 270° at a range of 6 miles. The enemy was proceeding at speed in the direction of Marseilles.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (27)

30 Sep 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) attacked an escorted German convoy of self propelled barges. Two torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained. It looks like one of the torpedoes prematured.

This convoy was made up of three self propelled barges and was escorted by the Auxiliary minesweepers M 6041, M 6043, M 6044, M 6045, M 6046 and M 6047. The convoy departed Marseille around 0715 B/30 to Toulon where it arrived around 1730 B/30. Nothing was reported by the German escorts and it appears they were not aware of the attack despite HMS Ultimatum stating that listening charges were dropped.
1224 A/30 - Sighted a vessel bearing 340°, close to the land. Altered course towards.
1230 A/30 - Next look revealed that target consisted of four large self propelled supply barges escorted by seven armed patrol vessels. Continued to close.

1247 A/30 - In position 43°02'N, 05°44'E started firing a salvo of four torpedoes from very close range. After two torpedoes had been fired a violent explosion shook Ultimatum. One of the torpedoes must have hit. Depth control was lost and Ultimatum started to descend. The remaining two torpedoes of the salvo were not fired.

1250 A/30 - Ultimatum came to rest on the bottom at 160 feet. Both motors were stopped, all machinery was stopped and the ship was shut off for depth charging. Many listening charges were dropped by the enemy for the next 7 hours but no depth charges were dropped during this period.
2000 A/30 - A depth charge was dropped but it was not very close.
2230 A/30 - Started blowing main ballast.
2245 A/30 - Ultimatum came of the bottom.
2300 A/30 - Obtained a good trim at 90 feet. Cleared the area not surfacing until 0120 hours the next day. (57)

30 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1102 A/30, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Cancel my signal timed 2338 A/24. Your patrol area is extended to 07°00'E. The south east limit being the line joining 43°00'N, 07°00'E and 42°30'N, 06°30'E. Leave patrol diving by day through position 42°30'N, 06°17'E at 0600 A/2 and 42°01'N, 06°12'E at 2100 A/2. Thence on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through positions 39°59'N, 05°14'E at 1300 A/3 and 38°02'N, 03°13'E at 0800 A/4 to make rendezvous with the escort into Algiers in position 37°06'N, 03°12'E at 1500 A/4. (58)

1 Oct 1943 At 1500 A/1, while patrolling to the south-east of Marseille, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small vessel steaming to the inside of Riou Island. HMS Ultimatum closed to investigate.

At 1727 A/1, when the range had been reduced to 3000 yards the vessel was seen to be a French pilot cutter. French national marking were painted below her bridge and while letters were painted on the hull. This vessel was considered not worth a torpedo although presenting a sitting target lying stopped 1.5 miles due south of Cassis. It was hoped this vessels presence suggested the arrival or departure of someting bigger to or from Cassis or Ciotat. Nothing however developed and at 1930 A/1, HMS Ultimatum retired to seaward. (27)
2 Oct 1943 At 0200 A/2, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) left patrol to proceed to Algiers.
[No logbook is available of HMS Ultimatum for the month of October 1943. Some details for this month may thus be missing.] (27)
3 Oct 1943 At 1200 A/3, in position 39°48'N, 05°14'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) sighted HMS Seraph (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, OBE, RN) proceeding northwards on the surface.
HMS Ultimatum was dived and was not detected by HMS Seraph. (59)
4 Oct 1943 At 1610 A/4, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 18th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
At 1355 A/4, she made rendezvous with HMS Staffa (T/Lt. R.L. Roat, RNVR) which then escorted her to the harbour. (27)
12 Oct 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) is docked at Algiers to make repairs to the propellers and the Asdic dome. (60)
15 Oct 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) is undocked. (60)
This convoy was assembled near Oversay on 18 October 1943.

It was made up of the following merchant vessels; Amstel (Dutch, 2115 GRT, built 1925), Baron Forbes (British, 3061 GRT, built 1915), Baron Stranraer (British, 3668 GRT, built 1929), Begum (British, 5843 GRT, built 1922), Belgian Seaman (Belgian, 7023 GRT, built 1941), Blairdevon (British, 3282 GRT, built 1925), Cape Hawke (British, 5081 GRT, built 1941), Carlton (British, 7210 GRT, built 1942), Celtic Monarch (British, 5824 GRT, built 1929), Corfirth (British, 1803 GRT, built 1934), Coulmore (British, 3670 GRT, built 1936), Cydonia (British, 3517 GRT, built 1927), Dan-Y-Bryn (British, 5117 GRT, built 1940), Empire Ballad (British, 6700 GRT, built 1942), Empire Cameron (British, 7015 GRT, built 1941), Empire Copperfield (British, 6013 GRT, built 1943), Empire Tern (British, 2479 GRT, built 1919), Eridan (French, 9928 GRT, built 1928), Errington Court (British, 4913 GRT, built 1925), Esneh (British, 1931 GRT, built 1919), Fort Fairford (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943), Fort Liard (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942), Fort Livingstone (British, 7135 GRT, built 1942), Fort Reliance (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Tadoussac (British, 7129 GRT, built 1941), Greathope (British, 2297 GRT, built 1926), Harberton (British, 4585 GRT, built 1930), Lech (Polish, 1568 GRT, built 1934), Ledaal (Norwegian, 3076 GRT, built 1899), Lodestone (British, 4877 GRT, built 1938), Marwarri (British, 8067 GRT, built 1935), Nolisement (British, 5084 GRT, built 1928), Olga E. Embiricos (Greek, 4677 GRT, built 1922), Riverton (British, 7307 GRT, built 1943), Sinnington Court (British, 6910 GRT, built 1928), Stancleeve (British, 5970 GRT, built 1942), Topdalsfjord (Norwegian, 4271 GRT, built 1921) and Wearwood (British, 4597 GRT, built 1930).

On assembly the convoy was escorted by the destroyers HMS Highlander (Cdr. C.W. McMullen, DSC, RN), HMS Walker (Lt.Cdr. A.N. Rowell, RN) and the corvettes HMS Abelia (Lt. R.I. Floris, RNZNR), HMS Asphodel (Lt.Cdr. H.P. Carse, DSC, RNVR) and HMS Pennywort (A/Lt.Cdr. O.G. Stuart, RCNVR). The corvette HMS Clover (Lt. T.E. Fanshawe, RNR) was unable to join them due to defects but sailed later to overtake and join the convoy at sea which she did on 20 October.

The Blairdevon and Fort Livingstone collided in position 55°39'N, 07°21'W, not long after assembly of the convoy. Both ships being heavily damaged and the Blairdevon was even in a sinking conditiom but both ships were successfully towed to Moville where they arrived on 19 October. HMS Pennywort remained behind with the damaged ships but later rejoined the convoy.

The convoy encountered heavy weather conditions and several ships were forced to turn back, these were; Amstel, Baron Forbes, Begum, Empire Ballad and Nolisement. The Begum had to be towed back as she had lost her propeller.

On 23 October 1943, the merchant vessels Corfell (British, 1802 GRT, built 1934), Scottish Monarch (British, 7004 GRT, built 1943) departed Ponta Delgada, Azores to join the convoy around 1200 Z/26 in position 41°53'N, 18°30'W. They were escorted by the destroyer HMS Viscount (Lt.Cdr. L.E. Woodhouse, RN) which was not to join the convoy.
From 26 to 28 October 1943, HMS Warwick and HMS Asphodel were not with the convoy as they had to fuel at Ponta Delgada and then rejoin the convoy.

During their absence the destroyer HMS Warwick (Cdr. Y.M. Cleeves, DSO, DSC, RD, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Buttercup (T/Lt. R.J. Jonckheere, RNR) and HMS Lavender (Lt. L.G. Pilcher, RNR) were with the convoy.

Also joining the convoy were the destroyers HMS Active (Lt.Cdr. P.G. Merriman, DSC, RN) and HMS Brilliant (Lt.Cdr. J. Smallwood, RN). They had departed Gibraltar on 27 October with orders to join the convoy around 1800 A/28 in position 37°37'N, 18°00'W. On these destroyers the three escorts from the Azores were then to part company and return to the Azores.

The convoy arrived off Gibraltar during the night of 31 October / 1 November 1943. The Corfirth arrived at Horta on 3 November after having been detached from the convoy. [or straggled from the convoy ?.] The Cydonia arrived at Lisbon on 1 November after having been detached from the convoy.

The following ships were detached to Gibraltar; Cape Hawke, Carlton, Corfell, Dan-Y-Bryn, Empire Copperfield, Esneh, Fort Tadoussac, Greathope, Lech, Olga E. Embiricos, Scottish Monarch and Stancleeve.

The original escort also proceeded to Gibraltar and was replaced by a new escort made up of the AA cruiser HMS Colombo (Capt. D.H. Hall-Thompson, RN), escort destroyer HMS Haydon (Lt.Cdr. R.C. Watkin, RN), frigate HMS Barle (Lt.Cdr. A.H. Davies, RNVR), corvettes HMS Bryony (T/Lt. T. Hand, RNR), HMS La Malouine (Lt. W.A. Ives, RNR) and the A/S trawler L'Ajacienne.

The following merchant ships joined the convoy off Gibraltar; Benjamin Huntington (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), British Merit (British (tanker), 8093 GRT, built 1942), Empire Derwent (British, 4026 GRT, built 1930), Empire Trent (British, 5006 GRT, built 1927), F.T. Freylinghuysen (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), George Bancroft (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), John Mary Odin (American, 7244 GRT, built 1943), Jonathan Trumbull (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Joseph Warren (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Montaigne (French, 2770 GRT, built 1920), Nemours (French, 673 GRT, built 1935), Norjerv (Norwegian, 5582 GRT, 1919), Orminster (British, 5712 GRT, built 1914), Puck (Polish, 1065 GRT, built 1935), Wearfield (British (tanker), 9795 GRT, built 1943), William B. Woods (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and William Coddington (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943).

On 2 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Oran; Coulmore, Empire Tern, George Bancroft, Joseph Warren and Montaigne while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Oran; Alexander Graham Bell (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Augustus S. Merrimon (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Caid (British, 255 GRT, built 1906), Fort de Douaumont (French, 5266 GRT, built 1918), Francis L. Lee (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), George B. McClellan (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), George M. Bibb (American, 7180 GRT, built 1943), Grand Quevilly (French, 2844 GRT, built 1914), Joseph Hewes (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Kemp P. Battle (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Kosciuszko (Polish, 6852 GRT, built 1915), Leland Stanford (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Mont Viso (French, 4531 GRT, built 1921), Robert Dale Owen (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Robert F. Stockton (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Selvik (Norwegian, 1557 GRT, built 1920), William C. Endicott (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), William F. Cody (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and Zebulon Pike (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942). The minesweeper HMS BYMS 2186 (?) also joined the convoy.

Around 0051 A/3, the merchant vessel Mont Viso was torpedoed and sunk by the German submarine U-593. HMS Bryony went to the assistance of the torpedoed ship but it sank in two minutes. The corvette then picked up the survivors and later obtained an asdic contact on the enemy and attacked with depth charges during which the German submarine sustained damage due to which she was to return to Toulon for repairs.

Around 0900 A/3, HMS Colombo was detached to return to Gibraltar. Between around 1100 A/3 and 1410 A/3 she was in the area where the Mont Viso had been sunk searching for survivors but none were found. She arrived at Gibraltar around 0805 A/4.

On 3 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Algiers; Belgian Seaman, Caio, Empire Trent, Eridan, Fort Fairford, Fort Reliance, Lodestone, Orminster and Topsdaldjord as was HMS Haydon, while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Algiers; Anna Capano (Italian, 1345 GRT, built 1938), Chateau Pavie (French, 2047 GRT, built 1930), Empire Daring (British, 7059 GRT, built 1943), George C. Childress (American, 7245 GRT, built 1943), Lars Kruse (British, 1807 GRT, built 1923), Letitia (British, 13595 GRT, built 1925), Pan-Delaware (American (tanker), 8128 GRT, built 1918), Richmond Mumford Pearson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and Ronan (British, 1489 GRT, built 1938). HMS Bryony also made a short stop at Oran to land the survivors of the Mont Viso before rejoining the convoy. The corvette HMS Bergamot (Lt. R.T. Horan, RNR) also joined the convoy off Algiers as did the submarines HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN).
On 4 November 1943, the Empire Daring arrived at Bougie after having been detached from the convoy.

On 4 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Philippeville; George C. Childress, Letitia and Sinnington Court while the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Philippeville; Afghanistan (British, 6992 GRT, built 1940), Cap Padaran (British (former French), 8009 GRT, built 1922), Fort Fork (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Lawrence (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943), Norman Monarch (British, 7005 GRT, built 1943) and Prometheus (British, 6095 GRT, built 1925).

On 4 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Bone; Empire Derwent, F.T. Freylinghuysen, John Mary Odin, Jonathan Trumbull, William B. Woods and William Coddington while the following merchant vessel joined the convoy coming from Bone; Yenangyaung (British, 5447 GRT, 1937).

On 5 November 1943 the following ships were detached to Bizerta; Anna Capano, George M. Bibb, Grand Quevilly, Kemp P. Battle and Wearfield while the following merchant vessel joined the convoy coming from Bizerta; Spero (Norwegian, 3619 GRT, built 1919).
On 5 November 1943 the Errington Court was detached to Bizerta.
On 6 November 1943 the Fort Liard, Puck and Selvik were detached to Malta as were HMS Ultimatum, HMS Ultor and HMS BYMS 2186.

On 6 November 1943 the Afghanistan, Alexander Graham Bell, Augustus S. Merrimon, Baron Stranraer, Benjamin Huntington, British Merit, Cap Padaran, Celtic Monarch, Empire Cameron, Fort de Douaumont, Fort Fork, Fort Lawrence, Francis L. Lee, George B. McClellan, Harberton, Joseph Hewes, Kosciuszko, Lars Kruse, Leland Stanford, Norman Monarch, Pan-Delaware, Prometheus, Richmond Mumford Pearson, Riverton, Robert Dale Owen, Robert F. Stockton, Ronan, Wearwood, William C. Endicott, William F. Cody and Zebulon Pike were detached to Augusta while the following merchant vessel joined the convoy coming from Augusta; Brighton (British, 7345 GRT, built 1943), City of Windsor (British, 7218 GRT, built 1923), Comliebank (British, 5149 GRT, built 1924), Fort la Traite (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Pembina (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Newbrough (British, 5255 GRT, built 1941), Pellicula (British (tanker), 6254 GRT, built 1936) and Talma (British, 10000 GRT, built 1923).

On 11 November 1943 the Brighton, City of Windsor, Comliebank, Fort la Traite, Fort Pembina, Norjerv, Talma and Yenangyaung arrived at Alexandria after having been detached from the convoy. They took HMS Bergamot and HMS Bryony with them.
The remainder of the convoy arrived at Port Said also on 11 November escorted by HMS Barle and HMS La Malouine. HMS Barle then continued on toward Haifa where she arrived on the 12th.
19 Oct 1943 At 1640 A/19, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Algiers for her 19th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Southern France in area 'ST'.

She was routed as follows; Through positions 38°01'N, 03°21'E at 0130 A/20, 40°02'N, 05°15'E at 1900 A/20, 41°59'N, 06°12'E at 1930 A/21 and 42° 30'N, 06°16'E at 0001 A/22 to patrol in zone 'ST'. Proceed on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion on 19th and 20th there after diving by day.
22 Oct 1943 In a signal timed 1002 A/22, The Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); You are to operate in zones 'SU' and 'ST' forthwith until 0100 A/25. (62)

24 Oct 1943 At 1735 A/24, while patrolling near Toulon, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted seven F-boats, painted brown, steaming towards Toulon. They were steaming widely spaced in single line ahead and appeared to have approached through the Little Pass between Porquerolles and Giens Peninsula.
[We have been unable to identify these lighters. It is doubtful these were F-boats though as none should have arrived at Toulon on this day.] (27)

26 Oct 1943 At 0230 A/26, while patrolling near Toulon, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a brightly illuminated ship approaching from the direction of Cape Sicie. HMS Ultimatum closed to investigate.

At 0307 A/26, the vessel was observed to be a German repatriation ship with a grey or white hull, and the word 'Freigeleite' painted on her side between two Swastikas on a red background in a white circle. As this vessel was known to be present in the area with the exception that she appeared to have sailed from Toulon instead of Marseille to approach was broken off and HMS Ultimatum dived. The vessels estimated course was 195° at 12 knots.
At 0319 A/26, HMS Ultimatum surfaced and resumed patrol.

Several hospital ships were in the area; The German hospital ship Djenné (Former French, 8790 GRT, built 1931) which had departed Marseille at midnight during the night of 25/26 October 1943 bound for Barcelona where she arrived at 1400 B/26. The German hospital ship Aquileia (former Italian, 9448 GRT, built 1914) sailed from Marseille at 1430 B/25 [she had arrived at Marseille at 1030 B/24 from Oran) and arrived at Barcelona at 2230 B/26. A third hospital ship, the German Gradisca (former Italian, 13868 GRT, built 1913) was at Marseille and was to sail on the 28th for Patras. We do not have information on her arrival there. Perhaps she was the one sighted ?

Djenné sailed from Marseille at 2400 B/25 and arrived at Barcelona at 1400 B/26, hospital ship Aquileia sailed from Marseille at 1430 B/25 [she had arrived at Marseille at 1030 B/24 from Oran) and arrived at Barcelona at 2230 B/26. A third hospital ship Gradisca was at Marseille and was to sail on the 28th for Patras. I do not have information on her arrival there. Perhaps she was the one?

At 1300 A/26, a small trawler was seen in Toulon Bay. She had a white funnel with one black band, yellow deck houses and a black hull. Shortly afterwards the trawler was seen to proceed towards the harbour.
At 1415 A/26, the trawler reappeared and seemed to be patrolling in Toulon Bay.

Around this time the auxiliary minesweeper M 6004 arrived at Toulon after having escorted a small convoy made up of a small harbour defence boat and three barges (convoy 5361). They came from Le Ciotat which they had departed around 0700 A/26. The trawler sighted may thus have been M 6004 which arrived at Toulon around 1345 A/26. She had parted company with the small convoy at 1300 B/26 which then also proceeded into Toulon.

At 1720 A/26, HMS Ultimatum sighted what was thought to be the conning tower of a U-boat leaving Toulon. Range was 7 miles. Lt. Kett altered course to close on a 120° track and ran in at full speed. The enemy's estimated course was 130°. The target was against the land and in fading daylight and shortly afterwards could no longer be seen but HE could be picked up on the correct bearing.
At 1738 A/26, the attack was broken off as the range could not be closed sufficiently and Lt. Kett would not fire torpedoes from long range using an Asdic bearing only.
This must have been U-593 departing Toulon for a patrol near Algiers around 1715 A/26 escorted out by the auxiliary minesweepers M 6001, M 6002 and M 6005.
At 2000 A/26, HMS Ultimatum picked up HE of a fast motorboat on bearing 095°.
At 2020 A/26, the HE increased while the bearing remained steady.
At 2040 A/26, the HE faded while the bearing remained steady. Eight minutes later no more HE was heard and HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 42°51'N, 06°00'E. (63)

27 Oct 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) attacked a German convoy made up of the Danish transport (under German control) Nicoline Maersk (4194 GRT, built 1925), German transport Canosa (former Italian, former French, 3823 GRT, built 1936) and the small German tanker Arpino (former Italian, former French, 584 GRT, built 1935). They were escorted by the German patrol vessel SG 15, auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2210, UJ 2211 and the German motor minesweepers R 10, R 14, R 15. No hits were obtained.
This convoy had departed Genoa around 0830 A/26 bound for Marseille where the entire convoy arrived around 1635 A/27.
No ships of the convoy entered Toulon though despite Lt. Kett thinking this was the case.
0855 A/27 - HE was reported ahead.
0905 A/27 - Sighted a motor torpedo boat approaching at speed. Took evasive action. The enemy passed close down the starboard side.
0920 A/27 - The enemy returned now passing close down the port side.
0931 A/27 - The motor torpedo boat dropped listening charges.
0940 A/27 - Sighted a second motor torpedo boat and also a flying boat patrolling low over the water.
0945 A/27 - Went to 80 feet.
0955 A/27 - HE now became very loud, passed close by and drew aft. The motor torpedo boats continued to patrol quite close until at ...
1040 A/27 - The enemy's HE faded out in the direction of Toulon so returned to periscope depth.
1110 A/27 - Heard A/S impulses ahead but sighted nothing.
1120 A/27 - Heard HE ahead. Several barrage balloons were sighted. Closed at speed.
1144 A/27 - Sighted a convoy of four or five ships. Range was about 8 nautical miles. They were making for Toulon.

1154 A/27 - Two merchant vessels escorted by two trawlers were seen to have left the convoy which was then entering Toulon Bay. The two merchant vessels and two trawlers which had split off proceeded to the westward, apparently towards Marseille. Ran in to attack one of these two merchant ships.
1219 A/27 - In approximate position 42°58'N, 05°56'E fired four torpedoes at the second merchant ship from 7000 yards. Went deep on firing.
1225 A/27 - Heard a torpedo explosion.
1228 A/27 - Heard the other three torpedoes explode against the land.
1229 A/27 - Heard the A/S trawlers hunting.
1240 A/27 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted only the two trawlers and the first merchant vessel of the line. Assumed the other ship to have sunk. Retired to seaward to reload. (64)
28 Oct 1943 At 0705 A/28, while patrolling off Toulon, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) heard HE bearing 095°.
At 0708 A/28, two motor torpedo boats passed very close by and proceeded at speed into Toulon harbour.
The were probably either S 58 and S 60 which arrived at Toulon from Italy at 0730 A/28 or S 57 and S 153 which arrived at Toulon five minutes after the first pair.
At 0830 A/28, two motor torpedo boats were sighted leaving Toulon and proceeding to seaward passing very close to HMS Ultimatum.
[We have been unable to identify these two 'motor torpedo boats']
At 1000 A/28, two armed trawlers were seen to leave Toulon and then commence to patrol to the east of Cape Cepet.
These were the German auxiliary minesweepers M 6004 and M 6005 which left Toulon around this time.
At 1100 A/28, a two-funneled merchant vessel was sighted bearing 210° at a range of 6 miles. Course was altered to close.
At 1125 A/28, the course of the merchant vessel was estimated to be 335° at 12 knots. HMS Ultimatum went to 80 feet to close as an aircraft was seen patrolling the area shortly before.

At 1133 A/28, back at periscope depth the merchant vessel was identified as a German repatriation vessel, thought to be either Djenne (Former French, 8790 GRT, built 1931) or Sinaia (Former French, 8567 GRT, built 1922), although neither vessel was expected. After having closed the land in the vicinity of Cape Sicie the vessel altered course and proceeded to Marseilles. The attack was broken off.

At 1220 A/28, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0945 A/28 was received which reported the German hospital / repatriation vessel Djenne being in the vicinity. [The vessel sighted was indeed Djenne which arrived at Marseille around 1420 A/28. M 6004 and M 6005 had been ordered to meet her.]
At 1233 A/28, a small naval drifter passed close by HMS Ultimatum proceeding eastwards.

At 1305 A/28, an armed trawler was seen bearing 334° patrolling to the south of Cape Cepet. She was dropping listening charges at frequent intervals. The last listening charge was heard to be dropped at 1550 A/28.
At 1830 A/28, a lighted ship was seen approaching from seaward bearing 211°.

At 1900 A/28, this vessel was identified as being the German hospital / repatriation ship Aquilea (former Italian, 9448 GRT, built 1914). Ten minutes later this ship was seen to be signalling to the shore after which she altered course and proceeded towards Marseilles where she arrived around 0200 A/29 escorted by M 6005.
[M 6004 and M 6005 had again left Toulon around 1830 A/28 to meet Aquilea. M 6004 arrived back at Toulon at 2000 A/28. They were apparently not sighted by HMS Ultimatum.]
At 2028 A/28, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0952 A/28 was receiving in which area 'SU' was extended to 06°10'E.
At 2140 A/28, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 42°57'N, 06°00'E and commenced a battery charge.

At 2151 A/28, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1302 A/28 was received which contained the following orders; Leaves patrol proceeding diving by day on 31 October thence proceed on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through positions 42°30'N, 05°21'E at 2330 A/30, 41°53'N, 06°09'E at 0700 A/31, 40°01'N, 05°16'E at 0730 A/1, 38°02'N, 03°18'E at 0230 A/2 to rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 37°04'N. 03°11'E at 0930 A/2. (65)
29 Oct 1943 At 0853 A/29, while patrolling south of Toulon, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a vessel approaching through the Little Pass. Commenced attack.
At 0915 A/29, the vessel was identified as a small naval drifter towing a lighter.

At 0920 A/29, the attack was broken off as the lighter, a steel construction of about 200 tons, was seen to be empty. The drifter and lighter proceeded to the westward south of Cape Sicie on an estimated course of 284° at 7 knots.
[We have been unable to identify this 'naval drifter towing a lighter'.] (27)

30 Oct 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) attacked a German U-boat to the southeast of Toulon, France, in position 43°04'N, 05°57'E. This attack was against U-73 but it inflicted no damage. U-73 moored in Toulon harbour at 0815 A/30 and was apparently unaware of the attack.

HMS Ultimatum is often credited to have sunk U-431 in this attack. However U-431 was sunk with all hands on 21 October 1943 in the Mediterranean off Algiers in position 37°23'N, 00°35'E, by depth charges from a British Wellington aircraft (Sqdn. 179/Z). U-431's fate was revised in November 1987 by the Foreign Documents Section (FDS) of the British Ministry of Defence (Naval Historical Branch / NHB).
[We could find nothing in German records of depth charges being dropped after this failed attack. Also there is no mention in the KTB (Kriegstagebuch) of U-73 which states she was attacked.]
0707 A/30 - Sighted a 500 tons U-boat bound for Toulon. Closed to attack.
0716 A/30 - Fired three torpedoes from 7000 yards. A fourth torpedo could not be fired as one of the torpedo tube was defective.

0721 A/30 - One torpedo explosion thought to be a hit on the target giving a range of 6600 yards. The U-boats HE ceased and could not be seen when the periscope was raised it is thought the submarine was sunk.
0723 A/30 - Retired from the scene at 80 feet.
0930 A/30 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight except a patrolling aircraft.
0950 A/30 - Went to 80 feet to reload the remaining torpedo.

1015 A/30 - Two sources of HE was reported to be approaching on a steady bearing. No A/S transmissions were heard. A/S vessels were probably hunting, frequently stopping and listening, and then coming in at speed.
1038 A/30 - One vessel passed fairly close to starboard so kept stern in at 180 feet until she had passed clear.
1050 A/30 - All HE had been lost so attempted to come up to periscope depth to have a look but the slightest noise resulted in renewed HE so remained deep instead.
1141 A/30 - A depth charge was dropped a fair distance off. From then until 1409 A/30 twelve patterns were dropped for a total of about thirty depth charges. All a long way off though.
1202 A/30 - Returned to periscope depth to find noting in sight. The reload had also been completed.
1600 A/30 - Left patrol and set course to comply with the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1302 A/28. (27)
2 Nov 1943 Around 0845 A/2, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 19th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
At 0615 A/2, she made rendezvous with HMS Staffa (T/Lt. R.L. Roat, RNVR) which then escorted her to the harbour. (27)
3 Nov 1943 At 0745 A/3, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Algiers for passage to Malta. She made the passage in convoy KMS 30 together with HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN).
[For more info on this convoy, see the event ' Convoy KMS 30 ' for 18 October 1943.]
For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during this passage see the map below.
6 Nov 1943 At 2240 A/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Malta to join the 10th submarine flotilla.
At Malta she was taken in hand for repairs. (27)
21 Nov 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. (66)
24 Nov 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was docked in No. 1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard.
She was docked together with HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN). (66)
4 Dec 1943 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) are undocked. (66)
4 Jan 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) completed repairs at Malta. (67)
5 Jan 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted compass adjustment trials at Malta. (67)
6 Jan 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted independent exercises off Malta. (67)
8 Jan 1944 Around 1000 A/8, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Malta for Beirut where she was to join the 1st Submarine Flotilla.

She was routes as follows; Through position 35°49'N, 15°06'E at 1358 A/8 thence proceeding independently on the surface to position 34°43'N, 20°02'E at 2001 A/9 thence diving by day to position 34°15'N, 25°01'E at 2010 A/11th for onward routeing to Beirut which will be signalled by the Capt. S.1. Speed of advance, 200 miles every 24 hours when on the surface and 120 miles every 24 hours when dived by day.

In a signal timed 1106 B/8 the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders; From position 34°15'N, 25°01'E proceed on surface at 8 knots through position 34°05'N, 29°59'E at 0232 B/13 to arrive at Beirut at 1031 B/14.
She was escorted out until 1313 A/8 by HMS Brave (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.S. Tolliday, RNR). (27)
8 Jan 1944 For the daily positions of HMS Ultimatum during the passage from Malta to Beirut see the map below.
14 Jan 1944 Around 1115 B/14, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Beirut from Malta. (27)
17 Jan 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted noise trials of Beirut. (67)
19 Jan 1944 Around 0715 B/19, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Beirut for Port Said where she was to be docked for a propeller change.
At 1155 B/19, she received a signal ordering her to return to Beirut where she arrived around 1655 B/19. (67)
23 Jan 1944 Around 0715 B/23, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Beirut for Port Said where she was to be docked for a propeller change. (67)
24 Jan 1944 Around 1045 B/24, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) arrived at Port Said where she was immediately docked in the floating dock. (67)

26 Jan 1944 At 1630 B/26, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was undocked. She then departed Port Said for her 20th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in south-east Aegean. She was clear of the swept channel around 2000 B/26.
28 Jan 1944 In the evening HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) makes the northbound passage of the Kaso Strait on the surface.
In a signal timed 1953 B/28, the Capt. S.1 ordered her to patrol between Milos and Santorini. Course was set accordingly around 2300 B/28 when clear off Kaso Stait. (68)
30 Jan 1944 At 2123 B/30, south of Milos, in approximate position 36°34'N, 24°37'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a dark shape ahead and up moon.
At 2124 B/30, an aircraft was heard very close. HMS Ultimatum dived.
At 2125 B/30, while diving the aircraft passed overhead.
At 2133 B/30, HE was picked up and also heard A/S transmissions. Closed to attack. It appeared the enemy was moving northwards.

At 2138 B/30, A/S transmissions were very loud but nothing could be seen through the periscope. Re-estimated the target was proceeding to the south-east at a speed (by counting the revolutions) of 10 knots. Altered course to 196° and closed at 4 knots.
At 2146 B/30, a dark shape was seen bearing 146°. Lt. Kett altered course towards and continued to close.'
At 2149 B/30, the gyro compass failed.

At 2153 B/30, despite the bright moonlight the identity of the target could not be established. The target was turbine engined so may have been a destroyer or torpedo boat. HE was very loud and it appeared to be very close.
At 2155 B/30, numerous red flares were dropped.
At 2300 B/30, HMS Ultimatum surfaced. The attack had already been broken off as it was not possible to close the enemy submerged.

From signals received from aircraft between 2324 B/30 and 0027 B/31 it was considered that this had been a convoy, of one or two merchant vessels and three escorts proceeding on course 090° at a speed of 12 knots.

Most likely HMS Ultimatum had encountered the convoy made up of the German transport Oria (2127 GRT, built 1920, former French Sainte Julienne, former Norwegian Oria). She was escorted by the auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2105, UJ 2106 and UJ 2110. This convoy had departed Piraeus around 1830 A/29 bound for Rhodos via Milos and Stampalia.
The convoy had arrived at Milos around 0730 A/30 and departed again around 1515 A/30.

In the evening the convoy, at 2120 B/30 the convoy was detected by enemy aircraft so it seems that the aircraft which were noticed by HMS Ultimatum were allied and not German. Shortly afterwards the escort also noticed that an Allied submarine was in the area. UJ 2106 and the Oria proceeded close inshore to Folegandros to evade while the other two UJ boats remained further from the shore to draw the attention of the submarine on them. The Germans were under the impression that this tactic worked.
At 0720 A/31, the convoy was attacked by a submarine. This was HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) which had fired three torpedoes but all missed.
At 1300 A/31, the convoy anchored off Stampalia.
At 0200 A/1, UJ 2105 and UJ 2110 got underway for an A/S hunt. One hour later UJ 2106 and Oria also got underway.
Around 1600 A/1, UJ 2106 and Oria entered Rhodos harbour while the other two UJ boats remained outside for an A/S hunt.
At 1725 A/1, UJ 2106 departed Rhodos and joined the other two UJ-boats after which they set course for Leros. They arrived at Portolago (Lakki) around 0715 A/2 for convoy duty. (69)

1 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1058 B/1, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN); Assume patrols in approaches to Suda Bay by 0401 B/3 as follows; (1) Ultimatum to the west of a line 27° from position 35°30'N, 24°14'E. (2) Sportsman to the east of this line. (70)

1 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1548 B/1, The Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and RHS Papanikolis (Lt. N. Roussen). (1) Ultimatum is to be south and west of position 36°15'N, 24°25'E by dawn/2 thence proceed to patrol as in my signal timed 1058 A/1. (2) Papanicolis is not to enter her patrol area until after dawn/2. (70)

1 Feb 1944 At 2300 B/1, south of Milos, sighted an enemy vessel bearing 220° at an estimated range of 10 miles. The vessel was to the left of the moon and appeared to be long and low. Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) turned on a parallel course and increased to full speed in an effort to get ahead and up moon of the enemy.

At 0030 B/2, the enemy's bearing drew slowly aft. Course was adjusted to close on a 160° track. Speed of the enemy was estimated as being 12 knots. With the very bright moonlight conditions prevailing and a fairly light horizon behind Ultimatum Lt. Kett did not want to close too fast on a broad track until the moon conditions were more favourable.
At 0100 B/1, the bearing of the enemy was now steady. Ultimatum was slowly closing the range.
At 0130 B/1, the moon set behind a bank of low cumulus cloud leaving a very dark horizon. Visual touch was lost with the enemy.
By 0200 B/1, there was still no visual contact so Lt. Kett stopped both motors and generators and set A/S listing watch.
At 0205 B/1, faint HE was picked up bearing green 040° and the chase was resumed.

By 0240 B/1, the enemy had still not been sighted so once again HMS Ultimatum stopped and A/S watch was set but no HE could be picked up. Also radar did not obtain contact. Ultimatum then proceeded south for 15 minutes but there was no sign of the enemy.

At 0300 B/1, the loom of lights was observed from the direction of Suda Bay and it looked like that arrangements had been made for the enemy vessel to proceed straight into harbour. The chase was now broken off as there was no chance at all to catch the enemy in time due to the slow surface speed of the U-class submarines. Course was set for the Anti-Kithera channel, hoping to intercept enemy traffic there.

The vessel sighted may have been the small German transport Gisela (211 GRT, built 1939) which was en-route from Piraeus via Milos to Suda Bay. She arrived at Iraklion early in the afternoon of the 2nd most likely she was diverted as the harbour entrance at Suda Bay was closed as it was suspected that the Allies had laid mines in the area. (71)

2 Feb 1944 At 1215 B/2, Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2359 B/1, which contained the following orders; Leave patrol forthwith passing through position 35°54'N, 24°48'E about 1801 B/2 thence Sophrana Island and through Scarpanto Strait to arrive in position 35°37'N, 27°37'E at 0201 B/4. Thence on the surface at 8 knots through position 34°03'N, 31°53'E to arrive at Beirut at 0701 B/6.
HMS Ultimatum was recalled from patrol early as apparently some of her patrol orders and arrangements, like bombing restictions, had been compromised. (72)

4 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1954 B/4, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; U-Boat sighted in position 34°13'N, 33°23'E (south of Cyprus) at 0533 B/4. Aircraft and surface forces will be carrying out a search in vicinity during daylight tomorrow. Total bombing restrictions are in force for your route.
This must have referred to U-453. The trawler HMS Islay (A/Skr.Lt. O. Jinks, RNR), whaler HMSAS Southern Maid and trawler FFS Reine des Flots were ordered to investigate the area but found nothing.

The next day, the 5th, the destroyers HMS Quilliam (Capt. R.G. Onslow, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Queenborough (Cdr. E.P. Hinton, DSO and Bar, MVO, RN) and HMS Raider (Lt.Cdr. K.W. Michell, RN) departed Alexandria around noon for an A/S hunt as a U-boat had been suspected by direction finding at 0515 B/5 in approximate position 34°00'N, 31°00'E. They were recalled on the 6th and returned to harbour around 1540 B/6. (73)
6 Feb 1944 At 0710 B/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 20th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut. (27)
20 Feb 1944 At 1800 A/20, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Beirut for her 21st war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Beirut. She was ordered to patrol in the northern Aegean.

She was to pass Kaso Strait during the night of 22/23 February thence proceed to the north east of Sirina and Stampalia and west of Skyros to patrol an area bound by 39°40'N, the coast of Greece, the Skopelos Islands and 24°00'E. The object of the patrol is destruction of Piraeus / Salonika traffic. Allied MTB's and Levant schooner force caiques may also be operating in the patrol area. On completion of the patrol it was the intention to recall you to arrive at Malta on 8 March.

23 Feb 1944 In a signal timed 1816 A/23, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unsparing (Lt. A.D. Piper, DSC and 2 bars, RNR) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Unsparing remains north of 39°32'N from dusk/25 until 0001 A/26th and then leaves patrol passing east of Kaloveri Rocks and by the southbound as in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1459 B/2(January) [unable to find this signal and this route] to arrive in position 35°12'N, 26°43'E at 0102 A/29. Further routeing signal will follow. Ultimatum enters patrol area about 2001 A/25 remaining south of latitude 39°32'N until dawn/26. Note all times are zone A (-1). (74)

25 Feb 1944 At 2100 B/25, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) entered her patrol area. She proceeded towards the Skiathos Channel to intercept enemy vessels expected to leave Salonika for Piraeus and also an enemy vessel bound for Salonika. (27)
26 Feb 1944 At 1350 B/26, while patrolling north of Skiathos, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small caique inside the Skiathos Channel.

At 1415 B/26, another small caique, of about 15 tons, was seen steering for the Skopelos Channel on course 100°. Lt. Kett did not want to compromise his position so early in the patrol so decided not to attack these small caiques during daylight. (27)
27 Feb 1944 At 0700 B/27, while patrolling north of Skiathos, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small caique leaving the Skopelos Channel steering 015°.
At 0815 B/27, two small caiques, probably fishing vessels, entering the Skopelos Channel steering 170°.
Several very small fishing boats were observed to enter the Skiathos and Skopelos Channels during the day. (27)
28 Feb 1944 At 1510 B/28, while patrolling north of Skiathos, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small caique heading for the Skopelos Channel.

29 Feb 1944 At 0815 B/29, while patrolling north of Skiathos in position 39°16'N, 23°25'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted a small caique bearing 105° at a range of 7 miles. Course of the caique was 190°. (27)

1 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 1631 A/1, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Dolfijn pass through position 39°30'N, 24°01'E at dawn/2 and patrol in an area bounded by latitude 39°40'N, coast of Greece, Skopelos Islands and latitude 24°01'E to intercept Salonika - Piraeus traffic. Ultimatum leaving patrol A.M./2 passing through position 39°40'N, 24°01'E at 0801 A/2 thence north of strait by southbound route and east of Stampalia to arrive in position 35°12'N, 26°43'E at 2301 A/5. (75)
2 Mar 1944 At 0400 B/2, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) left patrol in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1631 A/1. (27)
3 Mar 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) stopped a small caique in position 39°34'N, 25°22'E but then released it after boarding.
0058 B/3 - Sighted a caique. Altered course to close and went to action stations.

0111 B/3 - Opened fire from 1500 yards. After a second round the vessel lowered her sail and exhibited a white light. The target was now seen to be a small sailing boat. Fire was ceased and Ultimatum manoeuvred alongside and a boarding party was put on board. The sailing vessel had a crew of four and the cargo consisted of four bags of potatoes, two bags of onions and four empty drums. The boat was allowed to proceed. (27)
5 Mar 1944 At 0353 B/5, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) fired ten rounds at the Kandeliusa light house for target practice. (27)

7 Mar 1944 At 0245 B/7, south-west of Crete, in position 34°23'N, 23°11'E, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted the Swedish Red Cross ship Fenris (1904 GRT, built 1941) proceeding on course 136° at 13 knots. (27)
10 Mar 1944 Around 1100 B/10, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 21st war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (27)

25 Mar 1944 At 1310 A/25, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Malta for her 22nd war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the south-west Aegean, south of 36°05'N and west of 25°00'E. She was to enter the Aegean through the Anti-Kithera Channel.

27 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 2347 A/27, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; The German transports Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) and Gisela (211 GRT, built 1939) are expected to sail from Piraeus to Crete shortly. (77)

28 Mar 1944 In the morning, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) passed eastbound throught the Anti-Kithera Channel, submerged. On completing the passage course was set to patrol in the approaches to Suda Bay. (27)

30 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 1958 A/30 the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; The German transports Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) moved to Mardon. Probably about to sail for Crete. (77)

31 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 1210 A/31, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN); (1) HMS Ultimatum remain east of longitude 24°00'E from dawn/2 to dawn/3. (2) HMS Ultor after passing through Anti-Kithera Channel proceed to patrol area through position 36°05'N, 23°19'E. (3) HMS Sportsman leave patrol P.M./2. Pass through positions 36°11'N, 23°43'E, 35°50'N, 23°46'E to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°30'E at 2130 A/2. Thence dived at 200 feet to position 35°41'N, 23°15'E. (77)

31 Mar 1944 In a signal timed 1750 A/31, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information to HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; Departure from Salamis of the German transports Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) and Suzanne (former Spanish San Juan II, 552 GRT, built 1919) probably for Crete appears imminent P.M./31. The German tanker Centaur (1267 GRT, built 1915) may possibly sail for Leros. (77)

1 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 0041 A/1, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) of the following; Departure of the German transports Anita (former Italian Arezzo, 1165 GRT, built 1899) for Crete and tanker Centaur (1267 GRT, built 1915) for Rhodes [note: probably Leros was intended] from Salamis imminent. Three caiques ready to leave Suda. (78)
3 Apr 1944 At 0404 A/3, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 35°40'N, 24°16'E and set course to patrol four miles north of Cape Drepano.
At 1015 A/3, what is thought to be a motor torpedo boat was seen to round Cape Tripiti and proceed into Suda Bay. The enemy steamed about one mile off shore at an estimated speed of 12 knots.
Most likely this was an R-boat (motor minesweeper) of which three were at Suda Bay on this day. [No KTB of the 12th R-boat Flotilla is available for this month so no further details are available.]
At 1420 A/3, a schooner rigged caique of about 40 tons was seen to leave Suda Bay.
At 1500 A/3, this caique was seen to round Cape Drepano and proceeding to the eastward.

At 1600 A/3, it was seen that this caique had a green painted hull with a gun abaft the foremast. She appeared to be flying a Nazi ensign. Lt. Kett did not want to compromise his position by surfacing close to Suda Bay in daylight, he decided to keep the caique in sight until after sunset, then surface and engage her with the gun. The enemy's estimated course and speed were 110° at 3.5 knots.
At 1800 A/3, it was realised the caique might be making for Rethymnon.
At 1830 A/3, it was seen that the caique was exchanging signals with a shore station at Rethymnon and it was thought she subsequently entered harbour.
At 1900 A/3, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°25'N, 24°26'E and despite the bright moonlight the caique was not seen.
The caique sighted was most likely the Greek Elly (apparently German controlled) which proceeded from Suda Bay to Rethymnon on this day. (79)

5 Apr 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sank the Greek sailing vessel (in German service) PI 2659 / Agios Georgios with gunfire off Suda Bay, Crete, Greece. The German coastal guns of 10. Battery. M.A.A. 520 opened fire on the submarine which was hit by fragments on the casing.
0400 A/5 - Dived in position 35°38'N, 24°18'E.
0555 A/5- Sighted the mast of a caique bearing 040°. Range was 7 nautical miles. Altered course to intercept.

0642 A/5 - Surfaced in position 35°34'N 24°20'E and opened fire with the 3" gun from 2000 yards. Fired 15 rounds for 10 hits. The target was seen to break up and catch fire aft. The crew of six abandoned ship and pulled away in a small boat.
0645 A/5 - Dived.
0646 A/5 - Shore batteries in the vicinity of Cape Drepano opened fire. Shrapnel was heard to hit the casing.
0555 A/5 - Periscope observation showed that the caique had sunk. Survivors were seen pulling ashore towards Suda Bay at a distance of about 5 miles.
Several enemy aircraft were seen patrolling in the area later in the morning. (80)

5 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 2151 A/5, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) off the following; Two F lighters lying close north-west of Monemvasia A.M. today. [This were F 370 and F 497 which were bound from Crete to Piraeus.]

The transport Sabine (Former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) has been ready to sail from Salamis for several days. Destination Crete or Salonika. In fact Sabine departed Piraeus for Crete at 2000 A/5 together with the transport Suzanne (German, 552 GRT, former Spanish San Juan II, built 1919). They were escorted by TA 19 (former Italian torpedo boat Calatafini), TA 16 (former Italian torpedo boat Castelfidardo), TA 17 (former Italian torpedo boat former Italian San Martino) and the motor minesweeper R 34. The two transports arrived at Suda Bay at 1900 A/6. The escorts immediately returned to Piraeus. TA 17 had parted company with the convoy to hunt a suspected enemy submarine near Milos and was picked up on the way back to Piraeus by the other escorts. (81)
6 Apr 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sank the Greek sailing vessel (in German service) PI 2326 / Agios Nikolaos with gunfire off Anti-Kithera, Greece.
0408 A/6 - Dived in position 35°54'N, 23°26'E and commenced to patrol towards Patamo Bay (Anti-Kithera Island).
0630 A/6 - Sighted a caique bearing 290°. Range was 6 nautical miles. Closed for gun action.
0651 A/6 - Surfaced in position 35°53'N, 23°20'E and opened fire with the 3" gun from 1000 yards. Fired 11 rounds for 7 hits. the crew of 3 abandoned ship in their small boat. The caique soon sank.
7 Apr 1944 At 0430 A/7, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 35°36'N, 24°19'E.
At 0830 A/7, a two-masted caique was seen coast crawling from the direction of Rethymnon towards Suda.
At 0900 A/7, the caique was lost from sight against the land. Periscope visibility was limited due to the haze.
At 1045 A/7, the caique was seen again 207° from Cape Drepano, 2 miles. As bigger ships were expected to arrive at Suda Bay soon Lt. Kett decided not to attack this caique.
At 1215 A/7, the caique passed out of sight to the north of Cale Maleka.
[We have been unable to identify this caique.]
At 2135 A/7, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1802 B/7 was received which ordered HMS Ultimatum to remain east of 23.50'E until 1401 B/8 and then to proceed to patrol off St. Nikolo Bay. (27)
9 Apr 1944 At 0354 A/9, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 36°08'N, 23°10'E and patrolled off St. Nikolo Bay.
At 0700 A/9, the mast of a caique was seen through the Dragonera Islands. HMS Ultimatum tried to intercept but by 0800 A/9, the caique was no longer seen.
At 1900 A/9, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 36°09'N, 23°10'E and set course for the Anti-Kithera Channel to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1809 A/7. (27)
12 Apr 1944 At 1815 A/12, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 22nd war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Foxtrot (T/Lt. O.R. Turville, RNVR) which had joined around 1130 A/12. (27)
18 Apr 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) conducted A/S exercises off Malta with HMS Teazer (Lt.Cdr. A.A.F. Talbot, DSO and Bar, RN) and RHS Navarinon (?). (82)

26 Apr 1944 At 1800 B/26, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) departed Malta for her 23rd war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the South-West Aegean, off the south coast of Morea and west of Kithera and Antikithera.
28 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 1146 B/28, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); Proceed forthwith with despatch to examine Kapsali Bay (Kithera) tonight.
HMS Ultimatum examined the Bay very late in the evening but sighted nothing of interest.
The following morning, just after daybreak a second examination of the Bay was made and a small caique at anchor was sighted and the wreck a sunken steamer. (83)

29 Apr 1944 In a signal timed 0930 B/29, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); (1) You have freedom of action in your area. (2) Appreciate possible Danube-lighter lying in Kalamata. (78)
1 May 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sank the small Greek sailing vessel MYT 687 / Elevtheria with gunfire off Cape Matapan, Greece in position 36°21'N, 22°35'E.
0445 B/1 - Dived in position 36°36'N, 22°34'E and commenced to patrol at periscope depth.
0900 B/1 - In approximate positon 36°40'N, 22°34'E, sighted two small caiques sailing northwards.
1820 B/1 - Heard an underwater explosion.
2020 B/1 - Surfaced in position 36°25'N, 22°35'E and set course for Cape Matapan.
2141 B/1 - Sighted a dark object. Closed.
2201 B/1 - In position 36°21'N, 22°35'E opened fire on a caique.
2223 B/1 - Took three survivors on board. The caique sank. Set course towards Levadia Point. (27)

2 May 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) shells the harbour of Koroni, Greece. Two sailing vessels were claimed sunk, five were claimed destroyed on the slips and one was claimed to be damaged. Three shells fell in the village, killing one Greek was killed and wounding two more.
0443 B/2 - Dived in position 36°40'N, 22°07'E and patrolled at periscope depth off Koroni.
1000 B/2 - Several small caiques of 10 to 15 tons left Koroni and sailed northwards.
A close examination of Koroni harbour revealed three ship building yards with nine large caiques either building or in repair on the slipways. Numerous caiques of various sizes were seen afloat.
1540 B/2 - Adjusted course to investigate Koroni harbour again with a view to bombardment.
1919 B/2 - Surfaced 1400 yards from the breakwater to bombard Koroni harbour where several caiques had been sighted as well as three shipyards that were building caiques.
1937 B/2 - Completed the bombardment. 62 Rounds had been fired. Dived and retired seawards.
The next morning the harbour was examined for the results of the bombardment. Two caiques were seen to have sunk, five were destroyed on the slipways and one was seen to be holed and damaged. (27)

4 May 1944 In a signal timed 1031 B/4, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Unswerving (T/Lt. M.D. Tattersall, RNVR) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR); (1) Unswerving has freedom of action in western Aegean north of lat. 36°26'N, and west of long. 24°30'E. (2) Ultimatum enter Aegean through Antikithera at dawn tomorrow for patrol - North coast Crete south of lat. 36°05'E. (3) Sabine (German, former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) in Piraeus ready to sail destination probably Crete. Bourgas (Bulgarian, 2812 GRT, built 1900) in Piraeus ready to sail destination unknown. Considerable caiques today reported between Port Nikolo, Mirabello and Heraklion.<./br> (4) Estimated HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) westbound in approximate position 35°41'N, 23°05'E at 2005 hours today. (84)

5 May 1944 In a signal timed 1701 B/5, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Unswerving (T/Lt. M.D. Tattersall, RNVR) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; Sabine (German, former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) probably still in Piraeus. May sail for Crete at 2200 hours tonight escorted by three destroyers. (84)
6 May 1944 At 0430 B/6, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived north of Suda Bay in position 35°49'N, 24°11'E and commenced to patrol at periscope depth towards Suda Bay.
At 0506 B/6, a darkened shape was seen bearing 000°.

At 0512 B/6, the target was made up to be two patrol caiques and one 'R' boat (motor minesweeper). Estimated range was 7000 yards. The vessels were transmitting on Asdic and apparently engaged in an A/S sweep.
At 0517 B/6, four Arado 96 aircraft were seen patrolling low over the area.
At 0520 B/6, an aircraft fired a pyrotechnic signal consisting of three red and three white stars.
At 0530 B/6, a single bomb or depth charge was dropped fairly close. HMS Ultimatum went to 100 feet.

At 0540 B/6, HMS Ultimatum commenced to return to periscope depth. On reaching 40 feet an A/S vessel, whose HE was now very loud, appeared to be in contact and coming in to attack so Lt. Kett returned to 100 feet, increased speed and altered away.
At 0545 B/6, no attack had developed so once again commenced to return to periscope depth. Watched the A/S vessels and aircraft sweeping to the southward.
At 0620 B/6, the A/S vessels were lost from sight in the haze off Cape Drepano.

The Germans claimed that the bomb dropped at 0530 B/6 [0532 B/6 in German records] sank the submarine. The A/S vessels sighted were the auxiliary patrol vessels GK 01 and GK 91 which were escorting the motor sailing vessels SYR 438 / ???? (?) and PI 163 / ???? (?) from Milos to Suda Bay. Apparently GK 91 had sighted the submarine. After two depth charges had been dropped by an aircraft part of the submarine was seen breaking the surface. This was followed by air bubbles for several minutes. After that no contact could be gained on the submarine and it was thought it had been sunk.

At 1200 B/6, a large caique of about 100 tons and one coaster, also of about 100 tons, were seen coast crawling from eastwards towards Suda Bay. In view of the possible arrival of Sabine (German, former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) these were not attacked.

[We have been unable to identify these vessels or this must have been the two motor sailing vessels listed above but this seems unlikely as, according to German documents, they entered Suda Bay around 0900 B/6 together with GK 01 while GK 91 remained out hunting for the submarine.]
At 2035 B/6, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°39'N, 24°15'E.
At 2120 B/6, while patrolling on a course of 130° three small vessels appeared out of the fog on bearing 160° (Green 30°) at a range of about 6000 yards. HMS Ultimatum turned towards and closed.
At 2127 B/6, the bearing moved to starboard. The estimated course of the enemy was 260° at a speed of 7 knots. They were transmitting on Asdic and steaming in single line ahead.
At 2134 B/6, HMS Ultimatum dived. The vessels were made out to be small patrol craft, probably patrol caiques with sails furled. HE was classified as that of petrol engines.
At 2200 B/6, the HE faded out on bearing 260°. HMS Ultimatum surfaced. There was nothing in sight.

The vessels sighted were most likely the auxiliary patrol vessels GK 01, GK 60 and GK 92 which had departed Suda Bay at 1950 B/6 for Potamo, Anti-Kithera. They were joined later by the landing barge F 123 which departed Chania around 2100 B/6. They arrived at Potamo around 0600 B/7 but not before F 123 had been attacked and damaged by Allied aircraft shortly after midnight on the 7th. One of the crew / troops was killed, nine heavily injured and one lightly injured. (85)

6 May 1944 In a signal timed 2020 B/6, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Unswerving (T/Lt. M.D. Tattersall, RNVR) and HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) of the following; Three destroyers, two escort vessels and Bourgas (Bulgarian, 2812 GRT, built 1900) departed Piraeus 1530 B/6, destination unknown.

In a follow up signal timed 2235 B/6 they were also informed of the following; (1) My 2020 B/6, destination Saloniki. (2) Sabine (German, former Italian Salvatore, 2281 GRT, built 1921) with three destroyers and two escort vessels probably sail today for Crete.

The transport Bourgas, small tanker Elli (former Greek, 314 GRT, built 1878) towing the Siebel ferry, SF 265 had departed Piraeus for Saloniki at 2100 B/5. They were escorted by UJ 2105 and UJ 2110. They arrived at Saloniki at 1700 B/8 being delayed by fog while en-route. (86)
9 May 1944 At 0323 B/9, while patrolling off Suda Bay, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) sighted two darkened ships steering south. HMS Ultimatum altered course towards.

At 0350 B/9, it was seen that it were enemy patrol craft which were patrolling to the north of Cape Drepano. They were observed to steam northward as far as Cape Tripiti then turn and proceeded south. No Asdic transmissions were picked up. Also no HE was heard.

[We have been unable to positively identify these vessels, it seems likely that it were vessels from the Seeverteidigung Kreta, perhaps it were GK 60 and GK 92 proceeding from Chania to Suda Bay. [They had arrived at Chania around 0445 B/8 and the next mention of them is on 10 May when they left Suda Bay in the morning to assist GK 91 hunting HMS Ultimatum. The harbour entrance at Suda Bay had been closed until 2230 B/8 due enemy mining so GK 60 and GK 92 most likely must have departed Chania after this time and thus may well have been the vessels encountered at 0323 B/9.]
At 0358 B/9, the navigation lights of as steamer were sighted on bearing 115°. HMS Ultimatum closed on a 120° track.
At 0405 B/9, HMS Ultimatum dived in position 35°40'N, 24°16'E and continued to close the illuminated vessel.
At 0500 B/9, the vessel was identified as a hospital ship illuminated in accordance with the International regulations.
At 0530 B/9, it was seen that the hospital ship remained stopped off the entrance to Suda Bay. Half an hour later the ship entered the Bay. [For the identity of this hospital ship see below.]
At 0842 B/9, two unidentified vessels were seen to leave Suda Bay. HMS Ultimatum closed to attack.
At 0910 B/9, it was seen that the two vessels were small patrol craft patrolling between Cape Drepano and Paleo Suda Island.
At 0940 B/9, the enemy patrol vessels returned to Suda Bay.
At 1025 B/9, the enemy patrol vessels were seen once more.
Perhaps the patrol vessels sighted this morning were GK 60 and GK 92 yet again.

At 1726 B/9, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1345 B/9 was received which stated that the German hospital ship Tübingen (Former French Gouverneur General Tirman, 3509 GRT, built 1930) was at Suda Bay. This was indeed the hospital ship seen entering Suda Bay that morning. She came from Iraklion.
At 2035 B/9, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°35'N, 24°17'E. (27)

10 May 1944 In the early morning hours, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was detected by two German A/S vessels to the north of Suda Bay. She was depth charged by them sustaining some damage. After several hours she managed to escape.

The attacks were carried out by the German auxiliary patrol vessel GK 91 which was escorting a motorised sailing vessel Ismini (????). Between 0435 B/10 and 0640 B/10 she dropped a total of 20 depth charges. After the 6th pattern had been dropped a slight oil trace was sighted and an can of food with English language printed on it, as well as bread and pieces of wood were picked up. GK 60 and GK 92 were sent from Suda Bay to assist in hunting the enemy submarine. GK 91 and Ismini arrived at Suda Bay around 0700 B/10.
0355 B/10 - Sighted two ships.
0402 B/10 - Dived to carry out an attack.

0408 B/10 - The two ships were thought to be UJ boats (auxiliary submarine chasers) zigzagging on a main course of 130 degrees. Approached at slow speed keeping end on. The vessels were sweeping on Asdic.
0418 B/10 - Both vessels altered towards and appeared to gain contact. Lights were seen flashing aft on both vessels. Went deep and shut off for depth charging.
0421 B/10 - A pattern of five depth charges was dropped close as Ultimatum reached 100 feet causing some damage. Went to 200 feet.
0512 B/10 - After successfully avoiding several attempted attacks a second pattern was dropped causing some more damage.

0530 B/10 - Several bursts of high speed became necessary to assist in depth keeping. Ultimatum was becoming heavy despite constant pumping. Despite large alterations of course both vessels remained in contact but they did not drop depth charges unless they were very certain of their attack.

0640 B/10 - A pattern of five depth charges fell close causing a fire in the motor room besides causing more damage. Increased speed and checked the descent at 400 feet. Returned to 270 feet and restored trim.

0720 B/10 - A pattern of four depth charges was dropped. Some minor electrical damage was sustained. Both vessels were now, for the first time on the port side and by constant altering of course kept them there.
0740 B/10 - The vessels appeared to have lost contact. A third vessel was now also heard transmitting on Asdic. They all failed to regain contact and Ultimatum withdrew to the north-east.
0840 B/10 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
0915 B/10 - With all repairable defects made good, course was set to patrol off Suda Bay.
1815 B/10 - the hospital ship Tübingen (Former French Gouverneur General Tirman, 3509 GRT, built 1930) was seen leaving Suda Bay and proceeding on a course of 050°.
2048 B/10 - HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°40'N, 24°16'E and patrolled to the northward while charging the battery.

2200 B/10 - Left patrol and set course for the Anti-Kithera Channel in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1047 B/8 which contained the following orders; Leave patrol as necessary to arrive in position 35°49'N, 23°29'E at 0505 B/11 thence through the Anti-Kithera Channel.

Further onward routing had been signalled by the Capt. S.1 in his signal timed 1515 B/9 which contained the following orders; (1) From position 35°41'N, 23°15'E about 1201 B/11 proceed to rendezvous with escort in position 35°49'N, 15°18'E at 1530 B/13. (2) Dive by day on the 11th thence 8.5 knots. (87)
11 May 1944 At 0455 B/11, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) dived in position 35°48'N, 23°30'E and proceeded on course 240°.
At 0600 B/11, HMS Ultimatum dived to 200 feet to pass under the Anti-Kithera minefield.
At 1300 B/11, HMS Ultimatum returned to periscope depth and set course to 270°.
At 1955 B/11, HMS Ultimatum surfaced in position 35°43'N, 22°34'E.
At 2100 B/11, HMS Ultimatum passed her signal timed 2000 B/11 in which she reported being detected off Suda Bay the day before. (88)
13 May 1944 Around 2100 B/13, HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) ended her 23rd war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was swept in. The escort (identity not known) had joined around 1515 B/13. (27)
19 May 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was docked at the Malta Dockyard in No.1 graving dock. (89)
29 May 1944 HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR) was undocked.
[No logbooks are available for the months of June and July 1944. Some details for these months may thus be missing.] (89)
This convoy departed Port Said on 8 June 1944.

On departure from Port Said the convoy was made up of the transports / tankers; Baron Belhaven (British, 6591 GRT, built 1925), Cerion (British, 2588 GRT, built 1938), Clydefield (British (tanker), 7365 GRT, built 1928), Empire Almond (British, 6860 GRT, built 1941), Empire Wordsworth (British (tanker), 9891 GRT, built 1942), Eurybates (British, 6276 GRT, built 1928), Fort Walsh (British, 7126 GRT, built 1943), Mactra (British (tanker), 6193 GRT, built 1936), Murena (Dutch (tanker), 8252 GRT, built 1931), Pierre L'Enfant (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Samariz (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and Samblade (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943).
On departure from Port Said the convoy was escorted by sloop HMS Fleetwood (Cdr. W.B. Piggott, DSC and Bar, OBE, RD, RNR) and the corvette HMS Anemone (Lt. J.B. Sparkes, RNR).

On 9 June 1944, the following transports / tankers departed Alexandria to join the convoy; Crista (British, 2590 GRT, built 1938), Empire Cobbett (British (tanker), 9811 GRT, built 1942), Empire Nugget (British (tanker), 9807 GRT, built 1942), Empire Spartan (British, 7009 GRT, built 1942), Eridan (French, 9928 GRT, built 1928), Fort Clatsop (British, 7157 GRT, built 1943), Hermelin (Norwegian, 1683 GRT, built 1940) and Noesaniwi (Dutch, 6737 GRT, built 1936).
The minesweepers RHS Karteria, RHS Salaminia, RHS Afroussa and RHS Leros also departed Alexandria and joined the convoy for passage westwards.
They were escorted by the minesweeper HMS Hazard (Lt.Cdr. L.C. Smith, RN) and the corvettes HMS Bergamot (A/Lt.Cdr. W. McInnes, RNR) and HMS Convolvulus (A/Lt.Cdr. R.F.R. Yarde-Buller, RNVR).

On 13 June 1944, the following ships departed Augusta to join the convoy; A.P. Hill (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Andrew Briscoe (American, 7244 GRT, built 1944), Chiswick (British, 6006 GRT, built 1943), Djebel Aures (French, 2835 GRT, built 1929), Empire Gale (British, 7089 GRT, built 1941), Facto (Norwegian, 1522 GRT, 1921), Fort de Douaumont (French, 5266 GRT, built 1918), Fort Gaspereau (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943), Fort Maisonneuve (British, 7128 GRT, built 1942), Fort Pembina (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), George Davis (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Gylfe (Norwegian (tanker), 6129 GRT, built 1930), Hoegh Hood (Norwegian (tanker), 9351 GRT, built 1936), Iceland (British, 1236 GRT, built 1914), James Moore (American, 7177 GRT, built 1943), John Trumbull (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Kepong (British, 1874 GRT, built 1916), King Stephen (British, 5274 GRT, built 1928), Kiungchow (British, 2653 GRT, built 1921), Laurent Meeus (Belgian (tanker), 6429 GRT, built 1930), Lincoln Steffens (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Lochee (British, 964 GRT, built 1937), Ocean Gipsy (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Ocean Rider (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942)), Robert T. Hill (American, 7244 GRT, built 1943), Samhope (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Svanholm (Norwegian, 696 GRT, built 1917), Tabitha Brown (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Tore Jarl (Norwegian, 1514 GRT, built 1920) and Woodbridge N. Ferris (American, 7200 GRT, built 1943).

On 14 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Augusta after having parted company with the convoy; Baron Belhaven, Cerion, Crista, Empire Corbett, Empire Nugget, Empire Spartan, Empire Wordsworth, Fort Clatsop, Hermelin, Macrtra, Pierre L'Enfant and Samblade.

On 14 June 1944, the following transports arrived at Malta after having been detached from the convoy; Empire Almond and Eridan while the tug Empire Titania (British, 258 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy from Malta as did the submarine HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, DSC, RNR).
On 14 June 1944, the Djebel Aures arrived at Tunis after having been detached from the convoy.

On 15 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Bizerta after having been detached from the convoy; Facto, Murena, Tore Jarl and the four Greek (YMS-class) minesweepers while the following ships joined the convoy coming from Bizerta; Badjestan (British, 5573 GRT, built 1928) and Empire Summer (British, 6949 GRT, built 1941). The rescue tug HMRT Charon also joined the convoy as did the AA cruiser HMS Colombo (Capt. C.T. Jellicoe, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN).

On 16 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Bone after having been detached from the convoy; Empire Titania, Fort Gaspereau, Fort Maissoneuve, Kopong, Kiungchow and Ocean Gypsy while the following ships joined the convoy coming from Bone; Empire Ballad (British, 6700 GRT, built 1942) and Hardingham (British, 7269 GRT, built 1942). HMS Ultimatum was also detached and proceeded to Bone. The reason for her proceeding to Bone is not known to us. Normally she would have proceeded direct to La Maddalena.
On 16 June 1944, the following ships arrived at Philippeville after having been detached from the convoy as they were straggling; Fort de Douaumont and Svanholm.
